nodes.name: Anja C. Andersen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Anja C. Andersen studies cosmic dust as a tracer of nucleosynthesis, circumstellar grain-processing, and the chemical enrichment of the interstellar medium. She applies dust-depletion-sequence analysis to damped Lyman-alpha absorbers across a wide redshift range to constrain the dominant dust species — silicates, iron-oxide grains, and their evolving mix with metallicity. She investigates the apparent invariance of the dust-to-metals ratio across galactic environments using JWST/MIRI and VLT/UVES observations, and characterises anomalous dust deficits in specific metal-rich high-z galaxies such as the z=7.13 source. Her group links nucleosynthetic pathways to observable grain populations through molybdenum and ruthenium abundance measurements in Galactic stars and presolar grains. She models dust production efficiency of oxygen-rich versus carbon-rich AGB stars at low metallicity, and quantifies how the grain-temperature distribution biases cosmic dust-mass estimates derived from flux. She co-identified r-process strontium in the kilonova of GW170817, and conducts high-resolution ptychographic X-ray nanotomography of Murchison forsterite grains to reconstruct their thermal history in protoplanetary disks.
nodes.w_text: Anja Andersen is extending her dust-depletion-sequence framework from local ISM and damped Lyman-alpha absorbers toward reionisation-era galaxies, using JWST/MIRI and upcoming ELT spectroscopy to constrain the silicate-vs-iron-oxide grain-species balance across a wider metallicity range. She is probing the apparent near-invariance of the dust-to-metals ratio against anomalously dust-deficient high-z sources such as the z=7.13 galaxy, and quantifying how the grain-temperature distribution biases sub-mm-derived cosmic dust masses. In parallel she is linking observable grain populations to nucleosynthesis via Mo/Ru stellar-abundance + presolar-grain measurements, and pushing metal-poor AGB dust-yield modelling to resolve whether AGB production alone can account for the early-Universe dust budget.

nodes.name: Jes Kristian Jørgensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jes Kristian Jørgensen is an observational astrochemist who uses ALMA (and increasingly JWST) to probe the youngest, most deeply embedded Class 0/I protostars and ask how the chemistry of planet-forming material is inherited from the prestellar core. He is one of the driving figures behind the ALMA Large Program 'Early Planet Formation in Embedded Disks' (eDisk), which has produced a uniform high-resolution (~0.05–0.1", ~8–15 au) survey of 19 nearby protostellar disks — the first time such young disks have been imaged systematically at planet-forming scales. Earlier, he led the ALMA-PILS unbiased submillimeter line survey of IRAS 16293-2422, establishing that framework prototype for detecting complex organic molecules, deuterated species, and prebiotic building blocks (glycolaldehyde, peptide-chain precursors) around Sun-like protostars. A signature theme of his program is the chemical link between protostars and Solar-System comets — e.g. comparing the molecular inventory of IRAS 16293B to 67P/Churyumov-Gerasimenko to argue that comets inherit their ice composition from the natal protostellar phase. He also has a leadership role in the European ALMA2040 community effort defining the scientific case and reference design for the next-generation (sub-)mm facility, serving on its Steering Committee. His students and collaborators combine observations with laboratory rotational spectroscopy (for first interstellar detections of deuterated and oxygen-bearing species) and with hydrodynamic + radiative-transfer modeling of gravitationally unstable young disks.
nodes.w_text: Jørgensen is currently pushing the eDisk ALMA Large Program from first-look papers into synthesis: systematically mapping nine molecular tracers across 19 Class 0/I disks, linking continuum shoulder / bump features to Toomre Q~1 gravitationally unstable disks via coupled hydrodynamic + radiative-transfer models, and studying streamers, outflows, and hot-corino chemistry at ~8–15 au scales. His group is following up the richer COM inventory seen toward BHR71 IRS1/2 and IRAS16544, and combining ALMA with JWST (via the IPA/IceAge programs) to connect mm-traced disks to IR-traced jets and ices. Separately, he is a Steering-Committee leader of the European ALMA2040 initiative, coordinating 9 Scientific Working Groups and 27 White Papers toward a reference design for a 2040s next-generation (sub-)mm facility.

nodes.name: Martin Elias Pessah
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Martin Elias Pessah develops semi-analytic and linear perturbation theory frameworks alongside MHD simulations to model magnetic field amplification in differentially rotating flows such as accretion disks and protoneutron stars, providing the first systematic analysis of parasitic mode evolution as they feed off the exponentially growing MRI while being advected by background shear, and deriving an analytical expression for the MRI amplification factor that depends only logarithmically on the initial parasitic mode amplitude. A recurring methodological focus is gaseous dynamical friction, where he computes density wake structures and orbit-integrated forces for rectilinear, elliptical Keplerian, and hyperbolic trajectories using linear perturbation theory, finding that eccentricity evolves from negative to positive as orbits transition from subsonic to supersonic regimes, and identifying six distinct classes of hyperbolic orbits distinguished by wake structure and subsequent orbital evolution. Pessah applies these gas-dynamics tools to black hole binary-single encounters embedded in AGN disks, presenting the first hydrodynamical simulations of such three-body encounters and quantifying a gas-hardening enhancement of the merger probability by a minimum factor of 3.5–8, with triple systems shrinking by 2–3 orders of magnitude in semi-major axis within a few AGN orbits. He has developed a formal framework for assessing when the shearing-box approximation faithfully represents the global AGN disk problem by identifying radii beyond which global disk timescales exceed the gas-induced binary orbital evolution timescale across multiple AGN disk models. His work on circumbinary disk dynamics uses analytical eigenmode theory—decomposing eccentric cavity precession into quadrupole frequency ω_Q and pressure-support frequency ω_P—showing that ground-mode frequencies for 3D disks are approximately half their 2D values and are rather insensitive to cavity density profile when ω_P/ω_Q ≃ 0.1. He performs post-Newtonian N-body binary-single scattering simulations combined with gas-driven alignment prescriptions to show that eccentric binaries with in-band eccentricity e_{10Hz} ≳ 0.1 inspiral too quickly for gas torques to act, producing isotropic χ_eff distributions that naturally establish an eccentricity–χ_eff correlation as a diagnostic of the AGN merger channel, with potential application to events such as GW190521 and GW231123. He has also performed hydrodynamical simulations of black hole disc crossings over inclinations i ∈ [2°, 15°], finding that inclination damping scales as Δi/i ∝ m_{H,0}^{0.4} sin(i)^{−2.7} and that the damping timescale is shorter than predicted from episodic Bondi-Hoyle-Lyttelton accretion alone. Most recently he has extended MHD simulation work to tidal disruption events, showing that stellar magnetic fields stronger than ~10^4 G drive a transverse equilibrium between
nodes.w_text: Martin Pessah is pursuing three interlocking frontiers: magnetized tidal disruption events (TDEs), binary black hole (BBH) mergers in AGN disks, and the gravitational-wave signatures that distinguish them. He has built MHD simulations and semi-analytic models tracing magnetized stellar debris streams from disruption to pericentre return, finding that even modest stellar fields (~10⁴ G) magnetically support over half the stream mass within ~1 year. He is now incorporating magnetic fields into later TDE stages to clarify jet formation and X-ray/radio emission. In parallel, his shearing-box validity framework and post-Newtonian N-body simulations revealed that eccentric AGN-embedded mergers produce isotropic χ_eff distributions and a novel eccentricity–χ_eff correlation, which he is testing against LVK events including GW190521 and GW231123.

nodes.name: Thomas Rainer Heimburg
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Thomas Heimburg is a biophysicist at NBI whose work challenges the textbook Hodgkin-Huxley picture of nerve conduction by casting the action potential as an adiabatic, electromechanical soliton propagating through a lipid bilayer near its melting transition. Almost all of his arXiv output since the mid-2000s advances this thermodynamic soliton theory, arguing that biomembranes sit 10-20 degrees below physiological temperature so that voltage, tension, pH, anesthetics and pressure can reversibly tip them through a phase transition. His group couples calorimetry, viscosity and volume-expansion measurements on charged lipids like DMPG to show that membrane shape transitions couple to chain melting, producing multi-peaked heat-capacity profiles that require water to be treated as part of the membrane rather than a separate phase. He develops the 'excitable fluid mosaic' extension of Singer-Nicolson, in which lipids are active players that produce ion-channel-like pores and propagating pulses without requiring protein channels. He argues that the nerve pulse is isentropic based on the biphasic reversible heat production observed during action potentials, an observation the Hodgkin-Huxley capacitor model cannot explain. Heimburg is willing to take speculative but testable consequences seriously, including fast mechanical synapses, pressure-reversal of anesthesia, ultrasound neurostimulation, and thermodynamic explanations of lithium and alcohol effects in bipolar patients.
nodes.w_text: Heimburg's ongoing programme extends the thermodynamic soliton theory of nerve conduction and pushes it against new data. Recent work refines the theory for charged DMPG membranes with a two-geometry four-state model reproducing multi-peak heat-capacity profiles and their pressure dependence, exposing a breakdown of the usual enthalpy-volume proportionality. In parallel, he argues the action potential is a millimeter-to-centimeter-long mechanical contraction, large enough to support 'fast mechanical synapses' and explain focused-ultrasound neurostimulation. His excitable-fluid-mosaic view treats lipids as active players producing voltage-gated, ion-channel-like pores without proteins. The open thread is pinning down the intermediate DMPG phase structurally and finding direct imaging evidence of macroscopic contraction in active brain tissue.

nodes.name: Mogens Høgh Jensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Mogens Høgh Jensen is a senior biophysicist in the Niels Bohr Institute's Biophysics section whose career bridges nonlinear dynamics, statistical physics and quantitative biology. He came from a foundational background in chaos theory and turbulence (including shell-model studies of turbulent binary fluids and the "inverse statistics" gain-loss asymmetry in econophysics) and has for the past two decades applied those tools to genetic, cellular and epidemiological oscillators. His group has been central to the field of cellular oscillations: inducing phase-locking and chaos in cellular oscillators by modulating driving stimuli, predicting that A20 regulates the NF-kB oscillation period, and building theoretical analyses of the Wnt pathway and symbolic-dynamical descriptions of biological feedback networks. More recently he has led work on spatiotemporal cellular mechanotransduction, formulating one of the first spatiotemporal YAP–Rho GTPase models that predicts damped and sustained oscillations of the YAP nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio under fixed and cyclic mechanical stimulation — with cancer-relevant implications for stiffness-driven signalling. With Pigolotti, Tiana and Zhan he introduced a bifractal "hierarchical nested-domain" model of Hi–C chromosome contact maps that cleanly explains the two distinct fractal exponents observed in mammalian chromatin and detects subtle conformational changes during stem-cell differentiation. In 2025 his group (with Bjarke Frost Nielsen and Bryan Grenfell) published a Bayesian quasicycle model fitting 50+ years of Danish Mycoplasma pneumoniae surveillance data, showing that environmental and demographic stochasticity, rather than deterministic dynamics, sustain the observed multiannual epidemic cycles and explain mode-hopping behaviour. His collaborators span NBI (Heltberg, Doostmohammadi), Milan, Okinawa, and Princeton.
nodes.w_text: Jensen's current programme continues to weave dynamical-systems tools through biology. The 2025 M. pneumoniae quasicycle paper (with Bjarke Frost Nielsen and Bryan Grenfell) signals an active stochastic-epidemiology line that can be expected to extend to other endemic pathogens and to hybrid mechanistic–ML forecasting. His mechanotransduction work with Heltberg and Doostmohammadi is moving from 1D Rho–YAP toward 2D/3D and collective-tissue settings, targeting cancer-relevant feedback between ECM stiffness and proliferation. Chromatin organization via bifractal Hi–C analysis (with Pigolotti, Tiana, Zhan) continues as a diagnostic tool for cell-state transitions. Broader themes — phase-locking and chaos in cellular oscillators, NF-kB period control by A20, stochasticity as a driver of biological cycles — thread through the group's ongoing work. Expect new papers applying nonlinear-dynamics ideas to signalling and epidemiology, often with younger collaborators (Heltberg, Nielsen) driving the computational implementation.

nodes.name: Kunihiko Kaneko
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Kunihiko Kaneko is a theoretical physicist now at NBI whose work over three decades has built a universality-oriented, dynamical-systems approach to biology. His signature programme argues that evolved high-dimensional biological states become constrained to low-dimensional manifolds, yielding macroscopic proportionality relations between fluctuations and responses, between noise-induced and mutation-induced phenotypic variance, and between evolutionary and environmental responses - a biological analogue of fluctuation-dissipation theorems. Recurring themes include coupled-dynamical-systems treatments of cell differentiation (bifurcations from a single genotype into multiple phenotypes), Waddington-style epigenetic-feedback models that convert chaotic gene-expression dynamics into robust attractor landscapes, and minimal catalytic-reaction-network models that explain protocell reproduction, error-catastrophe avoidance via template-directed proofreading, and discreteness-induced transitions when molecule numbers are small. His ecological work uses cross-feeding leak-uptake networks to show that high-dimensional chaos lets many more species coexist than Gause's competitive-exclusion principle permits, reproducing fat-tailed rank-abundance distributions observed in soil and plankton ecosystems. In the last two years he has extended the framework to self-organized bioelectricity via Ising-like collective ion-pump alignment explaining chemiosmosis, enzymes as Maxwell's demons exploiting enhanced diffusion, soil as a living system, metastable-state stability control in cognition, a generalized Central Dogma as a division of labour, and formal kinship structures from multilevel evolution. Kaneko reliably argues that complex biological phenomena are not necessarily complicated and that universality, not parameter-tuning, should be the theoretical goal.
nodes.w_text: Kaneko's current program extends his universality-via-evolved-dimensional-reduction theory in several directions simultaneously. Recent papers argue that high-dimensional chaos in cross-feeding microbial networks breaks Gause's competitive-exclusion principle and reproduces fat-tailed rank-abundance distributions seen in soil and plankton. In parallel, a minimal Ising-like pump-alignment model provides a self-organizing physical origin for chemiosmosis and bioelectricity relevant to the origin of life. Other active threads include enzymes as Maxwell's demons via enhanced diffusion, cross-hierarchical generalizations of the Central Dogma, stability control of neural metastable states for flexible timing, epigenetic-feedback evolution of robust differentiation, and kinship-structure emergence from multilevel evolution. Across all of this he pushes the argument that biology should be treated as complex rather than merely complicated.

nodes.name: Kim Sneppen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Kim Sneppen builds cellular-automaton models in which soil aggregates serve as food for microbial species that produce nutrients enabling self-amplification, demonstrating that spatial heterogeneity at diverse length scales prevents population collapse and permits coexistence of parasitic species. He extends the classical SIRS framework to multiple interacting diseases linked by a short-lasting non-specific immunity lasting only a couple of weeks, showing that this inhibiting interaction alone can produce sequential seasonal epidemic patterns and reproduce the biennial alternation of parainfluenza strains PIV-1 and PIV-3. He applies two-species SIR models to derive criteria under which a single epidemic co-infecting an abundant species can drive a high-mortality species to extinction-level collapse, a result absent from endemic steady-state analysis. A persistent theme is the emergence of coexistence and speciation from competitive dynamics, including a model where each agent's genome is interpreted as the faces of an intransitive die, producing sympatric speciation and a metastable finite mutation rate in the absence of hierarchical fitness. He uses allometric scaling of biological quantities with animal mass to parameterise predator-prey models, deriving analytical predictions for prey-to-predator ratios and population-cycle lengths that compare reasonably with data from multiple ecosystems. At the tissue scale, he models vasculogenesis in three dimensions through apical-basal and planar cell-polarity alignment rules, demonstrating that a fully connected vascular network emerges above a critical cell density and that growth-induced buckling explains the density-invariant vasculature of pancreatic islets of Langerhans. He further optimises transport time rather than dissipation in distributed flow networks with single sources and sinks, recovering periphery-to-center and center-to-periphery flow topologies and branch-buckling patterns characteristic of insulin-producing islet vasculature. Across scales, he models collapse-driven redistribution of resources in microbial communities to produce cyclic diversity waves with a scale-free time-aggregated population distribution carrying a universal exponent of 1.7, and identifies a transition from full coarsening to an active steady state in quasi-one-dimensional rock-paper-scissors systems with critical exponents consistent with the directed-percolation universality class.
nodes.w_text: Kim Sneppen is pushing three interlocking fronts. He just built a cellular automaton model of soil—tracking soil, nutrient, microbe, and empty sites on 2D and 3D lattices with a mean-field counterpart—and discovered that nutrient-production maxima align with power-law aggregate-size distributions only in 2D, not 3D, leaving unexplained why the isotropic 3D model fails real soil behavior. He is extending this toward an anisotropic 2+1D geometry reflecting vertical water and oxygen gradients. Simultaneously, he is probing phage-bacteria coevolution: λ-phage senses host metabolic state via receptor hyperdiffusion, T7 spreads through dormant populations, and dormant infected cells act as survival capsules. He is now building a unified microbial-ecology framework encompassing physiology-dependent phage proliferation and future resistance mechanisms.

nodes.name: Alexander Baklanov
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Alexander Baklanov applies WRF-Chem regional modelling to diagnose how seasonal wildfires in Zabaykalsky Krai and the Republic of Yakutia, and transboundary pollution from northeastern China, degrade air quality in the Amur region, finding that modelled monthly mean NO₂ exceeded Russian state standards in July 2015. He employs the FLEXPART–SOSAA Lagrangian modelling system along 7-day backward trajectories to reconstruct aerosol size distributions—not merely bulk mass concentrations—during Beijing's November 2018 haze episode, tracing approximately 75% of particles to sources outside the city, including southern Hebei province and the Xinjiang region. His multilayer interactive machine-learning framework integrates tower-building and lidar vertical observations to forecast O₃ and co-pollutants up to 72 hours, demonstrating that boundary layer height and vertically integrated temperature together contribute more than 36% of predictive weight in upper layers, and that lower-layer information reduces mean absolute error in upper-layer forecasts by 6.44% while upper-layer information suppresses lower-layer predictability. He analyses PM₂.₅ chemical composition records from 2000 to 2023 across Beijing, Shanghai, and Guangzhou and their surrounding nonurban areas, attributing the narrowing urban–nonurban concentration gap specifically to convergence in organic matter proportions driven by differentiated emission-reduction strategies. He is engaged in designing a Global Ground-Based Earth Observatory (GGBEO), coordinating through the WMO and the University of Helsinki's INAR to harmonise in-situ, remote-sensing, marine, and airborne data into interoperable repositories for multi-scale modelling and AI frameworks. Emission-control scenario testing—showing that comprehensive multi-sector regional controls outperform city-level single-sector interventions, and that postponing agricultural waste burning away from forecast haze periods reduces Beijing pollution—constitutes a recurrent methodological strand across his modelling studies.
nodes.w_text: Alexander Baklanov is extending a multilayer interactive machine-learning framework—built by fusing tower and lidar vertical observations into a unified multipollutant forecasting system—toward three-dimensional, broader-domain air quality prediction, while probing why middle-layer atmospheric information paradoxically degrades rather than improves lower-layer forecasts. He is pursuing the unresolved physics behind this asymmetric interlayer coupling and testing whether it generalizes beyond Beijing. In parallel, he is expanding FLEXPART-SOSAA Lagrangian trajectory analyses—which traced roughly 75% of Beijing haze particles to sources outside the city, some as far as Xinjiang—to additional episodes, and investigating agricultural waste burning postponement as a mitigation lever. He is also refining WRF-Chem simulations for the Russian Far East Amur region, where a near-surface wind-speed bias of 0.8–1.9 m/s systematically distorts pollutant concentration estimates.

nodes.name: Thomas Blunier
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Thomas Blunier is an ice-core geochemist at the Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth (PICE) section / Niels Bohr Institute, best known internationally for pioneering the use of trapped-air records in polar ice to reconstruct past atmospheric composition and to time-link Greenland and Antarctic climate events. His signature methodological contribution is high-precision measurement and interpretation of stable-isotope and clumped-isotope compositions of methane, CO2, N2O, O2, and related trace gases extracted from ice cores and contemporary glacial systems. Current work is two-pronged. First, modern Greenland ice-sheet biogeochemistry: with collaborators (Adnew, Christiansen) he is measuring bulk 13C/D, 14C, and clumped Δ13CH3D and Δ12CH2D2 isotopologues of CH4 and CO2 emitted at the ice-sheet margin (Isunnguata Sermia) to identify subglacial microbial sources, distinguish thermogenic vs. biogenic pathways, and quantify aerobic vs. anaerobic oxidation during transport. Second, paleoclimate ice-core analysis: he contributes to Mount Brown South (East Antarctica, 1137-year continuous-flow-analysis record), the EGRIP / Mount Hope / Dome C programs, and is helping develop new 3D laboratory X-ray microscopy for ice texture — a non-destructive, lab-scale tomography method that visualizes ice grains and air bubbles together with their c- and a-axis crystallographic orientations, replacing destructive 1D/2D ice-core analyses used since the 1950s. He operates and develops the Copenhagen continuous-flow-analysis (CFA) system for chemistry and impurities on ice cores. His science thus spans cryosphere-carbon feedbacks for the modern climate and deep-time ice-core paleoclimate records.
nodes.w_text: Blunier's active program splits into three interlocking threads. (1) Modern Greenland-ice-sheet biogeochemistry at the Isunnguata Sermia glacier portal: bulk-isotope, radiocarbon, and clumped-isotope (Δ13CH3D, Δ12CH2D2) characterization of subglacial CH4 and CO2 (with Adnew and Christiansen), establishing acetoclastic methanogenesis of Neoglacial-buried organics as the dominant CH4 source and quantifying aerobic oxidation overprints. (2) New 3D non-destructive ice-core microstructure analysis using lab-based combined absorption + diffraction-contrast X-ray tomography, imaging grains and bubbles together with c- and a-axis orientations to feed anisotropic ice-flow models. (3) Continued high-resolution continuous-flow-analysis (CFA) records of chemistry and impurities in East Antarctic ice — most recently the Mount Brown South 1137-year record — run through the Copenhagen CFA system that he helps develop.

nodes.name: Jens Hesselbjerg Christensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jens Hesselbjerg Christensen is a climate physicist at NBI whose core expertise is regional climate modeling and the interpretation of large-scale climate projections. The arxiv-filtered material available for him is dominated by one clearly attributable paper (the rest of the arxiv hits appear to be by other 'Jens Christensen' authors - a mathematician working on Bergman spaces and the Euler-Poisson-Darboux equation, and a computer scientist working on CNOT-circuit optimization - and are not representative of his research). His identifiable recent thread sits at the interface of operational weather/climate forecasting and physics-informed machine learning. In 2025 he coauthored a study on 'Trading Carbon for Physics' arguing that embedding physics inductive biases (PINNs, Fourier neural operators, flow matching, physics-informed loss terms) into spatio-temporal forecasting models can deliver substantial compute, energy and carbon savings while retaining or improving predictive accuracy - directly relevant for large-scale operational weather and climate PDE solvers run many times daily on supercomputers. The study advocates carbon-aware evaluation practices for ML models used in climate forecasting, and warns that efficient training does not guarantee efficient deployment. Beyond that, his broader visibility at NBI and in IPCC-linked work is in climate and geophysics, but his arxiv footprint is sparse because his primary publication venues are climate-science journals rather than preprint archives. The available arxiv trace therefore gives a narrow but distinctive window onto one strand of his current interests: ML-for-climate with a principled concern for resource efficiency and sustainability.
nodes.w_text: Christensen's available arxiv trace captures one current thread clearly: the intersection of physics-informed machine learning and operational climate/weather forecasting. In 2025 with Wilson and Selvan he argued for 'Trading Carbon for Physics' - showing that embedding physics inductive biases (via PINNs, Fourier neural operators, flow matching and physics-informed loss terms) into spatio-temporal forecasting can substantially cut compute, energy and CO2-equivalent emissions while retaining or improving predictive accuracy. They introduce carbon-aware evaluation protocols and show that the best model depends strongly on the training-vs-inference cost balance, with UFNO offering the lowest training footprint but higher inference cost than tuned U-Nets. His broader regional-climate-modeling and IPCC-linked activity is not covered in the arxiv sample because it is published primarily in climate-science journals rather than preprint archives. (Note: other arxiv hits for 'Jens Christensen' in this dataset are by different authors.)

nodes.name: Dorthe Dahl-Jensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Dorthe Dahl-Jensen is a glaciologist in the Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth (PICE) section / NBI and one of the defining figures in deep-ice-core paleoclimate science. She led or co-led the drilling and interpretation of several signature Greenland deep ice cores — NGRIP, NEEM, EastGRIP — and through them contributed to the canonical record of past temperature, abrupt Dansgaard–Oeschger events, and Eemian-interglacial climate in Greenland. Her technical expertise spans ice-flow modeling, borehole thermometry, ice-sheet dynamics, age-scale construction, and the interpretation of trapped-gas and chemistry records. In recent years she has expanded into the physical-chemistry of atmospheric deposition on ice caps — using single-particle ICP-MS and Pb-isotope ratios on snow pits from sites like the Müller Ice Cap (Axel Heiberg Island, Canadian Arctic) to fingerprint the transport of metal-containing nanoparticles from North American and Asian sources into Arctic snow. She is also a visible scientific communicator on Arctic climate change, co-authoring high-profile synthesis reviews that translate model projections (e.g. '+2.7°C world') into concrete Arctic landscape and cryosphere consequences for policy audiences. Her Copenhagen group combines fieldwork on Greenland and the Canadian High Arctic with laboratory ice-core analysis and ice-sheet modeling, and she has been central to building international consortia around Greenland drilling.
nodes.w_text: Dahl-Jensen's current work continues the Greenland deep-ice-core program (EastGRIP and successors) while simultaneously pushing into two new directions. First, modern atmospheric-deposition physics on Arctic ice caps: using single-particle ICP-MS and Pb-isotope analysis on snow pits from the Müller Ice Cap (Canadian High Arctic) to quantify Zn/Pb/Cd nanoparticle transport from North American and Asian sources, and to build the bridge from contemporary snow chemistry to the century-scale impurity records preserved in deeper cores. Second, large-scale synthesis and communication of Arctic climate-change projections — co-authoring high-visibility reviews that translate model outputs into concrete landscape-scale consequences (ice-free summer Arctic Ocean, quadrupled Greenland melt area, halved permafrost area at +2.7°C). Across both, she is anchoring NBI/PICE's role in international ice-core drilling consortia and Arctic climate assessments.

nodes.name: Peter Ditlevsen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Peter Ditlevsen is a climate physicist at NBI's Climate and Geophysics section (Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth) whose work applies stochastic dynamical-systems and bifurcation theory to tipping elements in Earth's climate. His 2023 paper with Susanne Ditlevsen made international headlines by predicting, with statistical significance derived from proxy SST observations, that the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) will collapse around 2057 (central estimate) under a 'business as usual' emissions scenario - he developed a novel framework for establishing when early-warning signals (variance increase, autocorrelation increase / critical slow-down) rise significantly above natural variability, rather than merely showing a Kendall-tau trend. His broader portfolio covers the bifurcation structure of Pleistocene glacial cycles, the Middle Pleistocene Transition interpreted as frequency locking with slowly ramping internal period, recurrence-time statistics of Dansgaard-Oeschger events, stochastic cascade models and helicity/inverse-cascade structure in 3D turbulence, a climatic-thermostat argument for Earth habitability, seasonal-cycle tipping in Arctic sea-ice amplitude, and, most recently, a conceptual three-coupled-ice-stream model showing that thermomechanical coupling plus nonlinear coupling of incommensurate frequencies yields chaotic internal variability in ice streams (with bistability between chaotic and non-chaotic regimes). His recent collaborative work with Lohmann, Dijkstra, Jochum and Lucarini uses 2.75 million years of primitive-equation ocean simulation to show that AMOC collapse proceeds through a sequence of 'intermediate tipping points' traversing a rugged stability landscape with up to nine coexisting stable circulation states, where the path depends on the rate of meltwater input. Ditlevsen's style combines rigorous stochastic statistics with deep climate-dynamics intuition.
nodes.w_text: Ditlevsen's current work remains focused on tipping in Earth-system sub-systems, now with three active threads. The AMOC programme has moved beyond the 2023 statistical-significance-based tipping-time estimator (central estimate 2057) into structural work with Lohmann, Dijkstra, Jochum and Lucarini showing that collapse proceeds via a sequence of intermediate tipping points through a rugged stability landscape with up to nine coexisting states, and the path depends on the rate of meltwater input. In ice dynamics, Kypke, Ashwin and Ditlevsen just demonstrated that three nonlinearly coupled ice streams produce deterministic chaos with bistability between chaotic and non-chaotic regimes, providing an internal-variability mechanism for Heinrich-like oscillations. Older threads on Dansgaard-Oeschger recurrence statistics, Pleistocene glacial cycles and inverse energy cascades in 3D turbulence remain part of the broader portfolio.

nodes.name: Christine Schøtt Hvidberg
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Christine Schøtt Hvidberg works at the interface of glaciology and geophysics, modelling the Greenland Ice Sheet's past, present, and future behaviour by coupling rheological models of firn and bulk ice to paleoclimate constraints. She extends the Gagliardini-Meyssonnier non-linear-viscous firn rheology to multidimensional configurations to simulate firn-densification artifacts such as snow-cave collapse and enhanced densification at ice-stream shear margins. She runs large PISM ensembles (~841 runs) of Holocene GrIS evolution constrained by ice-core-derived elevation histories, recovering a best-estimate sea-level-equivalent contribution of 5.3 ± 0.3 m. She measures crystal-orientation-fabric anisotropy on the Northeast Greenland Ice Stream (NEGIS) using radar polarimetry and ice-core cofs analysis, showing that directional hardening from fabric anisotropy systematically affects ice-flow speed. Her work connects ice-core paleoclimate records to ice-sheet-model initialisation for next-generation sea-level projections.
nodes.w_text: Christine Hvidberg is closing the loop between firn-scale rheology, crystal-fabric anisotropy, and continental-scale Greenland Ice Sheet simulation. She is extending her 2D Gagliardini-Meyssonnier firn densification solver to further Greenlandic sites to test its generalisation at shear margins, and coupling it to bulk-ice-flow models. She is tightening the Holocene GrIS mass-loss prior (5.3 ± 0.3 m sea-level equivalent, from an 841-run PISM ensemble) using additional ice-core elevation-history constraints, and aims to propagate the calibrated ensemble to future projections. In parallel she is mapping crystal-orientation-fabric anisotropy on NEGIS via radar polarimetry + ice-core cofs, and working to integrate the resulting directional-hardening maps as initial conditions in large-scale ice-sheet models.

nodes.name: Markus Jochum
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Markus Jochum is a physical oceanographer and climate modeller in the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section (Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth group), with deep roots in primitive-equation ocean modelling of the Atlantic circulation and the coupled climate system. His group's recent output shows two very different but connected programmes. One is a long-running interest in tipping points and multistability of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation: in collaboration with Lohmann, Dijkstra, Lucarini and Ditlevsen he used 2.75 million years of primitive-equation simulations to map a rugged AMOC stability landscape with up to nine coexisting stable states and uncovered "intermediate tipping points" — small-amplitude abrupt circulation reorganizations that occur well before the canonical collapse threshold and whose sequencing depends on the rate of meltwater input. The other is a pioneering effort to apply causal machine learning to ocean physics, led with his student Dion Häfner: this produced FOWD, a free, quality-controlled ocean wave dataset built from over 4 billion CDIP buoy waves, and a machine-guided symbolic-regression discovery of a new causal rogue-wave model that outperforms Rayleigh, Tayfun and Mori–Janssen theories on held-out buoy data while staying interpretable. Jochum's signature is turning complex geophysical fluid dynamics into data- and theory-consistent predictive models, with a growing emphasis on causal and parsimony-guided AI for climate discovery.
nodes.w_text: Jochum's arXiv traces, combined with his Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth affiliation, suggest two active threads. On the ocean-circulation side, he continues mapping the AMOC stability landscape under glacial-melt and freshwater forcing, chasing intermediate tipping points and rate-dependent transitions jointly with Ditlevsen, Lohmann, Dijkstra and Lucarini — likely moving from idealized primitive-equation runs toward more fully coupled Earth system configurations and paleoclimate comparisons. On the data-science side, the FOWD-to-rogue-wave arc is maturing into a broader programme of causal, parsimony-guided machine learning for geophysical fluid dynamics, expanding toward operational forecast benchmarks, super-rogue-wave statistics, and potentially ocean-circulation diagnostics. Because the ingest ran on an author-only arXiv fallback, this list reflects only the portion of Jochum's corpus visible on arXiv; his broader oceanography and climate-modelling activity is likely wider than these three papers suggest.

nodes.name: Eigil Kaas
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Eigil Kaas is a climate and geophysics researcher at the Niels Bohr Institute whose recent work centres on convection-permitting regional climate modelling over Northern Europe. His 2026 study with Ruben Haberli applies the HARMONIE-Climate (HCLIM) model at approximately 3 km resolution across Fenno-Scandinavia, using the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) to quantify future drought probabilities. A distinguishing methodological contribution is a multi-threshold SPI analysis that separates moderate, exceptional, and unprecedented drought regimes rather than collapsing them into a single threshold. The work shows that moving from coarser regional climate models (RCMs) to convection-permitting resolution does not merely refine existing signals but changes the frequency prediction for the most intense droughts upward, especially during the growing season. Kaas's focus thus sits at the interface between numerical weather prediction heritage and climate projection, where explicit resolution of convective precipitation replaces parametrised schemes. The policy-facing implication of his recent output is that Fenno-Scandinavia is projected to experience drought intensities without historical precedent by mid- to late-21st century under the assessed scenarios.
nodes.w_text: Kaas's current work, built from abstracts only, focuses on pushing convection-permitting regional climate models (CPRCMs) as a tool for understanding drought risk in Fenno-Scandinavia. The active thread is a HARMONIE-Climate (HCLIM) run at ~3 km resolution, coupled with a new multi-threshold Standardized Precipitation Index method that disentangles moderate from exceptional and unprecedented droughts. He is probing whether resolution-driven improvements to convective precipitation systematically shift projected drought extremes upward compared with coarser regional models, with immediate implications for mid- to late-21st century growing-season drought risk across Northern Europe.

nodes.name: Klaus Mosegaard
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Klaus Mosegaard is a geophysicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose career-defining contribution is the Bayesian/probabilistic formulation of geophysical inverse problems — using Monte Carlo sampling to characterise posterior uncertainty in Earth properties that classical deterministic inversion could never quantify. His recent work shows two clear modern directions. First, he is pushing geophysical inversion into the deep-learning era: generative adversarial networks as geostatistical priors, differentiable forward models, and gradient-informed samplers for high-dimensional posterior exploration. Second, his method stack has been exported beyond Earth: he has co-authored work using InSight seismic data to hunt for Mars's background-free oscillations in ice-core-like continuous-record analyses. The review-scale synthesis coming out of his group ('Differentiable Bayesian Inversion') points toward an ambition to unify deep-learned priors, physics-based or surrogate forward operators, and probabilistic reasoning in a single differentiable architecture. His fingerprint is statistical and methodological — he is geophysics's Bayesian architect — rather than data-collection-focused.
nodes.w_text: Mosegaard's active line is the fusion of Bayesian geophysical inversion with modern deep learning. His recent review-scale synthesis proposes 'Differentiable Bayesian Inversion' as a unifying framework where deep-learning-based geological priors, physics-based or surrogate forward operators, and probabilistic reasoning all sit inside one modular differentiable architecture. In parallel, concrete papers deliver the building blocks: multi-scale GANs trained from a single geological training image (successfully applied to Lena-delta-like deltaic systems and 3D aquifer models) combined with gradual-deformation conditioning to match observed data, and InSight seismic-data stacking pipelines that search for Mars's background-free oscillations via volcanic-style deglitching and 1-sol autocorrelation stacks. He is positioning his group at the intersection of AI-accelerated geoscience and uncertainty quantification.

nodes.name: Jørgen Peder Steffensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jørgen Peder Steffensen is a senior ice-core climate scientist at NBI's Climate and Geophysics section (Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth / Centre for Ice and Climate), known for decades of work on Greenland deep ice cores (NEEM, NGRIP, GISP2). His scientific output is concentrated on reconstructing past temperature, atmospheric composition and abrupt climate events from ice, with high-precision seasonally unbiased methods. His recent collaborative work with Kirk, Koldtoft and Blunier uses NEEM, NGRIP and GISP2 to produce a multi-core, multi-proxy firn-and-borehole Greenland temperature reconstruction from the last glacial to pre-industrial, identifying a Holocene Thermal Maximum of approximately 2 C above pre-industrial - in agreement with Northern Hemisphere proxies but contradicting the monotonic warming simulated by many climate models. The timing of the HTM is heterogeneous, occurring earlier at high elevations, and the authors argue that elevation changes, regional oceanographic conditions, a weakened polar lapse rate, and variable near-surface inversions all matter; notably, the reconstructions support climate simulations that include dynamic Holocene vegetation feedbacks. Steffensen also co-authors interdisciplinary paleoclimate-archaeology papers, including a 2025 analysis of over 600 Neolithic 'sun stone' engraved plaques from Vasagaard on Bornholm, examining their function during a possible early-third-millennium-BC climatic crisis that affected the Northern Hemisphere. His breadth spans ice-core instrumentation, Holocene climate, and the social consequences of past climate extremes.
nodes.w_text: Based on abstracts scraped from KU's Pure profile (no PDFs available in corpus for Steffensen), his active threads are: (1) a multi-core, multi-proxy Greenland temperature reconstruction from NEEM, NGRIP and GISP2 spanning last glacial to pre-industrial that identifies a ~2 C Holocene Thermal Maximum with earlier timing at high elevations, in agreement with Northern Hemisphere proxies but contradicting monotonic-warming model simulations; the work points to dynamic Holocene vegetation feedbacks, lapse-rate and inversion effects as key missing model physics. (2) A 2025 interdisciplinary archaeology-paleoclimate paper with Iversen and Svensson on over 600 Neolithic 'sun stones' from Vasagaard (Bornholm), linking ritual deposition to a possible early-third-millennium-BC Northern-Hemisphere climate crisis.

nodes.name: Brian Møller Andersen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Brian Moller Andersen is a theorist of unconventional superconductivity in NBI's Condensed Matter Physics section, a prolific author (close to 100 arXiv papers on cond-mat) whose programme centres on the interplay between magnetism, disorder, and unconventional pairing symmetries in correlated materials. His most consistent thread is iron-based and iron-pnictide/chalcogenide superconductors, where he and collaborators (Kreisel, Fernandes, Hirschfeld) have developed a realistic orbital-selective and multiband framework that connects STM quasiparticle interference, in-gap Yu-Shiba-Rusinov states, nematic order and vestigial phases, and spin-fluctuation-driven s_{+-} / s_{++} pairing symmetries. A second signature thread is disorder physics in unconventional superconductors: when and how nonmagnetic and magnetic impurities protect or destroy sign-changing order parameters, gap-averaging arguments, the protection of unconventional superconductivity from disorder, and gapless superconductivity from extremely dilute magnetic disorder. A third thread is topological superconductivity and Majorana engineering: vortices bound by magnetic islands in correlated Rashba systems, fractional 1/3 flux-quantum vortices in chiral d+id kagome superconductors, Majorana zero modes on superconductor-topological-insulator heterostructures, and topological surface states revealed by the Zeeman effect in the triplet superconductor UTe2. A fourth, more recent strand is altermagnetic superconductors (momentum-dependent gap structures, odd-parity magnetism) and unconventional superconductivity in monolayer transition-metal dichalcogenides. Methodologically Andersen is known for marrying microscopic self-consistent Bogoliubov-de Gennes calculations with realistic band structures, inhomogeneous magnetic order and impurities, and for pushing theoretical predictions all the way to observable STM/ARPES fingerprints that direct experimental effort in Madhavan, Paglione, Hasan, and Hoffman groups.
nodes.w_text: Andersen's 2025-2026 output is strikingly broad across unconventional superconductors. The freshest paper combines vector-field STM by Madhavan with spectral-function theory to identify topological surface states in triplet UTe2 via site-selective gap suppression under Zeeman coupling. A companion two-author paper with Kotetes proposes a way to pin zero-field superconducting vortices (and Majorana zero modes) with out-of-plane-magnetized magnetic islands on correlated Rashba surfaces, exploiting magnetization gradients rather than external fields. A third thread predicts fractional 1/3 flux-quantum vortices in chiral d+id kagome superconductors. In parallel he continues the iron-pnictide/chalcogenide programme (odd-parity magnetism in Fe-based superconductors, protection of unconventional superconductivity from disorder, gapless superconductivity from dilute magnetic impurities in NbSe2-xSx), together with new work on altermagnetic superconductors and monolayer TMDs.

nodes.name: Karsten Flensberg
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Karsten Flensberg is a condensed-matter theorist at the Center for Quantum Devices (QDev) / NBI, working at the interface of superconductivity, mesoscopic physics, and quantum information. His research portfolio centers on hybrid superconductor–semiconductor devices: InAs/Al (and Ge) nanowires and 2DEGs/2DHGs engineered for proximity-induced superconductivity, with implications for topological qubits and Majorana physics. He is a long-time proponent of the 'minimal Kitaev chain' / 'poor man's Majorana' (PMM) program — using small chains of quantum dots and multi-terminal Josephson junctions to realize and braid Majorana modes without requiring large applied magnetic fields or clean wires. More recently he has helped drive the theoretical side of superconducting-qubit engineering at QDev: Born–Oppenheimer effective models for multi-mode circuits, 'harmonic parity' and higher-Josephson-harmonic qubits built from SIS + hybrid nanowire SQUIDs, and protected-qubit / Fourier-engineering proposals. A parallel thread explores non-Hermitian physics and exceptional points in open superconducting/normal mesoscopic systems, including how to detect the non-Hermitian skin effect via nonreciprocal nonlocal conductance. He also works on statistical/transport properties of Josephson-junction arrays (KPZ scaling, Andreev crystals as directional tunable filters) and proximitized 2D hole gases in germanium, deriving the HH/LH pairing structure from Luttinger–Kohn microscopics. Across all of this, his signature is tight theory/experiment coupling with the experimental QDev group, translating microscopic Hamiltonians into directly measurable spectroscopic / transport signatures.
nodes.w_text: Flensberg's active program is dominated by the theory of hybrid superconductor–semiconductor qubits. A major 2025–2026 thread is multi-mode superconducting circuit theory: Born–Oppenheimer effective models for double-junction transmons and 'harmonic parity' qubits, co-developed with QDev experimentalists (Kjaergaard, Krøjer) to fit real spectroscopy and find charge-noise-protected sweet spots. A parallel thread is poor-man's-Majorana / minimal Kitaev chain physics — adiabatic nonabelian braiding of imperfect MBSs, phase-controlled PMM in multiterminal Josephson junctions, and eliminating the need for magnetic fields to broaden material choices (Ge heterostructures). A third thread pushes non-Hermitian and multiterminal-Andreev physics: detecting the non-Hermitian skin effect via nonreciprocal nonlocal conductance, exceptional-point signatures in SN junctions, and directional Andreev-crystal filters. He also has ongoing work on proximitized 2DHGs in germanium and KPZ-regime Josephson-array dynamics.

nodes.name: Per Hedegård
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Per Hedegård is a condensed-matter theorist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose work concentrates on the interplay of spin, charge and vibrations in molecular-scale systems and strongly correlated oxides. A signature thread in his recent output is quantitative minimal modeling of metal-organic framework magnets: for CrCl2(pyrazine)2 he and a single student built a Slater-Koster tight-binding model that reproduces the measured 1.8 μB saturation moment from Cr-localized spins antiferromagnetically coupled to delocalized pyrazine electrons, and used Weiss mean-field plus second-order RKKY perturbation theory to predict a direct Cr-Cr coupling of 0.9-5 meV. A parallel thread combines DFT and valence-bond theory with STM experiments at Peking/Delft to explain biradical spin coupling through hydrogen bonds in retinoic-acid dimers and d-π-d spin coupling in Fe coordination structures, and to demonstrate ground-state spin blockade in a single-molecule transistor with a 3/2 spin difference that can be reversibly unlocked by magnetic field. A longer-running methodological thread has been the semi-classical generalized Langevin equation for current-induced forces in molecular junctions—including nonconservative and pseudo-magnetic forces, phonon runaway in dehydrogenated graphene nanoribbons, and electron-phonon drag—which unifies adsorbate dynamics, heating, and thermoelectric transport in one Green's-function framework. He has also contributed to CISS (chirality-induced spin selectivity) theory and, earlier, to SO(5) and checkerboard-stripe theories of high-Tc vortex cores and cuprate spin dynamics. Across all of this his habit is to cast an experiment into the smallest Hubbard- or Anderson-type Hamiltonian whose analytic solution still predicts the measured number.
nodes.w_text: Hedegård's most recent published thread (late 2024, coauthor with one student) is minimal Hubbard-plus-RKKY modelling of 2D metal-organic-framework magnets, exemplified by CrCl2(pyrazine)2 where he reproduces the measured 1.8 μB moment and predicts a 0.9-5 meV Cr-Cr coupling testable by neutron scattering. In parallel he continues a long-running collaboration with Peking University and Delft STM groups on atomic-scale spin coupling—hydrogen-bond-mediated biradicals and d-π-d Fe coordination structures—where he supplies the DFT + modern valence-bond theory that interprets sub-meV exchange measurements. Likely next moves: extend the CrCl2(pyz)2 model to VCl2(pyz)2 and TiCl2(pyz)2 to map the ferrimagnet-to-antiferromagnet-to-Fermi-liquid crossover, and push the spin-coupling framework into dark-matter-detection-relevant magnetic MOFs.

nodes.name: Kim Lefmann
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Kim Lefmann is a condensed-matter physicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose research spans two tightly coupled agendas: neutron scattering as a probe of frustrated and unconventional magnetism, and the design and simulation of neutron instrumentation. On the materials side, his group pursues geometrically frustrated lanthanide compounds, Cairo pentagonal and hyperkagome lattices, electron-doped cuprate superconductors, and quantum spin liquids — using inelastic and polarised neutron scattering down to sub-kelvin temperatures combined with DFT and linear spin-wave theory. On the instrumentation side, he is a primary author of McStas, the dominant neutron ray-tracing package, and contributes heavily to instrument design studies for the European Spallation Source (ESS), including the KVASIR backscattering spectrometer. His work sits squarely inside NBI's ESS-Lighthouse 'Q-MAT' effort, which positions ESS as the engine for Danish quantum-materials neutron science. Lefmann's fingerprint is unusual among his peers: he is both a virtuoso user of the machines and a co-builder of them.
nodes.w_text: Lefmann's current trajectory runs on two coupled tracks. The first is a neutron-scattering campaign on unconventional magnets — geometrically frustrated rare-earth oxyfluorides, Cairo-pentagonal and hyperkagome lattices, electron-doped cuprates, and classical spin-liquid candidates — pushing to sub-kelvin temperatures and polarised inelastic probes. The second is active development of the neutron-scattering toolchain itself: he is a central contributor to the McStas ray-tracing package, recently adding a dedicated spin-wave component (including altermagnetic dispersions) and co-designing the KVASIR backscattering spectrometer concept for the European Spallation Source. The two tracks feed each other: instrument-design simulations are driven by the magnetism he is trying to measure. A parallel line involves soft-matter sample environments (solvothermal vapour annealing chambers with magnetic-field control) for in situ GISAXS of block-copolymer films.

nodes.name: Charles M. Marcus
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Charles M. Marcus works at the intersection of mesoscopic superconductivity and semiconductor-based quantum devices, with a particular focus on hybrid superconductor-semiconductor heterostructures built from epitaxial InAs/Al material systems. His group fabricates gatemon qubits in which two Al-removed segments of an epitaxial nanowire serve separately as a Josephson junction and a transistor, enabling in-situ switching between dc transport and qubit operation, and revealing that multi-mode qubit frequency fluctuations fall below the theoretical universal value. A central long-term theme is the manipulation of Majorana zero modes, approached through non-Abelian holonomy in quantum-dot-coupled systems where five or more Majorana modes produce a degenerate ground-state manifold characterized using random matrix theory, and through spin-resolved spectroscopy of subgap states in an InAs two-dimensional electron gas device proximitized by Al. Marcus develops novel circuit elements, including Y-splitters—superconducting loops with three leads and three Josephson junctions arranged on a two-dimensional star (3,12²) lattice deformable into the kagome geometry—that yield topological superconducting phases with Chern numbers ±2, and phase-biased Andreev diffraction gratings in which a superconducting meander sets phase differences across two-to-ten parallel Andreev scatterers, producing local and nonlocal diffraction patterns consistent with a theory of multiple Andreev scattering. His group uses scanning SQUID microscopy to image local susceptibility in a square-lattice hybrid Josephson junction array, providing the first direct measurement of long-range phase coherence and observing that a finite field smaller than one percent of a flux quantum per unit cell fragments the system into large regions of constant superconducting phase. He investigates nonreciprocal superconducting phenomena including a phase diode effect in InAs/Al superconductor-normal-superconductor junctions, in which asymmetry of the Andreev bound-state spectrum reaches a maximum at 0.15 T applied transverse to the current direction and is attributed to finite-momentum Cooper pairing from orbital coupling to the in-plane magnetic field. His work also extends to automated disorder mitigation in quantum point contacts using the covariance matrix adaptation evolutionary strategy with a step-likeness metric for conductance quantization, and to on-chip bolometric detection of Josephson radiation in which an engineered thermal reservoir converts ac Josephson current at microwave frequencies up to 100 GHz into a measurable dc temperature rise.
nodes.w_text: Charles M. Marcus is building and probing superconductor-semiconductor hybrid circuits that manipulate Cooper-pair transport through geometric and topological means. His group has constructed Andreev diffraction gratings—circuits with 2, 3, 4, and 10 parallel Andreev scatterers—and used scanning SQUID imaging to make the first direct measurement of long-range phase coherence in Josephson junction arrays, discovering that fields below 1% of a flux quantum per unit cell fragment that coherence into large constant-phase domains. They have introduced the Y-splitter circuit element and assembled it into a two-dimensional star-lattice (Archimedean 3,12²) array capable of hosting Chern-number-±2 topological superconducting phases. Next steps include adding current-carrying taps for individual Andreev scatterer phase control, experimentally realizing Y-splitter arrays, and mapping the full topological phase diagram including higher Chern numbers.

nodes.name: Jesper Nygård
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jesper Nygård is an experimental condensed-matter / nanoelectronics physicist in the Condensed Matter section at NBI and a long-standing key figure in the Center for Quantum Devices (QDev). He leads one of the world's strongest programs on semiconductor-nanowire growth (notably InAs and InSb) and the integration of these nanowires with epitaxial superconductors (Al) and magnetic / ferromagnetic insulators (EuS) to form hybrid devices that underpin much of Denmark's topological- and semiconductor-qubit effort. His group is the go-to source of high-quality, bottom-up-grown nanowires used in gatemon, Andreev-level-qubit, spin-qubit, and Majorana / poor-man's-Majorana experiments. Scientifically he has focused on Josephson physics in hybrid nanowire weak links, proximity-induced superconductivity in semiconductors, quantum transport in quantum-dot and double-dot geometries, and more recently the interaction between superconductivity and magnetism in full-shell InAs/EuS/Al wires — including magnetic-texture-controlled superconductivity (domain-wall / multi-domain-averaged), imaged by scanning SQUID magnetometry with sub-mT field control. In parallel he has helped develop nanowire-based Josephson parametric amplifiers (JPAs) with on-chip gate tunability, bringing readout hardware into the same hybrid-material stack as the qubits. His portfolio also extends to photonic / optoelectronic nanoscale devices — including a recent nano-optoelectronic neuron with picowatt operation and biological-style inhibition — positioning his lab at the intersection of quantum-device nanowires and neuromorphic photonics. A central theme across all of this is materials control at the nanometer scale as the enabling technology for new device physics.
nodes.w_text: Nygård's group is active on three tightly coupled fronts. (1) Magnetic-texture-controlled superconductivity in full-shell InAs/EuS/Al nanowires: scanning SQUID + transport shows Al shell superconductivity is present only in multi-domain EuS states, with domain walls that are movable by ~5.5 µm per mT — opening the door to on-chip phase-biased Josephson junctions for Andreev spin qubits and to Josephson-transistor readout of racetrack memories. (2) Hybrid-material readout electronics: gate-tunable Josephson parametric amplifiers built from arrays of parallel InAs nanowires, achieving ~1 GHz tunability and > 20 dB gain near the quantum limit, allowing qubits and amplifiers to share the same materials stack on arbitrary substrates. (3) Neuromorphic photonics: a nanoscale optoelectronic neuron with picowatt operation, 100× smaller footprint than prior photonic neurons, and deterministic excitation + inhibition — a route toward dense, low-power photonic AI hardware and adaptive optical sensing. Underlying all three is the lab's continued focus on nanowire growth and hybrid-material engineering.

nodes.name: Jens Paaske
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jens Paaske is a condensed-matter theorist at NBI whose core research sits at the intersection of mesoscopic transport, correlated electrons, and impurity-superconductor physics, with a long career thread on the nonequilibrium Kondo effect in quantum dots and carbon nanotubes. He has repeatedly developed perturbative renormalization-group and Keldysh Green-function techniques to handle strongly correlated dots under bias, including cotunneling, singlet-triplet crossings, spin-orbit-dressed Kondo ridges, and vibrational (Franck-Condon) sidebands in single-molecule transistors. A second major thread is Yu-Shiba-Rusinov physics: Paaske and collaborators have mapped YSR states in phase-biased S-QD-S junctions, in chains that self-organize into topological superconductors, and most recently in double quantum dots and color-superconducting quark matter, building a transferable 'surrogate model solver' that replaces the superconducting bulk by a few effective levels. He also works actively on Majorana engineering in artificial Kitaev chains built from quantum dots coupled via floating superconducting islands (the 'poor man's Majorana tetron'), and on Shiba/nematic order on iron-based superconductors like FeTe0.55Se0.45. A third, more recent strand is Floquet-driven correlated systems and driven-dissipative circuit-QED: magnon damping in the Floquet-driven Hubbard model, limit-cycle synchronization of microwave resonators coupled through biased DQDs, and reduced-basis methods for Lindbladian steady states. A fourth strand is frustrated magnetism on compressible lattices (Nematic Bond Theory for J1-J2 Heisenberg models), where magnetoelastic coupling converts the nematic transition from continuous to weakly first-order. Across these threads, Paaske's signature is deriving analytically tractable effective theories (Keldysh, NBT, surrogate models, Floquet) that tie experimental signatures in dots, STM of iron-pnictide surfaces, and cQED devices to microscopic parameters.
nodes.w_text: Paaske's most recent pivot is into driven-dissipative quantum matter: Floquet-driven Hubbard magnons that go gain-unstable at a finite wavevector (a masing-like effect in a magnet), and circuit-QED limit-cycle oscillators that synchronize via a shared voltage-biased double quantum dot. Alongside, he is pushing the Nematic Bond Theory onto compressible lattices, showing that magnetoelastic coupling can flip the J1-J2 nematic transition from continuous to weakly first-order on the square lattice. A third active thread is Coulomb-corrected THz response of confined excitons in nanocrystals, delivering a closed criterion A_{10%} for when Coulomb mixing can no longer be ignored. Supporting all three is ongoing work on reduced-basis and surrogate-model solvers for driven-dissipative impurity problems, and on 'poor man's Majorana' tetrons in floating-superconductor quantum-dot arrays.

nodes.name: Johan Peter Uldall Fynbo
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Johan Peter Uldall Fynbo contributed to the multi-wavelength analysis of GRB 210704A, a Fermi/LAT-detected burst at z = 2.34 whose excess optical/infrared emission peaking at T₀ + 7 d (M_r = −22.0 mag) links it to Einstein Probe fast X-ray transients EP241021a and EP240414a and to the luminous fast blue optical transient (LFBOT) population, with the excess modeled as arising from an energetic refreshed shock. He contributed to the study of GRB 240825A, for which SVOM/C-GFT, GTC, VLT, and TNG observations at z = 0.659 revealed an early reverse shock with a magnetic field ratio R_B ∼ 302 relative to the forward shock, a high-energy prompt-emission cutoff at ∼76 MeV constraining the initial Lorentz factor to Γ₀ < 245, and an isotropic-equivalent kinetic energy of 5.25 × 10⁵⁴ erg. He was involved in characterizing EP240801a, an X-ray flash at z = 1.6734 with peak energy E_peak = 14.90 keV and an 80.61 s X-ray lead over gamma-rays, whose early R-band afterglow required modeling with two-component jet, forward-reverse shock, or energy-injection scenarios. He contributed to the discovery paper for EP250827b/SN 2025wkm, an X-ray flash at z = 0.1194 accompanied by a broad-line Type Ic supernova with a double-peaked light curve interpreted as a long-lived magnetar driving winds that break out at ∼0.35c through a circumstellar medium of radius ∼10¹³ cm. He co-authored the panchromatic JWST/NIRCam, NIRSpec, MIRI, and NOEMA characterization of GNz7q at z = 7.1899, confirming super-Eddington accretion (λ_Edd = 2.7 ± 0.4) onto a black hole of log(M_BH/M_☉) = 7.55 ± 0.34 in a host with SFR = 330 ± 97 M_☉ yr⁻¹ and stellar mass log(M_*/M_☉) = 10.5 ± 0.4 lying on the local M_BH–M_* relation. He contributed to the JWST/NIRSpec Prism analysis of z > 9 galaxies in which damped Lyman-α column density modeling and SFR surface densities showed star-formation depletion timescales of ∼10–100 Myr, greatly exceeding the Kennicutt-Schmidt relation and implicating
nodes.w_text: Johan Peter Uldall Fynbo is actively chasing two intertwined puzzles: why NIR-selected red quasars lack the radio excess seen in optically selected samples, and whether quasar redness and BAL prevalence share a common physical driver—possibly orientation or host-galaxy dust rather than selection bias. He is expanding spectroscopic follow-up of Gaia-detected lensed quasar candidates beyond the current eleven systems using joint astrometric+photometric selection criteria, while monitoring sightline B of DR3Gaia2107-1611 to distinguish microlensing from an asymmetric lens potential. He recently built PyLongslit, a transparent Python long-slit reduction pipeline for NOT ALFOSC data, and assembled the 534-quasar eHAQ+GAIA23 NIR-color-selected sample to probe radio properties free of optical-selection artifacts.

nodes.name: Peter Jakobsen
nodes.title: Affiliate Professor
nodes.p_text: Peter Jakobsen works primarily with JWST/NIRSpec multi-object spectroscopy through the JADES and OASIS programmes to characterise galaxies at z > 9. His work on JADES-GS-z14-0 (z = 14.18) includes analysing JWST/MIRI spectroscopy—the longest integration ever acquired by MIRI—to detect [O III]λλ4959,5007 at ~11σ and Hα at ~4σ, yielding a gas-phase oxygen abundance of [O/H] ≈ −1.1 ± 0.4 (~10% Z☉) from strong-line diagnostics and [O/H] ≈ −0.3 from detailed photoionisation modelling, alongside an ionising photon production efficiency of ξion ≈ 10^25.3 Hz/erg and a star-formation rate surface density of ΣSFR ≈ 23 ± 5 M☉/yr/kpc². A distinctive methodological contribution is exploiting the offset in NIRSpec shutter placement between the OASIS and JADES programmes to detect C III]λλ1907,1909 emission spatially displaced by ~400 pc from the stellar continuum in JADES-GS-z14-0, inferring a mass outflow rate of ~160 M☉/yr and a mass-loading factor η = 4–15, which constrains the instantaneous star-formation efficiency in massive haloes to ε★ ≲ 0.08. He also analyses ALMA [O III]88μm emission in JADES-GS-z14-0 at 223.524 GHz, obtaining a dynamical mass of log(M_dyn/M☉) = 9.0 ± 0.2 and a gas fraction of 50–80%. For GS-z13-1-LA at z = 13.1, he uses combined 56-hour NIRSpec/PRISM spectroscopy to confirm Lyman-α emission with a rest-frame equivalent width of 66 Å and a UV continuum slope β ≈ −3, inferring an ionised bubble of R_ion ≈ 0.2 pMpc and an ionising production efficiency as high as ξion ≈ 10^26.4 Hz/erg. He contributes to JADES data-release infrastructure, including photometric selection of 2081 z > 8 candidates across 469 square arcmin of GOODS-S and GOODS-N, spectroscopic redshift validation for 3297 galaxies in Data Release 4, and construction of rest-UV luminosity functions at 6 ≲ z ≲ 9 from tiered NIRSpec observations of 5190 targets.
nodes.w_text: Peter Jakobsen is using deep JWST NIRSpec spectroscopy—anchored by a 56-hour combined OASIS/JADES prism spectrum with bootstrap-covariance calibration—to chase Lyα transmission and ionised-bubble formation at z ~ 13–14, within the first 300–330 Myr after the Big Bang. His immediate puzzles center on GS-z14's spatially offset (~400 pc) CIII] emission, which he suspects traces an active or fossil outflow, and on GS-z13-1-LA's anomalously strong Lyα (rest-frame EW 66 Å, β_UV ≈ −3), whose extreme ionising efficiency challenges stellar models unless a surrounding galaxy overdensity or AGN contribution is confirmed. He plans follow-up spectroscopy to detect additional rest-frame UV lines, pin systemic redshifts, and statistically map early ionised bubbles across z > 10 galaxies.

nodes.name: Sune Toft
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Sune Toft is a professor of astrophysics and the founding director of the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) at NBI/DTU. He works on the formation and quenching of massive galaxies at high redshift, combining multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy (HST, JWST, ALMA, VLT, Subaru) with deep-field surveys to probe when and how the 'red-and-dead' massive elliptical population of today was assembled. He has long been associated with the discovery and characterization of compact, massive quiescent galaxies — the 'red nuggets' — at z~2-3, and with using submillimeter-bright dusty star-forming galaxies (DSFGs) as their star-forming progenitors. Under his leadership DAWN has built the DAWN JWST Archive (DJA), a key community resource of reduced JWST/NIRCam and NIRSpec data, which now underpins a large share of JWST-based galaxy-evolution studies at the Center. The current DAWN program pushes cluster and protocluster science into the z>3 regime — with spectroscopic confirmations of protoclusters like the Cosmic Vine at z=3.44 — studies the emergence of the red sequence and the stellar-to-halo-mass relation in the early universe, and measures the fundamental metallicity relation (FMR) deep into cosmic dawn via direct Te-based auroral-line oxygen abundances. He and his group also exploit COSMOS / CEERS / COSMOS-Web and JWST high-z supernova discoveries for independent cosmology, and continue to probe environmental versus mass quenching on small (<100 pkpc) scales at 1<z<5. Toft's signature is binding massive-galaxy evolution, protocluster / environmental quenching, and ISM/chemical-enrichment physics into a coherent story of galaxy assembly during the first 2 Gyr.
nodes.w_text: Toft and the DAWN group are pushing massive-galaxy assembly studies deep into cosmic dawn. One active thread is environmental quenching at 1 < z < 5, combining Subaru/FOCAS spectroscopic confirmations (e.g. a z=4.53 dense group around a massive QG) with statistical COSMOS-field analysis that shows small-scale (≲100 pkpc) environment — not large-scale overdensity — drives the QG fraction at z > 1.9. A closely related thread characterizes specific protoclusters: the Cosmic Vine at z=3.44, using the DAWN JWST Archive to identify 11 QGs, fit the earliest known red sequence, construct top-heavy stellar mass functions, and infer core halo mass log(M_h/Msun) ~ 13.2. A third thread is ISM chemistry and fundamental scaling relations at z~10, where custom NIRSpec Prism reductions (extended to 5.5 µm) yield direct Te-based O/H and extend the FMR break into the first Gyr of cosmic time. The group is also leveraging COSMOS-Web JWST supernova discoveries for independent cosmology.

nodes.name: Darach Jafar Watson
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Darach Watson works extensively on the spectral properties of high-redshift galaxies and transients, with a particular focus on radiative-transfer modelling of nebular continuum emission, Balmer and Paschen jumps, and recombination processes in dense gas environments. He has developed self-consistent cocoon-based radiative-transfer models to explain the continuum shapes, broad emission lines, and Balmer breaks of "Little Red Dots" discovered by JWST, demonstrating that non-spherical cocoons with comparable amounts of inflowing and outflowing material around supermassive black holes of order a million solar masses can reproduce all observed LRD spectral features without invoking stellar atmospheres, and predicting and confirming correlations between Balmer break strength, Balmer line-absorption, and scattering line width. He applies NLTE radiative-transfer calculations to kilonova AT2017gfo spectra to disentangle strontium and helium contributions to the 1 μm feature, using freshly computed strontium atomic data for electron-impact collisions, photoionization, and recombination to show that strontium self-consistently reproduces the feature's onset from absence at 0.92 days to clear emergence at 1.17 days, while a helium mass comparable to the total ejecta mass would be required at that same epoch. Watson conducts multi-wavelength characterisation of the gravitationally lensed galaxy A1689-zD1 at z~7, combining JWST/NIRSpec IFU spectroscopy and ALMA observations in bands 3, 6, and 8 at ~200 pc resolution to resolve five [CII] 158 μm and [OIII] 88 μm components within 2 kpc and to determine anomalously low dust-to-gas and dust-to-metal ratios—DTG=(5.1⁺³·⁰₋₁.₉)×10⁻⁴ and DTM=(6.1⁺³·⁶₋₂.₃)×10⁻²—an order of magnitude below local galaxies at comparable metallicity. He pushes direct Te-based metallicity measurements using auroral [OIII]λ4363 detections out to z≈10 by extending JWST/NIRSpec Prism wavelength coverage beyond the standard pipeline cutoff to 5.5 μm for 11 galaxies at z=9.3–10.0, constructing an empirical MUV–metallicity relation and demonstrating that the fundamental metallicity relation shows a gradual offset reaching metallicities approximately three times lower at z=10 than at z=0–3, likely driven by massive pristine gas inflows. His work on nebular-dominated galaxies includes modelling the full JWST/NIRSpec Prism spectrum of a z=5.124 CAPERS galaxy to show that a dominant (>95%) nebular continuum with T=(5.3±0.2)×10⁴ K and
nodes.w_text: Darach Watson is pursuing three interlocking frontiers. For Little Red Dots, he is extending Sirocco 2D radiative-transfer models—which already predict self-consistent Paschen and Brackett jump signatures—across broader JWST NIRSpec/PRISM samples to determine whether free-bound recombination in an ionised cocoon below 3000 K fully explains LRD continua without thermalisation. For kilonovae, he is deploying a fresh NLTE collisional-radiative framework with newly calculated strontium atomic data to resolve whether fixed Sr-plus-He abundances can reproduce the 1 µm feature across all epochs, and whether LTE underestimates r-process abundances by a full dex. For cosmic dawn, he is quantifying how nebular-continuum-dominated galaxies—one companion showing zero metal lines at z~5—artificially inflate the UV luminosity function and drive the galaxy overabundance tension.

nodes.name: Steen Harle Hansen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Steen Harle Hansen is a dark-matter theorist at the Dark Cosmology Centre (DARK) whose work spans the phase-space distribution of collisionless dark matter, the internal dynamics of galaxy clusters, and speculative alternatives to the cosmological constant. A recurring theme is deriving universal properties of dark-matter haloes (NFW-like density profile, radially varying velocity anisotropy, power-law pseudo phase-space density ρ/σ3) analytically or phenomenologically rather than purely from N-body simulations. His 2023 analytical phase-space distribution function reproduces NFW density profiles, velocity-magnitude distributions and separate radial and tangential velocity components, with an automatic escape-velocity cutoff relevant for direct-detection experiments. Recent speculative work proposes that accelerated cosmic expansion may be driven by internal velocity dispersion of galaxies or by black-hole growth rather than a cosmological constant, using the Press-Schechter mass function coupled to the Friedmann equation with a free parameter β probing the IR-UV connection of Cohen-Kaplan-Nelson (a simple BH-area model is disfavoured by SN1a data, but a velocity-dispersion-driven model with β ≈ 0.17-0.5 remains viable). A complementary 2023 paper maps out the long-term "destiny of dark matter": massive DM particles spiral into supermassive black holes via gravitational radiation and disappear via Hawking evaporation, while lighter DM particles eject through a run-away evaporation triggered by reduced central potential. He has also contributed to sterile-neutrino warm-dark-matter bounds, turn-around-radius cosmology tests, caustic mass estimators, the dark-matter/gas temperature connection in clusters, and the SHiP physics case.
nodes.w_text: Hansen's current research is anchored on analytically modelling collisionless dark matter rather than relying solely on N-body simulations. His 2023 phenomenological phase-space distribution simultaneously reproduces NFW density, radially varying anisotropy, a power-law ρ/σ³, and separate radial/tangential velocity distributions with an automatic escape-velocity cutoff - directly useful as a prior for underground direct-detection experiments.
