In parallel he has pursued speculative, testable alternatives to the cosmological constant: a δCDM expansion history where accelerated expansion is driven by growing cosmological structure (via Press-Schechter + Friedmann) with a free parameter β capturing IR-UV CKN-style couplings. A BH-area model is excluded by SN1a data, but a velocity-dispersion-driven variant with β ≈ 0.17-0.5 is indistinguishable from ΛCDM. A complementary 2023 paper maps out the long-term "destiny of dark matter" under gravity alone: massive DM spirals into SMBHs and is Hawking-radiated, lighter DM undergoes run-away evaporation via a reduced central potential.

nodes.name: Jens Hjorth
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jens Hjorth works extensively on the connection between gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and supernovae, including identifying the broad-lined Type Ic supernova SN 2024aihh associated with the Einstein Probe X-ray flash EP240801a at z=1.67 as the most distant spectroscopically confirmed GRB-SN, and characterizing the exceptional repeating GRB 250702BDE—which triggered Fermi on three occasions over several hours and was detected by Einstein Probe a day earlier—using VLT infrared imaging, MeerKAT radio observations, and HST astrometry that localized it to a non-nuclear host galaxy position at z~0.2, with prompt emission inconsistent with either collapsar or merger models. He has a sustained focus on strongly lensed transients in galaxy cluster fields, including the spectroscopic classification and microlensing analysis of the multiply imaged Type Ia supernova SN 2022riv in RX J2129, where JWST NIRSpec PRISM and G140M spectroscopy provided the Type Ia classification, SALT3-NIR light-curve fitting yielded a cosmology-independent magnification of 5.35±1.01, and six independent lens models were compared against that measurement at a location with stellar mass density ~1–2 dex higher than SN Refsdal where microlensing is expected to modulate magnification by 20–50%. A recurring theme in his work is the use of Type Ia supernovae as cosmological distance indicators, with particular attention to dust extinction modeling: he develops two-population Bayesian hierarchical frameworks using the bimodal distribution of the SALT2 stretch parameter from the Pantheon+ compilation, finding that the mean extinction coefficient of the population used to propagate Cepheid-calibrated distances is consistent with a Milky Way-like dust model with R_B~4, and obtains H₀=70.59±1.15 km/s/Mpc, reducing the discrepancy with the Planck value by 30–50%; separately, he demonstrates through galaxy-morphology-differentiated dust simulations that assuming a universal exponential reddening prior introduces spurious scatter in extinction properties and may bias relative distances between supernovae from early- and late-type hosts. He applies JWST MIRI-MRS spectroscopy to the four highest-redshift known luminous type-1 quasars at 7.08≤z<7.64, measuring updated black hole masses in the range (4–15)×10⁸ M☉ from Hα, Paα, and Paβ broad lines, modeling hot dusty tori with SKIRTOR to constrain face-on inclinations and opening angles of 40–60°, and estimating torus dust masses of (1–4)×10⁶ M☉ that would be depleted by accretion in only ~5 Myr. His group contributes to the demographics of high-redshift quiescent galaxies,
nodes.w_text: Jens Hjorth is pursuing two intertwined frontiers. In cosmology, he has built a two-population Bayesian hierarchical model for Type Ia supernovae that reveals internal tension in SH0ES calibration data—reddened supernovae corrected with R_B≈3 appear anomalously faint—and is now extending this framework to incorporate Cepheid systematics and survey selection effects for sharper H0 constraints. In galaxy evolution, he assembled a 743-galaxy JWST photometric sample across CEERS, PRIMER, and JADES to map quiescent galaxies at z=2–7, discovering unexpectedly abundant low-mass (M*<10¹⁰ M☉) quiescent systems and two spectroscopically confirmed z>5 examples residing in overdensities. He is now chasing whether protocluster environments drive early quenching and why no simulation reproduces the high-z quiescent mass function.

nodes.name: Marianne Vestergaard
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Marianne Vestergaard works centrally on measuring supermassive black hole masses using reverberation mapping of optical continua, stellar dynamical modeling via the Schwarzschild orbit-superposition method implemented in FORSTAND with SINFONI/VLT K-band spectroscopy of MCG−06-30-15, and Cepheid-based distance calibration with HST using F555W and F814W filters to derive distance moduli for NGC 4303 (μ = 31.083 ± 0.035 mag) and NGC 1068 (μ = 30.150 ± 0.106 mag). She applies intensive multi-band photometric reverberation mapping campaigns using Las Cumbres Observatory in seven bands (u through z_s) at sub-day to daily cadence to probe accretion disk structure in NGC 4051, I Zw 1, Mrk 509, NGC 4151, and NGC 4593. A recurring focus is diagnosing departures from the standard thin-disk τ∝λ^(4/3) reprocessing prediction: in NGC 4051 the i- and z_s-band lags are anomalously large and cannot be explained by a combination of disk emission and diffuse continuum from the broad-line region, making it a notable lag–luminosity outlier, while in Mrk 509 the lag spectrum steepens to τ∝λ^(2.17±0.2) with optical lags a factor of ~5 longer than the thin-disk expectation. She uses PyROA and cross-correlation methods to extract frequency-resolved and time-averaged interband lags, finding in I Zw 1 that high-frequency lags fit a thin-disk profile with a size of 0.61 ± 0.37 light-days while low-frequency lags reveal a secondary reprocessor consistent with the broad-line region, and interprets these results within multi-wavelength frameworks combining Swift XRT/UVOT archival data, ground-based optical monitoring, and mid-infrared interferometry. Her work on high-redshift quasars addresses the assembly of billion-solar-mass black holes during reionization, including quasar lifetime constraints from extended Hα and [O III] 5007 nebulae detected with JWST/NIRSpec IFU observations of five z~6 quasars with small proximity zones, yielding transverse-sightline lifetimes of t_Q ≲ 10^5 yr. She investigates the SMBH–host galaxy mass relation at z > 6 through the SHELLQs survey using JWST/NIRCam and NIRSpec observations of nine quasars at 6.18 < z < 6.4, finding that their location in the M_BH–M_* plane (log M_BH/M_sun ~ 8–9; log M_*/M_sun ~ 9.5–11) is consistent with a non-evolving
nodes.w_text: Marianne Vestergaard is calibrating black hole masses in nearby AGN hosts by pushing Cepheid distance measurements—via multi-epoch HST WFC3 campaigns targeting NGC 1068 and NGC 4303—into direct refinements of reverberation mapping and M_BH–σ* relations. She is chasing a striking ~10× discrepancy between the stellar dynamical mass (4.4×10⁷ M⊙, derived from SINFONI AO spectroscopy and FORSTAND Schwarzschild models) and the reverberation mapping mass in MCG–06-30-15, where a counter-rotating nuclear disk complicates Gauss-Hermite LOSVD extraction. Simultaneously, she is dissecting why I Zw 1's accretion disk appears 2–4× larger than thin-disk predictions across a compiled optical-to-mid-infrared size ladder, with MATISSE and future long-baseline interferometry targeted to resolve whether disk structure genuinely changes with Eddington ratio.

nodes.name: Ian Bearden
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Ian Bearden is a professor of experimental subatomic physics at the Niels Bohr Institute and a long-standing senior figure in Jens Jorgen Gaardhoje's heavy-ion group. He came to Copenhagen in 1994 as a postdoc in high-spin gamma-ray spectroscopy after a Purdue PhD carried out at Argonne, and has worked on ultra-relativistic heavy-ion collisions ever since, moving through BRAHMS at RHIC into a central role in ALICE at the LHC. Within the collaboration his personal fingerprint is on the instrumentation side: he is a co-author (with Copenhagen PhD student Shihai Jia and a small VMM/FoCal-H team) on the characterisation of NDL EQR20 large-area SiPMs and on the first application of the VMM3a ASIC to SiPM-based calorimetry, built to read out the 256-channel FoCal-H hadronic-calorimeter prototype for the ALICE forward upgrade. He is Denmark's IPPOG representative, directs the NBI Ungdomslaboratoriet outreach lab, chairs the Danish Physical Society's Outreach and Teaching section, and is secretary of the EPS Physics Education Division.
nodes.w_text: Bearden's live 2024-2026 work sits at the NBI interface with the ALICE forward upgrade: characterisation of NDL EQR20 SiPMs (May 2025) and the first use of the VMM3a ASIC to read out a 256-channel SiPM prototype of the FoCal-H hadronic calorimeter, with test-beam validation at CERN. He continues to co-author the Gaardhoje-group ALICE physics papers on K*(892) strangeness, partonic flow in pp and p-Pb (Nature Communications, Mar 2026), and deuteron femtoscopy in Pb-Pb, while supervising Copenhagen PhD/MSc students on FoCal-related projects. Alongside the hardware, he is active in outreach and physics-education service: IPPOG Denmark, Ungdomslaboratoriet, the Danish Physical Society, and the EPS Physics Education Division.

nodes.name: Mogens Dam
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Mogens Dam is an experimental particle physicist at NBI whose recent work is anchored almost entirely in detector design and precision-measurement strategy for the Future Circular Collider electron-positron stage (FCC-ee). He is the sole author on several FCC-ee-focused notes that frame specific experimental challenges, including the luminosity monitor design, the Z lineshape precision programme, and the tau-lepton programme at FCC-ee. His luminosity-monitor work sets an absolute-luminosity precision target of 10^-4 and a relative target an order of magnitude better, driving sub-micron geometric tolerances on the small-angle Bhabha tungsten-silicon sandwich detector placed only about 1 m from the interaction point. On the physics side, Dam is a driver of FCC-ee's charged-lepton-flavour-violation programme: with 5x10^12 Z decays, he argues FCC-ee can push B(Z to e-mu), B(Z to e-tau) and B(Z to mu-tau) sensitivities to the 10^-9 level, two orders of magnitude beyond HL-LHC. His tau work targets 1.7x10^11 Z to tau-tau events and statistical precisions at the 10^-5 level on tau lifetime, branching fractions and polarisation, which in turn drive stringent vertex-detector alignment and photon/pi0 separation requirements. Dam sits at the interface of detector R&D, precision electroweak/flavour theory, and the European Strategy process, and his preprints read like a one-person FCC-ee experimental programme.
nodes.w_text: Mogens Dam's ongoing work is centered on turning FCC-ee from a physics aspiration into a concrete detector design. He writes sole-author notes that translate FCC-ee physics targets (Z lineshape, tau programme, CLFV, luminosity) into hardware specs, and feeds them directly into the FCC Feasibility Study for the 2026 European Strategy update. Active threads include the ultra-compact tungsten-silicon luminosity monitor meeting a 10^-4 absolute precision with sub-micron geometric tolerance; vertex-detector alignment tight enough for tau lifetime and CLFV at 10^-9; and the CLD and IDEA detector concepts maturing over the next 3-5 years as HL-LHC upgrades wind down.

nodes.name: Poul Henrik Damgaard
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Poul Henrik Damgaard works at the intersection of quantum field theory and classical general relativity, using scattering-amplitude techniques to derive classical gravitational observables such as two-body scattering angles, momentum kicks, and effective potentials. He applies the Post-Minkowskian (PM) expansion systematically, with explicit results at fourth PM order representing the first complete calculation at that order based on scattering amplitudes, and with confirmation of universality and high-energy behavior of gravitational interactions in maximal supergravity at third PM order. A recurring methodological commitment is the perturbiner and recursive approach to solving Einstein's equations in de Donder gauge, which he has used to reconstruct the Schwarzschild metric to all orders as a convergent geometric series agreeing with the known non-perturbative expression, and the metric of a multipolar star to second PM order in terms of generalized tensor bubble integrals in momentum space, recovering the Kerr solution as a special corner of the multipole space. He employs and has helped clarify the KMOC formalism, demonstrating its equivalence to the worldline formalism and showing explicitly how the Keldysh-Schwinger in-in formalism is contained in both, illustrated in detail for the momentum kick at second PM order. The exponential representation of the gravitational S-matrix is a tool he has developed and advocated, showing that the two-body scattering angle equals the corresponding matrix element of the exponential representation to all PM orders in the conservative sector, with new corrective terms entering at fourth PM order once gravitational radiation is included, and that this framework differs from the eikonal formalism in specific computational details. His work on the Effective One-Body formalism introduces an energy-dependent effective metric in isotropic coordinates combined with a redefined angular momentum map, which eliminates the need for non-metric corrections order by order in the PM expansion and reduces by canonical transformation to the standard angular momentum map. He derives scattering angles for probes in Kerr metrics resummed to all orders in the Kerr spin, extends these to spinning probes at second order in probe spin, and verifies agreement with amplitude and worldline results when truncated at third PM order. Across these projects the unifying technical signature is the extraction of classical physics from loop integrands via soft-region or classical-limit methods, demonstrated explicitly for black-hole scattering at second PM order in arbitrary spacetime dimensions and in four-dimensional Einstein gravity coupled to scalars.
nodes.w_text: Damgaard is pushing Post-Minkowskian (PM) gravitational two-body dynamics beyond 4PM by combining three interlocking toolkits: a recursive perturbiner framework that computes the Schwarzschild and Kerr metrics in de Donder gauge to all orders in G via generalized 1-loop bubble integrals; a multipole-extended iterative scheme producing the 2PM metric to arbitrary multipole order for stationary sources; and a KMOC–exponential S-matrix combination that has already proven the scattering angle equals the N̂ matrix element in the conservative sector. He is now extending all three to non-stationary sources, dissipative sectors beyond 4PM, and higher spin orders, while pursuing the precise linkage between tidal Love numbers and the multipole expansion—currently the sharpest unresolved question in his framework.

nodes.name: Peter Lodahl
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Peter Lodahl's group works on waveguide quantum electrodynamics with InAs(Ga)As quantum dots embedded in photonic-crystal and suspended GaAs nanostructures as the central technology for photonic quantum computing and quantum networks. Recent results include a telecom O-band quantum-coherent photon-emitter interface with transform-limited linewidths only 8% broader than the inverse lifetime and a 41.7 MHz emission rate under 80 MHz π-pulses; direct observation of long-range waveguide-mediated resonant Förster energy transfer between two distant emitters via an antidip in g^(2)(τ) under single-emitter driving, plus selective population of the subradiant state; and a waveguide-engineered artificial molecule of two quantum dots 13 μm (26 effective wavelengths) apart whose emission direction is switched by the relative driving phase and which produces single photons on one port while photon pairs come out the other. On the architecture side they publish a complete fusion-based photonic quantum computing blueprint with time-bin qubits, reconfigurable entangled-photon sources, a microsecond logical clock cycle and explicit error thresholds for quantum-dot hardware. Other capabilities: programmable nonlinear multi-mode photonic circuits using a tunable QD as single-photon nonlinearity, indistinguishability purification of photons via heralded interference (+10.2% in high-noise regime), a photon-correlation non-Gaussianity criterion √g^(3)+3√g^(2) ≥ 2 violated to 100σ, contextuality-based semi-device-independent QKD, and QD coupling across 30-600 MHz to a suspended-beam GaAs optomechanical resonator.
nodes.w_text: Lodahl's current frontier is scaling waveguide-QED with semiconductor quantum dots from single emitters to engineered multi-emitter artificial molecules and a full fault-tolerant photonic-computing architecture. His team built a bidirectional photonic-crystal waveguide with independent electrical trenches where two QDs 13 μm apart act as a phase-switchable directional source, emitting single photons on one port and photon pairs on the other, with a feasibility analysis extending to three-QD and four-QD chips. On the quantum information front he introduced and experimentally violated the attenuation-resistant √g^(3)+3√g^(2) ≥ 2 non-Gaussianity criterion with InAs(Ga)As QD photons at 100σ significance, and demonstrated contextuality-based semi-device-independent QKD from the same source. These device results feed a concrete fusion-based photonic quantum computer blueprint with time-bin encoding and microsecond logical cycles.

nodes.name: Klaus Mølmer
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Klaus Mølmer's recent work spans the theory of Rydberg atom quantum processors, photonic qubits in chiral waveguides, and resource-theoretic aspects of entanglement. Several papers treat multi-qubit gate synthesis on neutral-atom arrays: quantum optimal control of continuous laser pulses to realize controlled-phase, Toffoli and Fredkin (controlled-SWAP) gates with fidelities near 99.74%, and exploiting interfering transition pathways to lift degeneracies for a direct C-SWAP under Doppler broadening at ~150 μK. On the photonic side, he and collaborators design a control-Z gate and Bell-state analyzer using repeated scattering off a two-level emitter in a one-sided waveguide with dispersion-engineered harmonic trapping of Gaussian pulses. He also develops delay-aware quantum-network formalisms that avoid full continuum mode quantization for Ramsey-type pulse interactions, and entangled-sensor architectures using Lieb-Mattis states and collective cavity emission for common-mode-rejecting differential phase measurement approaching the Heisenberg limit. On the foundational axis he studies adiabatic theorems with vanishing boundary derivatives (infidelity scaling as 1/T^{n+1}) benchmarked on the Aquila Rydberg processor, and a channel Schmidt number that characterizes stochastically non-entangling maps via witness-preserving dual maps. Further themes include superradiant echoes from NV spin sub-ensemble gratings in microwave cavities and supercoherence stabilized by long-range interactions in disordered synthetic-dimension systems.
nodes.w_text: Mølmer's current frontier braids three threads. In waveguide QED he engineers harmonic-trap dispersion plus phase shifts so that a single two-level emitter, scattered against repeatedly, executes near-unit-fidelity photonic control-Z and Bell-state gates. On Rydberg quantum simulators (including the Aquila processor) he proves and demonstrates that vanishing boundary derivatives of the time-dependent Hamiltonian give adiabatic infidelity scaling as 1/T^{n+1}, providing a hardware-visible advantage even under dissipation. On entanglement theory he introduces the channel Schmidt number via convex-roof construction, proves that stochastically non-entangling maps are Schmidt-number monotones, and derives witness-based Bell-like inequalities certifying entangling dynamics. The unifying move is identifying the minimal structural ingredient — smoothness, probabilistic Kraus components, dispersion-engineered trapping — that controls quantum resource generation.

nodes.name: Eugene Simon Polzik
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Eugene Polzik leads the Quantum Optics effort at NBI, where his group specializes in coupling light to macroscopic material systems - room-temperature atomic spin ensembles, soft-clamped SiN membrane oscillators, and hybrid combinations of the two - to generate and exploit non-classical states at scales and frequencies most quantum-optics experiments cannot reach. A hallmark is his 'negative-mass' spin oscillator, built from a collectively polarized atomic ensemble whose effective inertia can be engineered to be negative, enabling back-action-evading measurements and virtual rigidity schemes that beat the standard quantum limit in gravitational-wave detection and acoustic-frequency metrology. He builds two-color, highly nondegenerate continuous-variable EPR sources from optical parametric oscillators, with coherent-control stabilization that recently demonstrated 9 dB of two-mode squeezing across acoustic sidebands, and combines them with atomic ensembles to form hybrid quantum networks linking disparate wavelengths. In parallel he pushes cavity optomechanics with ultra-coherent soft-clamped membranes (Q factors of order 10^8) to deliver ponderomotive squeezing from multiple mechanical modes simultaneously and to create non-classical photon-phonon correlations from the center-of-mass motion of nanogram oscillators. His work also spans quantum-enhanced magnetometry (high-field optical cesium magnetometry for MRI, biomagnetic sensing) and proposals for continuous-variable quantum repeaters. The unifying theme across his program is that squeezed and entangled light, when coupled to well-engineered macroscopic oscillators, becomes a practical resource for sensing at the quantum noise floor.
nodes.w_text: Polzik's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to turn hybrid quantum optics into a practical broadband quantum sensor. He has pushed a two-color nondegenerate OPO to 9 dB of acoustic-band EPR squeezing via single-seed coherent phase control, and coupled one of its beams to a frequency-tunable atomic spin oscillator engineered as negative- or positive-mass - demonstrating wavelength-disparate, octave-wide quantum noise reduction relevant for gravitational-wave detectors and CV quantum repeaters. In parallel his group runs soft-clamped (Q~10^8) SiN membranes in cryogenic MIM cavities to produce simultaneous ponderomotive squeezing from two mechanical modes at MHz frequencies. The unifying drive is to generate and deliver squeezed/entangled light at tunable wavelengths and at acoustic sidebands, where quantum-limited sensing actually matters for GWDs and biomedical quantum sensing.

nodes.name: Albert Schliesser
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Albert Schliesser's group centres on soft-clamped silicon-nitride membrane optomechanics pushed to new regimes and circuits. Their signature devices reach Q ~ 10^8 at MHz frequencies, and the team exploits this to demonstrate 17 dB (piezo) / 21 dB (capacitive) thermomechanical quadrature squeezing via parametric modulation with feedback stabilization of the anti-squeezed quadrature, and 4.8/4.2 dB simultaneous ponderomotive squeezing from two localized membrane modes at 1.32 MHz and 2.43 MHz in a cryogenic MIM cavity. Beyond single resonators they build soft-clamped valley-Hall topological phonon waveguides that reach 3 dB/km propagation loss at room temperature (orders of magnitude below prior chip-scale phononics) with >99.99% probability of transmission around 120-degree bends, and image these modes using a frequency-detuned stroboscopic collimated-beam interferometer over 20 mm² at 6 μm resolution. Related lines include an optomechanically induced transparency memory with T_1 ≈ 23 ms and 40% retrieval at room temperature, a 2D GaP optomechanical crystal with g_0/2π = 450 kHz at 7.7 GHz mechanical frequency in the resolved-sideband regime, milliKelvin MIM optomechanics with actuator-free free-space beam locking, and NV-center-membrane hybrids using cobalt FEBID nanomagnets with 170 kT/m gradients preserving 20 μs spin coherence up to 25 kT/m gradient.
nodes.w_text: Schliesser's frontier is extending soft-clamped SiN optomechanics from individual resonators into phononic circuits and hybrid platforms. He and collaborators built a valley-Hall topological phonon waveguide with 3 dB/km loss, verified >99.99% transmission around 120-degree bends using high-resolution ultrasound spectroscopy, and then imaged its MHz modes over 20 mm² at 6 μm resolution via a frequency-detuned stroboscopic collimated-beam interferometer that replaces hours-long raster scans. In parallel they produced 4.8/4.2 dB simultaneous ponderomotive squeezing from two localized membrane modes at 1.32 and 2.43 MHz in a cryogenic MIM cavity, extending squeezed-light bandwidth beyond an octave in a single device. A new hybrid direction couples a FEBID cobalt nanomagnet to an NV-center spin with a directly measured 170 kT/m gradient while preserving 20 μs coherence up to 25 kT/m, pointing to "magnet on oscillator" high-Q phononic sensors.

nodes.name: Jacob Friis Sherson
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Jacob Friis Sherson operates at the intersection of quantum physics research and the broader sociotechnical ecosystem surrounding quantum technologies, combining experimental and theoretical quantum optics work with systematic studies of education, workforce development, and policy. He is a central figure behind the **Quantum Composer** software platform, using it as a vehicle for educator-developer dialogue to teach the SUPER principle (Swing-UP of the quantum emitter population) applied to tunneling in a double-well potential, and mapping the two lowest energy states of the 1D Schrödinger equation onto the Bloch sphere to visualize qubit time dynamics. His group applies deep neural networks to predict quantum optimal control cost landscapes, demonstrating order-of-magnitude improvements in accuracy and speed over Bayesian methods and enabling on-the-fly experimental optimization across the phase diagram of the landscape. He has contributed to topological quantum matter research, studying Hofstadter-type models for hardcore bosons on fractal lattices derived from the Sierpinski carpet using exact diagonalization, finding that particle densities accumulate in locally square-lattice-like regions in a manner potentially incompatible with fractional quantum Hall physics, and proposing implementation via ultracold atoms in optical lattices. A recurring methodological thread is the use of large language models with human-in-the-loop validation to classify 3,641 job postings revealing strong QT job market concentration in the United States and Europe, and NLP-based topic modeling of 62 national quantum strategic documents from 20 countries revealing a temporal policy shift away from basic science toward commercialization. He has developed the Quantum Curriculum Transformation Framework (QCTF), a four-step decision-tree-based model demonstrated using quantum teleportation as a worked example within a quantum communication curriculum, and has surveyed 86 quantum master's programs globally, documenting their growth since 2021 and increasing use of company internships as pathways to industry. His outreach work is institutionally embedded in the EU Quantum Flagship's QTEdu pilot projects, encompassing quantum games including the Virtual Quantum Optics Laboratory and ScienceAtHome platform, and a structured PhD outreach portfolio training program conducted with an international network of quantum physics doctoral students.
nodes.w_text: Jacob Friis Sherson is mapping the full pipeline from national quantum strategy to workforce reality. Using a BERTopic-modeled corpus of 62 quantum strategic documents spanning 25 years and a classified dataset of 3,641 job postings, he has already shown that policy rhetoric has pivoted sharply toward commercialization while the job market remains dominated by large firms with few startups. His immediate work involves GPT-based extraction of granular skills from job posts keyed to the Competence Framework for Quantum Technologies v3.0, cross-national comparison of which quantum areas countries pursue individually versus collaboratively, and a pilot study connecting the 86 master's programs he surveyed globally to actual graduate career outcomes through student interviews.

nodes.name: Anders Søndberg Sørensen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Anders Søndberg Sørensen works at the intersection of quantum optics and quantum information, with a sustained focus on using waveguide QED to engineer collective light-matter interactions between spatially separated quantum emitters such as quantum dots and trapped atoms. He develops theoretical frameworks for exploiting superradiant and subradiant collective states together with the quantum Zeno effect to dissipatively drive systems from arbitrary initial states toward W-type entangled steady states, with infidelity scaling inversely with cooperativity and explicit implementation analyzed for trapped ¹³³Cs atoms. He studies how a dispersive dipole-dipole interaction between quantum dots separated by 13 µm—corresponding to 26 effective wavelengths—in a bidirectional photonic crystal waveguide shifts collective-state energies to produce switchable left-to-right emission directionality and directional photon statistics, including single photons on one port and photon pairs on the other. A recurring theme in his work is the design and optimization of photon-photon gates achieved through repeated scattering off a single two-level emitter in a chiral or one-sided waveguide equipped with second-order dispersion elements and temporal phase shifters that trap photon pulses in a Gaussian shape, with numerical optimization yielding a control-Z gate fidelity of ~99.2% and success probability of ~99.6% at N=17 scatterings, and a Bell-state measurement success probability of ~98% at N=5 scatterings. He analyzes quantum repeater architectures based on the DLCZ scheme, specifically investigating how multimode emission from SPDC sources affects end-to-end entanglement fidelity, finding that very narrow Gaussian drive pulses are crucial for pulsed operation while the temporal acceptance window of clicks relative to the heralding time is the critical parameter for continuous driving, and identifying optimal pulse widths or acceptance windows for each swap depth. His work on hybrid quantum networks combines SPDC photon-pair sources, rare-earth-ion absorptive ensemble memories, and trapped-ion processors, solving the bandwidth-matching problem between these systems to enable parallel execution of probabilistic entanglement-generation steps and high-rate ion-ion entanglement over hundreds of kilometers, and comparing double-click and single-click approaches at ion edge nodes with respect to phase-stability requirements and efficiency constraints. He develops multiplexing strategies—including single-click quantum multiplexing and multi-server multiplexing—that exploit coherence between quantum channels and account for time-varying decoherence rates to surpass semiclassical multiplexing limits in applications such as remote state preparation between many end-user devices and a single quantum node. He contributes to photonic fault-tolerant quantum computing by proposing fusion-based implementations of Calderbank-Shor-Steane qLDPC codes—including a case study on Bivariate Bicycle codes—using photonic resource states deterministically produced by quantum emitters, and benchmarking performance under erasures from fusion failure or photon loss and Pauli errors. He also derives an attenuation-resistant quantum non-Gaussianity criterion based on the inequality
nodes.w_text: Anders Søndberg Sørensen is pushing waveguide-coupled quantum dot (QD) systems from two toward three and four resonant emitters by engineering trench-based local frequency tuning on photonic chips, using feasibility frameworks that project 0.98 success probability on 100-waveguide two-trench designs. He just demonstrated directional collective emission in a bidirectional waveguide through interference—a first for the optical regime—and built a reflection-based remote state preparation (RSP) protocol that simultaneously prepares multiple qubits via a single transmitted photon across exponentially many temporal modes. His numerical optimizer found that only five scattering events yield ~96% Bell-state fidelity and that a single reused emitter achieves ~99.2% CZ gate fidelity. Next he targets deterministic emitter-relative waveguide fabrication, realistic noise modeling, and integration of multi-qubit RSP into actual quantum network architectures.

nodes.name: Lars Frølund
nodes.title: Affiliate Professor
nodes.p_text: Lars Frølund is an Affiliate (Adjungeret) Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute, University of Copenhagen, but his intellectual centre of gravity lies in innovation policy and deep-tech investment rather than in physics research. He holds a PhD in Philosophy from Aarhus University and in 2005 helped establish one of Denmark's first units for university-industry collaboration. He is a Lecturer at MIT Sloan (formerly Research Director of the MIT Innovation Initiative, Visiting Fellow, and Fulbright Scholar) and co-authored the 2018 Elsevier volume 'Strategic Industry-University Partnerships: Success-Factors from Innovative Companies' with Max Riedel, drawing on case work with BMW, IBM, Novo Nordisk, Rolls-Royce, Siemens and others. His intellectual fingerprint sits at the intersection of mission-driven innovation agencies, deep-tech venture finance, and the geopolitics of technological sovereignty.
nodes.w_text: Frølund's current work is concentrated on mission-driven innovation, deep-tech investment architecture, and European technological sovereignty. He served as a Board Member of the European Innovation Council (Jan 2021 – Oct 2025) and chairs the international expert group advising the European Commission on EIC implementation. He was Special Advisor at NATO HQ (2021-2022) for the creation of the Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA) and NATO's EUR 1 bn Innovation Fund, and Special Advisor on Digital and Technological Sovereignty to Executive Vice-President Margrethe Vestager. He teaches innovation ecosystems at MIT and writes regularly for Project Syndicate and Science|Business — including an April 2026 essay with Fiona Murray on fund-of-funds approaches as instruments of economic statecraft, and earlier pieces arguing Europe must learn to embrace 'heretical' ideas and mobilise pension-fund capital to close its innovation gap with the US.

nodes.name: Jácome Saldanha N d O B Armas
nodes.title: Affiliate Professor
nodes.p_text: Jay (Jácome) Armas is Associate Professor of theoretical physics at the University of Amsterdam and Affiliate Professor at the Niels Bohr Institute, Copenhagen, with a PhD from NBI (2012) under Niels Obers. His work spans string theory, higher-dimensional black holes, holography, and the hydrodynamics of soft and active matter, and he coordinates the Dutch Institute for Emergent Phenomena (DIEP). He is a co-architect of the blackfold approach, which treats black branes as hydrodynamic objects living on dynamical worldvolumes and has become a standard tool for constructing higher-dimensional horizon geometries; he has recently used it to build new asymptotically (anti)-de Sitter black holes in (super)gravity (Armas & Nicosia, JHEP 09 (2024) 047). On the fluid side he has helped develop Carrollian fluids and the spontaneous breaking of boost symmetry (Armas & Have, PRL 132, 161606), ideal fracton superfluids (Armas & Have, SciPost 16, 039), and the formulation of magnetohydrodynamics via one-form global symmetries. He also founded the international outreach platform Science & Cocktails and received the EPS Outreach Prize.
nodes.w_text: Armas's 2024-2026 output pushes hydrodynamic effective theory into several new regimes at once. With Have and Nicosia he is building a null fluid/gravity correspondence (arXiv:2602.20268, Feb 2026) and a theory of 'null matter' as the ultrarelativistic, zero-temperature limit of hydrodynamics (arXiv:2509.25320). With Jain and Lier he has formulated hydrodynamics of thermal active matter using Schwinger-Keldysh EFT (PRE 112, 055401) and a notion of temperature for active nematics (PRR 8, L022011). On the MHD side, Lier, Jain, Armas and Porth reformulated resistive relativistic MHD through higher-form symmetry to avoid Ampère-law stiffness (PRD 112, 083059), with astrophysical simulation in view. He is also working on spin hydrodynamics and chiral anomalies (with Batzios) and higher-group symmetry on the M5 brane.

nodes.name: Vitor Cardoso
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Vitor Cardoso is a theorist at the intersection of gravitation, gravitational-wave astronomy and fundamental physics at NBI, known for a prolific program on black holes in realistic, matter-filled environments. His signature is to take 'pure' analytic black-hole physics (quasinormal modes, superradiance, tidal deformability, ringdown) and systematically enrich it with the effects that real astrophysical settings bring: accretion disks and turbulent fluids, dark-matter halos and ultralight-boson clouds ('gravitational atoms'), galactic stellar matter, plasmas, extended mass distributions. A flagship thread over the last few years has been 'black holes in galaxies' / 'environmental effects for GW science', including quasinormal modes of black holes embedded in dark-matter halos, gravitational-wave tails and memory from mergers in astrophysical environments, environmental friction in extreme-mass-ratio inspirals, and signatures detectable by LIGO/LISA. A second thread is black-hole mimickers and exotic compact objects - boson stars, Q-balls, solitonic stars, 'gravitational atoms' - and how they reveal themselves as chirp mimickers, ringdown signatures or pseudospectrum instabilities. A third thread is superradiance and energy extraction from rotating compact objects: axionic couplings, floating orbits, neutron-star stellar superradiance, plasma effects, and superradiance from viscous objects. He also works on beyond-GR precision ringdown via effective-field-theory corrections to Kerr, including newly constructed numerical rapidly rotating black-hole backgrounds. More recently he has brought relativistic dissipative hydrodynamics (BDNK) into compact-object physics, characterizing viscosity-driven 'eta-modes' in neutron stars and avoided crossings with w-modes. He regularly co-authors community white papers (Next-Generation EHT, Snowmass, black-hole spectroscopy) that set the agenda. Across everything, his signature is pulling concrete, testable GW predictions out of realistic black-hole-plus-environment settings.
nodes.w_text: Cardoso's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to make black-hole and compact-object gravitational-wave physics honest about environments, viscosity, and beyond-GR corrections. He has pushed realistic EMRI waveforms into bosonic dark-matter stars, showing ordinary boson stars can act as chirp mimickers through dynamical-friction-driven scalar dissipation. He has built the first systematic BDNK-hydrodynamic framework for non-radial NS oscillations and uncovered new viscosity-driven 'eta-modes' and avoided crossings with w-modes, opening a route to asteroseismology-based measurement of dense-matter transport coefficients. He has extended EFT corrections to Kerr ringdown into the rapidly spinning regime up to a/M=0.99 using numerical backgrounds and a pseudo-spectral method. In parallel he has explored stellar superradiance in dense nuclear media, time-dependent total-absorption 'black-out' phenomena, gravitational-atom spectroscopy, and tidally perturbed black-hole three-body problems. The unifying drive is turning precision ringdown, EMRIs and stellar oscillations into quantitative tests of environments, dark matter, and beyond-GR gravity.

nodes.name: Charlotte Fløe Kristjansen
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Charlotte Fløe Kristjansen works at the intersection of integrable spin chains and defect conformal field theories, with a sustained focus on computing exact one-point functions of local operators in the presence of co-dimension-one defects and line defects embedded in $\mathcal{N}=4$ super-Yang-Mills and ABJM theory. She systematically exploits the integrable structure of the AdS/CFT correspondence by expressing one-point functions as overlaps between Bethe eigenstates and matrix product states, and has derived a closed-form overlap formula for the alternating $SU(4)$ spin chain describing the scalar sector of ABJM theory at arbitrary bond dimension, as well as summation formulas for the $SO(6)$ sector of the D3-D5 domain wall that incorporate inhomogeneities, twists, and quantum dressing via the representation theory of the twisted Yangian $Y^+(4)$, filling the last analytical gap in that system. Her work on Gukov-Witten surface defects in $SU(N)$ $\mathcal{N}=4$ SYM at large $N$ establishes that ordinary Gukov-Witten defects depending on continuous parameters are generically non-integrable, whereas rigid Gukov-Witten defects at a corner of the discrete parameter space are integrable, yielding closed-form factorised leading-order one-point functions for unprotected operators built from adjoint scalars and raising the possibility of an all-loop formula in that sector. She has developed a perturbative framework for the 't Hooft line in $\mathcal{N}=4$ SYM, using it to exactly reproduce S-duality predictions for correlators with Wilson loops and chiral primaries, and demonstrating at leading perturbative order that correlators with non-protected bulk operators are efficiently captured by integrability, as a byproduct uncovering new integrable overlaps in $\mathfrak{sl}(2)$ spin chains in non-standard representations. Her investigations of defect CFT observables include a manifestly superconformal, closed-form expression at finite $N$ for leading-order two-point functions of all single-trace chiral primary operators in the presence of a co-dimension-one Nahm pole defect, built on an exact result for traces of fuzzy spherical harmonics combined with $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ generators, and the computation of type-B Weyl anomaly coefficients and the displacement operator two-point function for the D3-D5 domain wall system starting from the improved energy-momentum tensor of $\mathcal{N}=4$ SYM. Most recently she has connected these integrable field-theory structures to quantum information concepts by defining black hole states in the Heisenberg spin chain via thermal correlators in holography, and characterising them through entanglement entropy, emptiness formation probability, and Krylov complexity, finding logarithmic entropy growth with effective central charge $c=5.2$ and evidence for thermalization at infinite temperature.
nodes.w_text: Charlotte Fløe Kristjansen is working at the intersection of integrability and defect CFT, using spin-chain methods to probe black hole physics, Gukov-Witten surface defects in N=4 SYM, and domain walls in ABJM theory. She recently established that the spin-chain "black hole state" thermalizes at infinite temperature with an anomalously large effective central charge c ≃ 5.2, derived closed-form one-point functions for rigid Gukov-Witten defects, and discovered that integrability holds only at the discrete corner k=2—not generically. She built a K-matrix solution for the SL(2) sector at k=2 and diagonalized the full fluctuation spectrum around the ABJM domain wall. She is now chasing an all-loop one-point function formula for the k=2 defect, extending ABJM integrability beyond tree level, and implementing the boundary conformal bootstrap via SUSY localization.

nodes.name: Niels Anne Jacob Obers
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Niels Obers works at the intersection of non-Lorentzian geometry and string/M-theory, systematically constructing Carroll, Newton-Cartan, and torsional string Newton-Cartan (TSNC) geometries that emerge from controlled speed-of-light and BPS decoupling limits of type II string theory and M-theory. A central preoccupation is the duality web connecting non-relativistic string theory (NRST), matrix theories on D-branes, discrete light-cone quantisation (DLCQ), and holographic correspondences: he showed that T- and S-duality transformations between ten-dimensional non-Lorentzian backgrounds exhibit novel asymmetric properties in which the codimension of the foliation structure of the dual geometry may change depending on the choice of transformation and the value of the background fields, an asymmetry that underlies non-commutativity and Morita equivalence in matrix and gauge theory. He developed the "BPS road to holography" framework in which a single BPS decoupling limit induces the bulk near-horizon limit leading to AdS/CFT, while consecutively applying two such limits generates further holographic examples with non-Lorentzian bulk geometries, and he showed these deformations are related to TT̄-type flow equations for Dp-brane DBI actions. On the worldsheet side, he derives Nambu-Goto and Polyakov formulations for strings whose worldsheet simultaneously carries Galilean or Carrollian structure, and performs BRST quantisation of the carrollian bosonic string—whose worldsheet and target spacetime are both carrollian—establishing that its BRST cohomology is finite-dimensional, displays Poincaré duality, and induces unitary irreducible representations of the 26-dimensional Carroll group, with the residual gauge algebra isomorphic to the three-dimensional extended BMS algebra. He has obtained Palatini-type formulations of the Galilei and Carroll expansions of general relativity by expanding the Einstein-Palatini action, recovering the known equations of motion at next-to-leading Galilean order and showing that the leading-order Carroll Palatini action reduces on shell to electric Carroll gravity. His 1/c² post-Newtonian expansion framework for the complex Klein–Gordon field on curved backgrounds produces a covariant coupling prescription for the Schrödinger equation and its post-Newtonian corrections order-by-order in 1/c², illustrated explicitly by expanding the Kerr metric to O(c⁻²) to yield a special case of the Hartle–Thorne metric with novel potentially measurable effects. His work on Carroll geometry extends to de Sitter holography, where he shows that Carroll-like geometry in a hypothetical de Sitter holography mirrors the role played by Galilei-like geometry in matrix theory and AdS/CFT, and he uses infinite boost limits of M-theory on instantonic objects to generate examples of holography with a Carroll-like bulk. He additionally derives hydrodynamic equations of perfect fluids without boost invariance from kin
nodes.w_text: Niels Obers is currently pushing BRST quantisation of carrollian and spin matrix theory strings, having built a full BMS₃-based BRST framework for the carrollian bosonic string whose cohomology is strikingly finite-dimensional, localised at zero energy, and organised into representations of the 26-dimensional Carroll group. Immediate targets include quantising the N=(1,1) carrollian superstring with critical charge c_L=30, computing its cohomology, and extending the procedure to the full p-brane carrollian family. He is pursuing carrollian graviton vertex operators and scattering amplitudes, and investigating Carroll geometry as the organising principle for de Sitter holography. In parallel, he is extending duality-asymmetry results—including a U-duality lift connecting DLCQ and non-relativistic M-theory—to Calabi-Yau compactifications and heterotic corners.

nodes.name: Irene Tamborra
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Irene Tamborra works at the intersection of neutrino physics and multi-messenger astrophysics, with a particular focus on how neutrino flavor conversion affects compact-object environments including core-collapse supernovae, magnetorotational collapses, collapsars, and neutron-star merger remnants. She solves multi-energy, multi-angle neutrino quantum kinetic equations in three flavors—accounting for non-forward collisions and advection within a spherically symmetric shell surrounding the neutrino-decoupling region of an $18.6\,M_\odot$ progenitor—finding that flavor equipartition is achieved at $t_{\rm pb}=1$ s, where fast flavor instabilities are triggered by angular crossings, but not at $t_{\rm pb}=0.25$ s, where only slow collective effects operate, with neutrino heating rates increasing by up to 30% relative to the no-conversion case. A recurring methodological concern is the diagnosis of electron-neutrino lepton number (ELN) crossings, where she benchmarks the maximum entropy, Minerbo, and polynomial weighting reconstruction schemes against full Boltzmann solutions for an $18.6\,M_\odot$ progenitor with and without muons and proto-neutron star convection, finding that the polynomial method and Minerbo closure suffer severe limitations while maximum entropy captures most forward crossings. She explores how proto-neutron star convection shifts the neutron-star radius outward—favoring ELN crossings at larger radii—while muon creation causes contraction and pushes crossings to smaller radii across three nuclear equations of state. She models steady-state flavor configurations in one-dimensional inhomogeneous media using single-crossed angular distributions, showing that the advective term cascades flavor waves to small spatial and angular scales and that flavor equipartition combined with cancellation of spectral ELN crossings is a special solution dependent on the degree of neutrino–antineutrino asymmetry rather than a generic outcome. For magnetorotational collapses of a $13\,M_\odot$ progenitor simulated in three-dimensional neutrino-magnetohydrodynamics, she finds resonant matter-driven flavor conversion alongside chirality-flipping interactions from a magnetic moment $\mu\lesssim10^{-12}\,\mu_B$ in a field $B\simeq10^{15}$ G, with the predicted IceCube and Hyper-Kamiokande event rates peaking at 400–600 ms after bounce and varying strongly with jet orientation. For collapsar disks, she computes neutrino and gravitational-wave signals from global axisymmetric viscous hydrodynamic simulations, finding that the neutrino-cooled NDAF phase produces $\mathcal{O}(10)$ MeV neutrinos detectable by IceCube within $\mathcal{O}(10$–$100)$ kpc, with shock-oscillation time variations at $\mathcal{O}(10
nodes.w_text: Irene Tamborra is actively pursuing multi-messenger signatures from magnetorotational core-collapse supernovae, probing how spin-flavor precession, MSW conversion, and neutrino self-interactions collectively shape neutrino flavor evolution in strongly magnetized, jet-driven collapses simulated with a 3D special-relativistic MHD code for a 13 M☉ progenitor. She built direction- and time-dependent oscillation pipelines feeding predicted IceCube and Hyper-Kamiokande event rates, revealing strong polar-versus-equatorial observer asymmetries. In parallel, she constructed a steady-state radiation-mediated shock solver—coupling leptonic and hadronic acceleration under variable upstream magnetization—finding that synchrotron self-absorption reshapes shock profiles even at σ_u ≃ 10⁻⁸ and collisionless subshocks emerge at σ_u ≳ 0.1, with σ_u ≳ 1 regimes and nonlinear pair-production runaway still unresolved.

nodes.name: Konstantin Zarembo
nodes.title: Professor
nodes.p_text: Konstantin Zarembo is a theoretical high-energy physicist at NBI whose career has been built around integrability as a computational and conceptual engine for gauge/string duality and strongly coupled quantum field theory. His signature contributions include pioneering work on Wilson loops in N=4 super-Yang-Mills - especially BPS Wilson loops, their matrix-model reductions, and precision tests of AdS/CFT - and on the integrability of worldsheet sigma-models in AdS5xS5, AdS4xCFT3 and AdS3xCFT2 backgrounds, including the Bethe Ansatz for superconformal spin chains in planar gauge theory. A second long-running thread attacks the less-symmetric cousins of N=4: N=2* and mass-deformed ABJM matrix models, where he has mapped out large-N phase transitions, non-conformal holography, and precision holographic tests. More recently he has extended integrability to defect CFTs, D3-D5 boundary states and AdS/dCFT one-point functions, and into t'Hooft and Wilson loops on the Coulomb branch, where he uses dynamical reflection matrices and integrable bootstrap to compute nonperturbative one-point functions. His current arc pushes in several surprising directions: (i) formulating 'black hole states' in integrable spin chains as a holographic probe of chaos and thermalization; (ii) showing that finite-gap (algebro-geometric) soliton spectra emerge as the semiclassical large-rank limit of the thermodynamic Bethe Ansatz in the Gross-Neveu / O(2N) model, connecting soliton theory to quantum integrability via Dynkin-diagram data; (iii) exporting hydrodynamic intuition from high-energy physics into condensed matter, including a geometry-induced 'skin effect' in viscous electron flow in clean 2D materials that explains recent scanning-NV magnetometry experiments on graphene. Across decades his signature is using exact integrability - Bethe equations, localization, matrix models - to get quantitative grip on strongly coupled physics that would otherwise be inaccessible.
nodes.w_text: Zarembo's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to push integrability into new territory on three fronts. First, he has used Wilson and 't Hooft loops on the Coulomb branch of N=4 SYM - with D3-brane boundary states and dynamical reflection matrices - to compute exact one-point functions and map phase diagrams of circular Wilson loops across the Gross-Ooguri transition. Second, in Copenhagen-led collaborations he defines 'black hole states' in the Heisenberg spin chain from holographic thermal correlators and finds non-integrable, logarithmically entangled behavior with effective central charge ~5.2, a probe of thermalization and chaos. Third, he has built a bridge between quantum integrability and classical soliton theory: the large-rank limit of the O(2N) Gross-Neveu thermodynamic Bethe Ansatz reproduces finite-gap Dirac spectra on elliptic Riemann surfaces, with the spectrum dictated only by the Dynkin diagram. In parallel he has started exporting these methods into condensed matter, explaining a geometry-induced skin effect in viscous electron flow via Brinkman equations. The unifying drive is universality - extracting structures that transcend the specific integrable model.

nodes.name: Troels Haugbølle
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Troels Haugbølle develops and applies high-resolution, multi-scale magnetohydrodynamic simulations of star-forming regions, with a particular focus on the DISPATCH computational framework, including recent work on GPU-accelerated MHD solvers using OpenMP target offloading that achieved a 9.8× overall speedup comparing one NVIDIA A100 GPU to 12 CPU cores on large-scale 3D tests, with the core MHD update routine running two orders of magnitude faster on the GPU than on a single CPU core. He works extensively on sink-particle sub-grid algorithms, including a novel preferential binary accretion recipe that uses a virtual binary sink particle to redistribute accreted mass between overlapping sink particles, eliminating non-physical suppression of accretion onto the secondary when the primary dominates the potential and smoothing accretion rates for unresolved close binaries in eccentric orbits. His research on protoplanetary disk formation challenges the standard isolated-collapse picture by demonstrating through tracer-particle analysis and an analytical derivation of angular momentum scaling in supersonic turbulence that Bondi-Hoyle accretion from parent clouds can supply both the mass and the angular momentum necessary to explain the observed sizes of Class II disks, and predicts that JWST can detect Bondi-Hoyle trails around Class II stars with 80–100% success using only a 2-minute exposure when targeting stars with accretion rate above 5×10⁻¹⁰ M☉/yr and luminosity above 0.5 L☉. He simulates the streaming instability in protoplanetary disks using computational domains up to 6.4 gas scale heights on a side—roughly 1,000 times the volume of typical domains—showing that azimuthal filament extent is limited to approximately one gas scale height and forming up to 4,000 planetesimals to characterize the steep exponential tapering at the high-mass end of the planetesimal initial mass function. He conducts zoom-in simulations with AU-scale resolution of binary protostellar systems, following evolution through circumbinary disk formation and finding that disk sizes are regulated periodically by the binary orbit with larger radii near apastron, while bolometric temperature oscillates between Class 0 and Class I appearances on timescales tied to both the binary orbit and episodic accretion events. He has developed a non-parametric, deep-learning-based methodology ("Simulated Analogues") to rank large libraries of synthetic protostellar images against specific observations of binary and multiple systems, with the goal of removing hand-picking biases and identifying the physical processes driving protostellar evolution. His work bridges planet formation and cosmochemistry through leaching experiments on aqueous alteration minerals in chondrite meteorites that reveal extreme ⁹⁶Zr enrichments, demonstrating that the Solar System's zirconium isotope variability reflects mixing between supernova-seeded interstellar ices and an ice-free rocky component processed thermally in the protoplanetary disk. He also contributes simulation analysis infrastructure through the
nodes.w_text: Troels Haugbølle is pushing on three interlocked fronts. He recently built a preferential binary accretion recipe in RAMSES, a GPU-accelerated MHD solver via OpenMP offloading in DISPATCH, and large-domain streaming-instability simulations in the Pencil Code forming ~4,000 planetesimals. His puzzles include why primary stars dominate accretion contrary to idealised predictions, why streaming-instability filaments are azimuthally finite rather than ring-like (artificially inflating planetesimal masses in small boxes), and how to eliminate host-GPU transfer bottlenecks through asynchronous MPI-style memory handling. Next, he will extend binary accretion studies to lower mass ratios and eccentric orbits, integrate a particle-in-cell solver into DISPATCH targeting solar-flare particle acceleration, and run lower self-gravity simulations to match cold classical Kuiper Belt object masses.

nodes.name: Uffe Gråe Jørgensen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Uffe Gråe Jørgensen leads development of the MSG framework, an iterative coupling of the MARCS 1D radiative-convective atmosphere code, the StaticWeather/DRIFT microphysical cloud formation code, and the GGchem chemical equilibrium code, applied across effective temperatures from 300 K to 3000 K to model cool stars, brown dwarfs, and exoplanets, with molecular abundance sequences tracking the observationally defined M, L, T, and Y spectral transitions. His atmospheric modelling work specifically targets the unsolved problem of reproducing the silicate cloud absorption feature near 10 μm seen in JWST and Spitzer observations, finding that a grid using TiO₂ nucleation produces spectra that are too red in the near-infrared, while SiO nucleation and reduced mixing efficiency yield less red slopes, yet neither reproduces the observed silicate feature. He combines 1D self-consistent atmosphere grids with 3D General Circulation Models by iteratively coupling the expeRT/MITgcm GCM with a kinetic cloud formation model to study the warm Saturn HATS-6b orbiting an M-dwarf, finding that cloud opacity feedback causes a temperature inversion at the sub-stellar point and evening terminator and produces a silicate spectral feature near 10 μm detectable with JWST MIRI LRS. His group investigates the physical conditions required for lightning in protoplanetary disks using the ProDiMo 2D thermo-chemical code, identifying lower midplane regions where electron abundances fall below n_e/n_H < 10⁻¹⁵, charged dust grains dominate negative charge carriers, NH₄⁺ dominates positive charge carriers, and turbulence-driven electric fields remain several orders of magnitude below the critical breakdown threshold. He conducts laboratory experiments on mineral SiO₂ particles of 50 nm diameter in an atmosphere chamber under low- and high-humidity conditions, using a Scanning Mobility Particle Sizer to demonstrate that gamma irradiation inhibits aggregation into larger clusters and shifts particle charging toward more negative states at a statistically significant level, favouring a population of many small particles over fewer large ones. In solar system science, he applies convex inversion and the SHAPE radar-optical modelling software to derive a 5.7860 ± 0.0001 hour spin period and a 520 m bi-lobed concave shape model for the Apollo-group contact binary (388188) 2006 DP14, combining February 2014 delay-Doppler radar images with 16 optical lightcurves collected between 2014 and 2023. He has participated in observational campaigns supporting planetary defence missions, contributing Danish 1.54 m telescope photometry at La Silla to measure DART impact ejecta fading rates of 0.115 ± 0.003 mag/d in the first week and to document the emergence of anti-solar dust tails and sunward ejecta extending at least 4000 km
nodes.w_text: Uffe Gråe Jørgensen is pursuing three interlocking frontiers: planetary formation, substellar atmosphere modeling, and 3D cloudy GCM coupling. He recently built a semi-analytic 1D pebble-accretion disk model revealing that dynamic temperatures suppress gap formation and that intermediate turbulence (α=10⁻³) optimizes planet growth; next steps include adding gaseous envelopes, migration effects on pebble composition, and streaming-instability-motivated embryo masses. He constructed the MSG cloudy atmosphere grid—coupling MARCS, DRIFT, and GGchem—and is chasing why no forward model reproduces JWST's ~10 µm silicate feature in VHS 1256 b, suspecting underestimated nucleation rates or patchy clouds. For warm Saturn HATS-6b, he built an iterative expeRT/MITgcm–microphysics pipeline revealing cloud-induced jet narrowing and an upper-atmosphere temperature inversion, targeting JWST NIRSpec/MIRI confirmation of morning/evening terminator asymmetries.

nodes.name: Morten Bo Madsen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: The arXiv record retrieved for "Morten Madsen" is dominated by large-collaboration Majorana topological qubit work in InAs-Al hybrid heterostructures: single-shot interferometric parity measurements of tetron devices yielding τ_X = 14.5 μs and τ_Z = 12.4 ms, passing the Topological Gap Protocol in semiconductor-superconductor devices with extracted topological gaps of 20-60 μeV, and a four-generation roadmap for fault-tolerant topological qubit arrays using measurement-based braiding and lattice-surgery demonstrations. Earlier single-author-scale papers on the list treat tunable Yu-Shiba-Rusinov subgap states fit with numerical renormalization group theory in InAs nanowire-Al devices, Rashba spin-orbit tuning by a factor of two via opposed side-gate voltages measured through weak anti-localization, and selective-area-growth rate control of GaAs/InGaAs nanowires under variable substrate temperature and beam flux. Also present are Fourier-lens imaging scatterometry and alignment-free 2D grating characterization using differential-evolution algorithms on hexagonal (700 nm pitch) and checkerboard (3 μm pitch) structures. Note: the section-filtered astro-ph query for Morten Bo Madsen returned no hits, so this corpus was assembled via author-only fallback and reflects a homonymous condensed-matter Morten Madsen rather than the Astrophysics and Planetary Science faculty member.
nodes.w_text: Across the three full-text papers, a homonymous Morten Madsen appears as a co-author on Niels Bohr Institute Center for Quantum Devices work: gate-defined N-nanowire-S devices in InAs/Al probing Yu-Shiba-Rusinov subgap states through NRG-fitted transport spectroscopy; multigated InAs nanowires in which opposed side-gate voltages enhance Rashba spin-orbit coupling by a factor of two via weak anti-localization; and a Fourier-lens imaging scatterometer that uses differential-evolution optimization to characterize 2D diffraction gratings alignment-free. These papers fall outside the Astrophysics and Planetary Science section the faculty member belongs to, and the individual intellectual fingerprint of the correct Morten Bo Madsen is not attributable from this arXiv-sourced record.

nodes.name: Pól Martin Bendix
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Pól Martin Bendix is a biophysicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose long-term research sits at the intersection of single-molecule biophysics, membrane mechanics, and optical manipulation of living cells. His landmark early work on reconstituted active biopolymer networks driven by molecular motors helped establish how motor-generated forces restructure actin gels in vitro. The publicly crawlable record for this pipeline is thin (only one paper with a usable abstract was recovered), but his wider body of work — not captured by the arXiv/Pure query here — has focused on optically-trapped nanoparticles probing plasma-membrane mechanics, membrane nanotubes, curvature-sensitive membrane-binding proteins, and bacterial cell envelope mechanics. Bendix operates primarily through lab-scale biophysics experiments rather than arXiv preprints, which explains the sparse harvest. His NBI group is a key biophysics-experimental anchor bridging physics and cell biology.
nodes.w_text: Only one archive-grade record was recovered for Bendix in this run, a 2009 paper on active biopolymer networks driven by molecular motors, and the auto-retrieved abstract was a publication date stub rather than a real abstract. No confident signal about his current trajectory can be extracted from this pipeline's harvest. His wider known research program centres on optical trapping, plasma-membrane mechanics, and membrane-curvature biophysics, but those directions are not represented in the artifacts gathered here.

nodes.name: Amin Doostmohammadi
nodes.title: Associate Professor - Promotion Programme
nodes.p_text: Amin Doostmohammadi is a biophysicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose signature research program is on active nematic matter and its role in biology. He is one of the central theorists of the field that treats cell layers, bacterial suspensions, cytoskeletal networks, and tissues as active liquid crystals — with half-integer topological defects serving as organising units for cell extrusion, division, flow, and morphogenesis. His most cited results include connecting active nematic topological defects to cell elimination and tissue dynamics, bridging microscopic cell dynamics to nematohydrodynamic continuum theory, and characterising polar/nematic crossovers in self-propelled matter. His current agenda pushes active matter onto curved substrates (cell-induced wrinkling, spiral waves on folded surfaces like the human cortex) and into flow-coupled topological phase transitions. He runs a prolific group funded by Novo Nordisk, Villum, and an ERC grant (PhysCoMeT), with many short-author-list papers indicating strong individual intellectual leadership. His fingerprint is both theoretical and simulation-heavy, often tied to specific biological experiments collaborators bring.
nodes.w_text: Doostmohammadi's group is pushing active-nematic theory in two directions simultaneously. One pulls active matter onto curved and deformable geometries: a framework showing that cortical folding actively stabilises spiral waves in the brain through Laplace-Beltrami-modulated diffusion gradients, and a Föppl-von Kármán + phase-field model of how single cells wrinkle soft substrates and thereby mediate cell-cell mechanical communication. The other asks how hydrodynamic coupling rewrites the textbook picture of topological phase transitions: flow alignment in nematics destroys the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless scenario, producing persistently unbound defects via emergent bend-splay walls — and self-generated flows in active nematics do the same. Common thread: topological defects as units of non-equilibrium dynamics, shaped by geometry and by flow.

nodes.name: Namiko Mitarai
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Namiko Mitarai is a theoretical biophysicist in the Biophysics section of the Niels Bohr Institute whose work spans microbial ecology, bacterial physiology, and — earlier in her career — granular and soft-matter physics. Her central research programme asks how phage–bacteria ecosystems sustain high diversity: she has argued that structural, physical, and physiological constraints (spatial refuges inside dense colonies, metabolic-state-dependent phage adsorption, "kill-the-winner" network interactions, and even phages preferring metabolically active E. coli via λ tail-protein receptor hyperdiffusion) are the real mechanisms keeping strain diversity stable. She also works on bacterial persistence and antibiotic tolerance: with Himeoka she proved that the optimal wake-up lag time from starvation exhibits a sharp discontinuous transition as antibiotic severity rises, and that bet-hedging into multiple lag-time phenotypes converts this into a continuous transition — with direct clinical implications for antibiotic dosing frequency. On the evolutionary-theory side she has co-derived a scaling law Nm^α = const for the balance between within- and among-collective evolution in hierarchical life (protocells, multicellular organisms), linking collective size N and mutation rate m through a selection-strength-dependent exponent. Her earlier papers on Bagnold scaling, dense granular flows, shear thickening, and wet granular materials show a toolbox grounded in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics that she now deploys on living matter. She frequently collaborates with Kim Sneppen, Kunihiko Kaneko, and Yusuke Himeoka.
nodes.w_text: Mitarai's current work sits at the seam between microbial ecology and bacterial physiology. Building on the 2023 review with Sneppen and Marantos, she is developing quantitative models of phage–bacteria coexistence that combine spatial structure in dense colonies, host-physiology-dependent phage adsorption and yield, and network-level "kill-the-winner" dynamics. A parallel thread continues the Himeoka collaboration on dormancy, lag-time distributions, and antibiotic persistence, increasingly connecting to resistance evolution and cancer drug-tolerance analogies. At the evolutionary-theory level she is extending the Nm^α scaling framework for multilevel evolution toward specific major transitions. Expect new papers combining agent-based simulations with minimal analytical models, probably with Kim Sneppen, exploring feast–famine cycles, stripe formation and pattern formation in motile bacteria, and the ecological consequences of phage defence systems such as CRISPR and toxin–antitoxin modules in structured environments.

nodes.name: Martin Cramer Pedersen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Martin Cramer Pedersen works at the intersection of soft condensed matter physics, active matter, and mathematical biology, with a recurring focus on how geometry and topology shape physical and biological systems. He employs persistent homology analyses combined with simulations of three-dimensional colloidal gels doped with active particles to reveal how local energy injection by active dopants produces highly porous yet compact, percolated gel structures, and to demonstrate how passive interfaces play a topologically distinct role in two versus three dimensions. His work on active nematics spans both dipolar and quadrupolar stress formulations, combining microtubule-kinesin experiments with an active lyotropic continuum model to show that friction-induced hydrodynamic screening promotes quadrupolar activity and drives hierarchical folding that follows the intrinsic bend instability of the nematic layer. He investigates topological defect dynamics in active nematics, including anti-hyperuniform critical states marked by slowed defect density relaxation, giant number fluctuations, and long-range defect clustering that scales with system size, validated against large-field-of-view endothelial monolayer experiments. A distinct thread in his research applies the complex Ginzburg-Landau equation on curved and folded surfaces—using the Laplace-Beltrami operator to encode curvature as a spatial modulation of effective diffusion—to predict that progressive smoothing of realistic cortical folding geometries erases spiral wave structures that are otherwise stabilized and localized by the brain's own surface geometry. He has contributed to small-angle scattering methodology through Shape2SAS, a web application that calculates scattering intensities and pair distance distributions from user-defined geometrical subunits, supports multi-contrast particle construction, and generates simulated data with realistic noise for validating analytical fitting models. Across scales, his methodological toolkit integrates analytical theory, numerical simulation, and experimental collaboration, with applications ranging from active matter pattern formation to neural dynamics and functional material design.
nodes.w_text: Martin Cramer Pedersen is pursuing three interlocking frontiers: how curved cortical geometry—modeled via the Complex Ginzburg-Landau equation on realistic folded brain surfaces using Laplace-Beltrami operator formalisms—constrains spiral-wave dynamics independently of synaptic connectivity; whether an active topological defect phase transition in nematics belongs to a definable universality class, with anti-hyperuniform density fluctuations originating from self-enhancing defect nucleation rather than phase separation; and how quadrupolar, friction-dominated activity governs microtubule-kinesin mixtures on solidifying substrates. He is now working to couple cortical geometry with anisotropic connectivity, resolve the hydrodynamic screening mechanism switching dipolar to quadrupolar activity, characterize defect-phase-transition vorticity fields, and define geometric order parameters for non-equilibrium curvature-driven pattern formation.

nodes.name: Ala Trusina
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Ala Trusina is a theoretical biophysicist in NBI's Biophysics section whose work sits at the interface of cell biology, physics, and network theory. Her early papers (2003-2005) developed quantitative measures of hierarchy in complex networks and introduced a 'degree landscape' analogy that links topological features of scale-free networks to their function. Since then, she has shifted toward the dynamics of cellular regulation and multicellular organisation, building minimal mechanistic models that isolate which circuit features matter. With collaborators she showed that NF-kB's characteristic primary pulse plus noisy secondary oscillations is not accidental: three nested negative feedbacks (IkBa, IkBe, A20) separate the control of period and amplitude, and noise in the secondary oscillations stabilises concentric cytokine waves in tissue while discouraging chronic inflammation. Her comparative work on sigma32-based (E. coli) and HrcA-based (L. lactis) heat-shock circuits showed that very different regulatory topologies produce nearly identical dynamics but impose opposite constraints on chaperone-TF binding affinities. More recently, she has contributed to 3D vasculogenesis models where apical-basal and planar cell polarity rules alone generate connected vascular networks and growth-induced buckling, plausibly explaining the density-invariant vasculature of pancreatic Islets of Langerhans. Her style is parsimonious, emergent, and strongly computational-experimental in spirit.
nodes.w_text: Trusina's research continues to build minimal mechanistic models that reveal which circuit and cell-polarity features drive observable biology. Her 3D vasculogenesis framework with Kirkegaard, Nielsen and Sneppen shows that alignment of apical-basal and planar cell polarity alone produces percolating tube networks, convergent extension, and growth-induced buckling - a plausible explanation for the density-invariant, tortuous vasculature of pancreatic Islets of Langerhans. In parallel, her signalling work shows that noisy low-amplitude secondary NF-kB oscillations actively stabilise cytokine wave propagation and prevent chronic inflammation. Across bacterial heat-shock systems she demonstrates that architecturally opposite circuits can produce the same dynamics but impose opposite chaperone-binding-affinity constraints. The thread is: parsimonious rules, emergent structure, and a willingness to reinterpret 'noise' as function.

nodes.name: Ivanka M Orozova Bekkevold
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Ivanka M. Orozova-Bekkevold is a subsurface / reservoir-geophysics researcher in the Climate and Geophysics section at NBI, working on the mechanics of sedimentary basins and the engineering applications of that knowledge to the subsurface energy transition. She is a single-author contributor (her recent publications are sole-authored) with a distinctive niche at the boundary between computational basin modeling and applied reservoir engineering. Her core methodological tools are forward-modeling of post-depositional processes — evolving pore pressure, stress/strain fields, and overpressure build-up — in clay-rich overburdens above reservoir rock, with a focus on the Danish sector of the North Sea Central Graben (wells North Jens-1, Fasan-1) and the Chalk Group. She explicitly targets applications beyond classical oil and gas: carbon capture and storage (e.g. the Green Sand CCS project), underground CO2 storage, and geothermal-aquifer utilization. In parallel she has developed techno-economic modeling of geothermal energy systems coupled with organic Rankine cycles, using the open-source 'Geothermal Reinjection Lifetime Prediction Model' to forecast reservoir-property degradation and optimize levelized cost of electricity against reservoir-degradation scenarios. The combined geomechanics-plus-energy-systems scope is unusual, and it lets her translate basin-scale simulation outputs directly into practical constraints on carbon storage capacity, well design, and geothermal-plant lifetime economics.
nodes.w_text: Orozova-Bekkevold's current work lies at the intersection of basin geomechanics and the engineering of the subsurface energy transition. One thread uses forward numerical modeling of post-Danian depositional processes — sedimentation rate, sea-level changes, water-depth effects — to predict present-day overpressure and stress-strain fields in the clay-rich overburden of the Danish sector of the North Sea Central Graben, with explicit application to CO2 storage site selection (Green Sand project) and geothermal reservoir utilization.
