A second thread builds integrated techno-economic assessments of geothermal-plus-organic-Rankine-cycle plants, coupling the open-source Geothermal Reinjection Lifetime Prediction Model to ORC performance calculations in order to identify the design power output that minimizes levelized cost of electricity under realistic reservoir-degradation scenarios. The combined ambition is to provide end-to-end, physics-consistent input for Danish CCS and geothermal projects, from basin-history geomechanics to plant-lifetime economics.

nodes.name: Trine Dahl-Jensen
nodes.title: Affiliate Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Trine Dahl-Jensen is a seismologist in the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section whose published record concentrates on the deep crustal and upper-mantle architecture of volcanic rifted margins, particularly the Southeast Greenland margin and the Greenland-Iceland Ridge. Her technical signature is combining multichannel seismic reflection, wide-angle onshore/offshore refraction, gravity and drilling data into joint tomographic velocity models that resolve continent-ocean transitions at roughly 30-40 km horizontal resolution. She co-developed a hybrid ray-tracing inversion (graph method plus local ray bending) with Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis that achieved ~0.05-0.10 km/s velocity uncertainty over 350-km transects, and used velocity-thickness relationships as a proxy for bulk igneous composition to constrain mantle melting during breakup. Her North Atlantic breakup synthesis argued that along-margin variations in magmatism from 1800 to 300 km³/km/Myr are driven mainly by active upwelling rather than temperature, with a modest transient thermal anomaly of 100-125 °C, and that a 300-km-radius plume head impacted the margin around 61 Ma. She has also mapped the seaward-dipping reflector sequences and the abrupt 25-km continent-to-zero crustal thinning of the southeast Greenland shelf along the ODP Leg 152 drilling transect. Her published arXiv/Pure trail is concentrated in the 1998-2001 window on plume-margin interaction, suggesting current work most likely continues under the Climate and Geophysics section's Greenland lithosphere and GLISN seismic-network programmes.
nodes.w_text: Dahl-Jensen's available publication trail (via the NBI Pure fallback) concentrates in 1998-2001 on joint refraction-reflection tomography of the Southeast Greenland rifted margin, plume-margin interaction at the Greenland-Iceland Ridge, and the crustal architecture of ODP Leg 152 drill transects. Public records do not surface more recent first-author arXiv or preprint activity, so her current research direction cannot be attributed from this record alone. Her NBI Climate and Geophysics affiliation suggests ongoing involvement in Greenland lithospheric seismology and the GLISN/Greenland broadband seismic network, but specifics are not inferable from the available abstracts.

nodes.name: Vasileios Gkinis
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Vasileios Gkinis is an ice-core paleoclimate scientist in the Climate and Geophysics section at NBI, specializing in stable water isotopes (delta-18O and delta-D) as paleothermometers for Greenland and Antarctic ice cores. His most distinctive methodological contribution is the exploitation of molecular diffusion in the firn column: because heavy-isotopologue diffusion in the porous snow pack is temperature-dependent and attenuates the high-frequency part of the isotopic record in a predictable way, the power spectrum of a high-resolution delta-18O time series itself encodes past surface temperature. Using this 'diffusion-length' and 'differential-diffusion' approach, he and collaborators have reconstructed 16,000 years of temperature from the NorthGRIP core, capturing the Bolling-Allerod, Younger Dryas, and the Holocene climatic optimum, and benchmarked six different diffusion-based temperature estimators across thirteen Holocene sections from Greenland and Antarctica. A parallel strand is instrumentation: Gkinis led the development of a continuous-flow-analysis (CFA) front end for Wavelength Scanned Cavity Ring Down Spectrometers (Picarro) and a continuous-stream flash evaporator that vaporises sub-microliter water samples at 170 C with 100 percent efficiency for online VSMOW-SLAP-calibrated delta-18O and delta-D analysis of continuously-melted ice cores. This enabled isotope measurements in the field (NEEM 2010 season in North Greenland) at precisions of 0.1/0.5 permil comparable to laboratory IRMS. He also pairs water-isotope diffusion models with steady-state firn-densification models and ice-flow models (Dansgaard-Johnsen) to invert for accumulation and temperature histories, and he has shown that the ice-flow thinning function at NorthGRIP overestimates past accumulation by about 10 percent at 8 ky b2k. His research sits at the interface of instrumentation, signal processing (optimal filtering, spectral inversion), firn physics, and paleoclimate reconstruction.
nodes.w_text: Gkinis' arxiv-visible line stops in 2018, but the thread it culminates is a decade-long programme turning the firn column into a quantitative paleothermometer. He established that the power spectrum of high-resolution delta-18O in a polar ice core, once corrected for temperature- and accumulation-dependent molecular diffusion in the firn, directly yields past surface temperature, and he benchmarked six estimators across Greenland and Antarctic Holocene sections, finding the single-isotope diffusion length most precise (~1 C). In parallel he built the laser-spectroscopy instrumentation (continuous-flow analysis plus flash evaporator calibration of IR cavity ring-down spectrometers) that makes such records practical in the field, already deployed during NEEM. Ongoing work at NBI on current-generation cores (EGRIP, Antarctic deep cores) is expected to continue both methodological strands.

nodes.name: Aslak Grinsted
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Aslak Grinsted is a glaciologist at the Niels Bohr Institute in the Climate and Geophysics section who works on the mechanical and hydrological behavior of the Greenland Ice Sheet, with a recurring focus on the Northeast Greenland Ice Stream (NEGIS). His group combines airborne and ground-based radar surveys, ice-core observations, satellite DInSAR measurements, and numerical ice-flow models (including PISM, the Parallel Ice Sheet Model) to constrain how ice deforms over timescales from days to millennia. A distinctive thread in his work is the treatment of ice not as a simple isotropic fluid but as a material whose crystal orientation fabric produces direction-dependent hardness, which he has shown can make ice stream interiors over an order of magnitude harder in along-flow extension while softening the shear margins. He also revisits and extends classical firn rheology (Gagliardini-Meyssonnier) into multi-dimensional, transient problems such as the collapse of firn tunnels and densification contrasts across shear margins. Another strand uses ensembles of hundreds of paleo ice sheet simulations, anchored to ice-core-derived Holocene elevation histories, to attribute Greenland's past sea-level contribution and argue the ice sheet is still dynamically responding to deglacial forcings. A third strand, enabled by high-resolution InSAR, maps episodic subglacial drainage cascades hundreds of kilometers inland and shows how transient basal hydrology modulates ice-stream flow speed. Across these threads, his signature is tying microphysical or sub-kilometer process understanding to continental-scale mass-balance and sea-level questions.
nodes.w_text: Grinsted's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to make ice flow modelling honest about microstructure and hydrology. He is pushing a 2D nonlinear-viscous firn rheology toward ice-sheet-scale applications, calibrating it against new Greenlandic sites and transient tunnel-collapse experiments. In parallel he is using ensembles of Holocene PISM simulations anchored to ice-core elevation histories to separate deglacial legacy from present-day forced mass loss, arguing Greenland is still dynamically adjusting. A third thread uses DInSAR to map episodic subglacial drainage cascades hundreds of kilometers inland on NEGIS and tie them to transient ice-flow speedups. The unifying question is how anisotropy, firn compressibility, and basal hydrology jointly control ice-stream discharge and therefore Greenland's sea-level contribution.

nodes.name: Helle Astrid Kjær
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Helle Astrid Kjær works with the EGRIP ice core from central Greenland and the Mount Brown South (MBS) ice core from coastal East Antarctica (69.111°S, 86.312°E) as high-resolution archives of past atmospheric and climatic change. For the MBS core, she applies the Copenhagen continuous flow analysis (CFA) system to produce meltwater electrolytic conductivity, sodium (Na⁺), ammonium (NH₄⁺), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), and insoluble microparticle records at 1 mm depth resolution across a 1,137-year archive (873–2008 CE), and has provided a detailed quality assessment of the system's precision and functional resolution. In the EGRIP core, she reconstructs high-resolution iodine fluxes spanning the last 15.7 kyr, finding that iodine fluxes were higher during the cold periods of the Last Glacial Termination (15.7–11.7 kyr BP) than during the Holocene, attributable to enhanced long-range transport of iodine adsorbed onto dust and sea-salt aerosols under stronger glacial wind regimes. She integrates EGRIP iodine data with complementary proxies from RECAP, NEEM, and marine sediment cores to show that Holocene iodine sources shifted toward marine emissions from expanded seasonal sea ice and biologically active subpolar North Atlantic waters, before declining through the Neoglacial alongside regional cooling and reduced ocean productivity. Her mercury work on the same EGRIP interval (15.7–9.0 kyr BP) identifies a threefold increase in depositional mercury fluxes from the Last Glacial Termination into the early Holocene, and uses atmospheric chemistry modelling combined with sea-ice proxies to attribute this increase to oceanic mercury evaporation and atmospheric bromine chemistry coinciding with abrupt regional warming. Together, these findings link past changes in sea-ice extent, ocean productivity, and wind regimes to variability in iodine's role in tropospheric ozone depletion and new particle formation, and to natural mercury cycling with consequences for Arctic ecosystem contamination.
nodes.w_text: Helle Astrid Kjær is building high-resolution ice-core proxy records to interrogate how natural climate variability—not just anthropogenic forcing—shapes Arctic biogeochemical cycles. She recently produced an EGRIP iodine flux record spanning 15.7 kyr, an Arctic mercury archive from 15.7–9.0 ka BP coupled to an atmospheric bromine-sea ice chemistry model, and a 1137-year continuous flow analysis dataset from the 290 m Mill Island (MBS) core, archived at the Australian Antarctic Data Center. She is now chasing why iodine transport efficiency drops toward the Greenland interior, what drove the Neoglacial iodine decline, how a threefold glacial-to-Holocene mercury increase propagated into Arctic ecosystems, and what paleoclimate signals are encoded in the previously underrepresented coastal East Antarctic MBS record.

nodes.name: Sune Olander Rasmussen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Sune Olander Rasmussen is a paleoclimate researcher in the Climate and Geophysics section at the Niels Bohr Institute, specialising in high-resolution Greenland and Antarctic ice-core records of the last glacial period. His recent output combines three complementary threads: (i) bipolar ice-core synchronization via volcanic tie-points over the entire last glacial period with collaborators including Svensson, Dahl-Jensen, and Steffensen, producing a unified chronology that lets Greenland and Antarctic records be compared at sub-decadal precision; (ii) conceptual dynamical-systems modelling of Dansgaard-Oeschger (DO) events using an extended Stommel framework with an internal feedback parameter on Antarctic Bottom Water formation, co-authored with Melcher, Halkjaer, and Ditlevsen; and (iii) proxy-based reconstruction of tropical monsoon response to Greenland interstadials, using speleothem delta-18-O records from the Indian and East Asian summer monsoon regions interpreted with an isotope-enabled climate model. The common thread is using ice-core stratigraphy as a master clock against which global paleoclimate archives can be aligned and dynamically interpreted. Methodologically he sits at the interface of field ice-core science, stratigraphic data analysis, and low-order climate dynamical modelling, and distinguishes himself by co-authoring both the observational synchronization infrastructure and the theoretical models that explain the events embedded in it.
nodes.w_text: Rasmussen's current programme, reconstructed from abstracts, ties together ice-core chronology and dynamical interpretation of abrupt climate change. In 2025-2026 he co-authored a bipolar volcanic synchronisation of the entire last glacial period with Svensson, Dahl-Jensen, and Steffensen, providing a common timescale between Greenland and Antarctica. In parallel he is extending a two-equation Stommel-derived dynamical-systems model with an internal feedback on Antarctic Bottom Water formation that, for the first time, reproduces the full distribution of Dansgaard-Oeschger event durations and their short precursors. A third thread uses four new ISM/EASM speleothem delta-18-O records plus isotope-enabled climate modelling to show that short-interstadial divergences in East Asian monsoon isotopes arise from northward shifts of the Northern Westerlies and expansion of the Western Pacific Subtropical High.

nodes.name: Anders Svensson
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Anders Svensson works on polar ice core analysis with a particular focus on developing and applying advanced imaging techniques to characterize polycrystalline ice microstructures. He has pioneered the use of three-dimensional laboratory X-ray microscopy—combining absorption and diffraction contrast tomography—to simultaneously resolve ice grain geometries, crystallographic orientations (both c- and a-axes), and air bubble distributions in a non-destructive manner, recovering volumetric and crystallographic context that is lost during traditional sample preparations. His methodological contributions include designing a specialized cooling device for the laboratory X-ray system that extends observable sample volumes by several orders of magnitude beyond previous synchrotron-based measurements, enabling more representative textural statistics. He critically evaluates how 3D versus 2D data representations differ and examines how access to both c- and a-axis grain directions may help constrain micromechanical models of ice flow and large-scale ice sheet evolution. In parallel, Svensson uses ice core sulfate records as chronological constraints on the timing of past major volcanic eruptions, incorporating this realistic volcanic forcing into large-ensemble glacial-era coupled atmosphere-ocean model simulations. Through these simulations, he investigates whether very large equatorial eruptions can induce abrupt changes in the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation via atmospheric and ocean circulation changes and air-sea buoyancy fluxes, potentially pushing the climate system between persistent warm and cold states lasting millennia. His work explicitly addresses tipping-point dynamics, examining how unforced natural climate variability exerts a stabilizing influence on the climate system's response to volcanic perturbations in the decades following an eruption, particularly as the system nears a tipping point.
nodes.w_text: Anders Svensson is pushing into deeper sections of polar ice cores using a newly built lab-based diffraction contrast tomography pipeline—complete with a custom cooling device—that maps 3D crystallographic grain orientations (both c- and a-axes) and air-bubble distributions at volumes far exceeding previous synchrotron measurements. He is chasing the question of how much 2D sample preparation distorts microstructural statistics, and whether full 3D orientation data meaningfully tightens micromechanical models of ice deformation. In parallel, he is probing how large equatorial volcanic eruptions interact with unforced natural variability near AMOC tipping points, using large coupled atmosphere-ocean ensembles constrained by ice core sulfate records to determine whether single eruptions can lock the climate into millennial-scale cold states.

nodes.name: Bo Møllesøe Vinther
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Bo Møllesøe Vinther is a paleoclimatologist in the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section who works on reconstructing past temperatures from Greenland and Antarctic ice cores. His signature contribution is extracting climate signals from the spectral fingerprint of water-isotope diffusion in firn: because molecular diffusion smears δ¹⁸O and δD profiles at a rate that depends on temperature, the surviving high-frequency structure of the isotopic record can be read as a paleothermometer. Vinther has authored benchmark analyses showing that single-isotope diffusion lengths reconstruct Holocene surface temperatures to better than 1 °C on thirteen Greenland and Antarctic records. He is best known in the community for NorthGRIP reconstructions covering the last 16,000 years, where his group's method resolves the Bølling–Allerød, Younger Dryas and a Holocene climatic optimum at 7–9 ky b2k, and uses the isotopic record to correct the assumed ice-flow thinning function by roughly 10% at 8 ky b2k. Methodologically his work couples diffusion models to Herron–Langway densification and Dansgaard–Johnsen ice-flow chronologies (GICC05), bridging glaciology, ice-sheet dynamics, and isotope geochemistry. He collaborates closely with the Centre for Ice and Climate at the Niels Bohr Institute, and his papers repeatedly confront the weakness of the classical δ¹⁸O "temporal slope" thermometer in favour of diffusion-based and dual-isotope techniques.
nodes.w_text: Vinther's arXiv trail, combined with his NBI Climate and Geophysics affiliation, points to ongoing work that continues the NorthGRIP-style diffusion-thermometry programme: extracting temperature signals from the spectral smoothing of δ¹⁸O and δD in Greenland and Antarctic ice cores, refining densification and ice-flow models, and benchmarking reconstruction techniques against new high-resolution Holocene datasets. His role as senior author with the NBI Centre for Ice and Climate suggests he is steering a team drilling and measuring new cores, reconciling isotope-based temperatures with δ¹⁵N and δ⁴⁰Ar gas thermometry, and probing the variability of the temporal isotopic slope across glacial–interglacial regimes. Because the arXiv filter was author-only, this inference is indirect; his most recent peer-reviewed publications may reach further, into East Greenland ice-stream dynamics or EGRIP-era records, than these arXiv traces show.

nodes.name: Morten Kjaergaard
nodes.title: Associate Professor - Promotion Programme
nodes.p_text: Morten Kjaergaard works primarily on superconducting transmon and gatemon qubits, with particular focus on identifying and quantifying dissipation mechanisms that limit coherence times in hybrid superconductor-semiconductor Josephson junction devices. He employs co-fabrication strategies that pair gatemons with SIS-junction transmons on nominally identical circuit layouts—sharing gate dielectrics and control lines so that the Josephson element is the only intentional distinction—to isolate junction-intrinsic loss, finding that transmons reach relaxation times in the tens of microseconds while gatemons saturate in the few-microsecond range even after accounting for Purcell decay, control-line emission, and dielectric loss. He investigates higher Josephson harmonics in tunable double-junction circuits consisting of a tunnel junction in series with a SQUID loop, resolving a second harmonic with amplitude up to ~10% of the fundamental through spectroscopy of the first four qubit transitions, and applies a Born-Oppenheimer approximation to derive an effective single-mode qubit model containing a correction term from the internal mode while analyzing how eliminating the internal degree of freedom affects periodic boundary conditions and charge-noise sensitivity. He pursues Fourier engineering of the Josephson energy-phase relation by combining two aluminum-oxide tunnel junctions in parallel with a gate-tunable InAs/Al nanowire junction in a SQUID geometry, demonstrating suppression of odd harmonics by up to two orders of magnitude relative to even harmonics at half flux quantum to produce a double-well potential dominated by cos(2φ) with minima near ±π/2. On the experimental control side, he develops on-FPGA workflows that co-locate pulse generation, data acquisition, analysis, and feed-forward to achieve millisecond-scale calibration—estimating T1 in 10 ms and performing Clifford randomized benchmarking in 107 ms—and employs real-time Bayesian estimation on an FPGA to track relaxation-time fluctuations on timescales of tens of milliseconds, identifying two-level system switching events at rates up to 10 Hz. He applies shadow Lindblad tomography to a five-qubit superconducting transmon processor, recovering all single-qubit dissipation and two-qubit coupling parameters in 9 hours compared to an estimated 58 hours for extensible Lindblad tomography, under experimentally verified locality assumptions. He also works on Andreev spin qubits in InAs nanowire weak links fabricated by in situ shadow evaporation, designing microwave readout circuits tuned to the spin-dependent inductance of the weak link and implementing gap-engineering strategies to mitigate quasiparticle poisoning while characterizing spin relaxation and dephasing over the full range of magnetic flux and gate voltage at odd-parity bias points.
nodes.w_text: Morten Kjaergaard is chasing the microscopic origin of junction-intrinsic dissipation in superconductor–semiconductor–superconductor (S–Sm–S) gatemons, where T1 saturates in the few-microsecond range despite standard loss channels being ruled out by more than an order of magnitude. To isolate this, he built a co-integrated gatemon–transmon chip with an on-chip loss budget framework, and plans to engineer cleaner S–Sm interfaces and test larger-gap superconductors. In parallel, he developed an effective single-mode Born–Oppenheimer Hamiltonian for double-junction transmons—reducing spectral errors by two orders of magnitude—and will extend it to flux-tunable and multi-mode regimes. He also built Shadow Lindblad Tomography, experimentally confirming logarithmic resource scaling, and now targets time-dependent gates and leakage diagnostics within that framework.

nodes.name: Heloisa Nunes Bordallo
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Heloisa Nunes Bordallo is a condensed-matter physicist at NBI who uses neutron scattering - especially inelastic and quasi-elastic neutron spectroscopy - together with X-ray diffraction, thermal analysis, and DFT vibrational calculations to map how small molecules move and bind inside functional matrices. Her distinctive niche is applying these hard-condensed-matter tools to problems from pharmaceutical and biomedical materials: drug-delivery carriers, dental glass ionomer cements, and bio-nanocomposites. A recurring theme is that the efficacy and safety of encapsulated drugs is set not by chemistry alone but by the dynamics and mobility of particular functional groups once the drug is confined, and that neutron scattering is uniquely able to see hydrogen motion at the relevant timescales. She has used pH-controlled intercalation of ciprofloxacin in synthetic smectite clay (fluorohectorite) to show that drug loading jumps from half to full unit-cell occupancy as pH drops, tied to water expulsion from the interlayer. She has paired neutron scattering with DFT to identify which vibrational modes of local anesthetics (bupivacaine, ropivacaine) are most perturbed by cyclodextrin complexation. In a third line she has demonstrated that biocompatible encapsulation of anti-cancer drugs restricts mobility of active functional groups in a way that appears to spare healthy cells. In dental materials, she used neutrons and X-rays to connect proton mobility and pore structure to the mechanical weakness of glass ionomer cements. Across projects her signature is turning scattering measurements into molecular-level arguments about encapsulation, release, and biomedical performance.
nodes.w_text: Bordallo's current arc, read across her most recent papers, is to turn neutron and X-ray scattering into a molecular-dynamics probe of biomedical materials. She has used inelastic neutron scattering, XRD and TGA/FTIR to show that pH controls ciprofloxacin intercalation in fluorohectorite clay through interlayer-water expulsion, doubling drug loading at acidic pH. She has paired neutron spectroscopy with DFT vibrational calculations to identify which piperidine and methyl modes of bupivacaine and ropivacaine are most perturbed by cyclodextrin complexation. In a cancer-drug project she uses X-rays and neutrons on a bio-nanocomposite carrier to argue that partial mobility restriction of specific active groups is what spares healthy cells. In parallel she has connected proton mobility and porosity in dental glass ionomer cements to their mechanical weakness. The unifying drive is to let hydrogen-sensitive scattering reveal why and how confinement changes drug efficacy.

nodes.name: Saulius Vaitiekenas
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Saulius Vaitiekenas works primarily on semiconductor-superconductor hybrid nanowire and nanostructure devices, combining epitaxial Al and NbN superconductor shells with spin-orbit-coupled InAs semiconductors and EuS ferromagnetic insulators to engineer proximity-induced superconductivity and exotic pairing symmetries. Tunneling spectroscopy of full-shell hybrid nanowires reveals Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon analogs appearing as one-dimensional van Hove singularities with energy spacings comparable to the superconducting gap and independent of Fermi energy, evolving with axial field within the Little-Parks lobe structure. Transport studies of InAs nanowires with overlapping epitaxial EuS and Al shells show a three-peak tunneling spectrum attributed to exchange-split superconductors, where spin mixing driven by spin-orbit coupling is essential to reproducing the observed features. He designs and measures Josephson junction arrays on square, dice, and star (3,12²) lattice geometries using hybrid semiconductor-superconductor weak links, exploiting double-layer electrostatic gate geometries to independently tune inter-island coupling and proximity-induced pairing and thereby access superconductor-insulator, superconductor-metal, and anomalous-metal transitions in a single device. In dice-lattice arrays surrounding rhombic plaquettes of proximitized semiconductor, switching current patterns under applied flux distinguish frustrated phase-only behavior in depleted plaquettes from a decreasing envelope with increasing frustration in occupied plaquettes. Control of the current-phase relation is demonstrated through the hybrid Josephson rhombus, where balanced Josephson couplings at full magnetic frustration produce a cos(2φ) potential indicative of coherent charge-4e transport, and tuning away from balance yields a Josephson diode effect with efficiency exceeding 25%. In field-free single-junction devices, gate-controlled nonreciprocal supercurrent persisting in a remanent magnetization state established by a controlled demagnetization procedure probes intrinsically broken inversion and time-reversal symmetries. Beyond nanowires, he engages with few-layer NbSe₂ nanowires fully encapsulated in hexagonal boron nitride as superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors achieving single-photon sensitivity over a 650–1550 nm spectral range, and with locally thinned NbN films exploiting the thickness-driven superconductor-insulator transition to fabricate planar transmon qubits with measured anharmonicity α/2π = 235 MHz without conventional AlOₓ tunnel junctions. Scanning SQUID microscopy serves as a real-space imaging tool to directly measure long-range phase coherence and periodic phase patterns at commensurate magnetic fillings in hybrid Josephson junction arrays, revealing fragmentation into large regions of constant superconducting phase at fields smaller than one percent of a flux quantum per unit cell.
nodes.w_text: Saulius Vaitiekenas is pushing semiconductor-ferromagnetic insulator-superconductor nanowire heterostructures toward topological quantum computing. He recently built hybrid epitaxial InAs/EuS/Al nanowires—achieving complex structural matching across wurtzite/rock-salt and rock-salt/FCC interfaces—and used tunnel spectroscopy to watch Andreev bound states coalesce directly into Majorana bound states in single devices. He also developed a buffer-layer growth platform that markedly improves crystal quality and field-effect tunability in InAs nanowire networks. Next, he aims to better control the ABS-to-MBS crossover using quantum-dot-coupled geometries, characterize exchange and superconducting gaps more precisely via tunnel spectroscopy, extend the buffer platform to superconductor hybrids and complex network geometries, and exploit EuS single-domain magnetic anisotropy for topological qubit integration.

nodes.name: Gabriel Brammer
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Gabriel Brammer is an observational extragalactic astrophysicist at the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) whose recent work is tightly bound to JWST NIRCam, NIRSpec, MIRI imaging and grism/IFU spectroscopy. He is a lead architect of key JWST data-reduction and archival infrastructure: grizli (grism redshift fitting), msaexp (MSA extraction), and the DAWN JWST Archive (DJA), which provide community-facing panchromatic photometric and spectroscopic catalogues behind surveys such as JADES, CEERS, COSMOS-Web, UNCOVER/MegaScience, PANORAMIC, RUBIES, CANUCS/Technicolor, FRESCO, NGDEEP and GLIMPSE. His recent arXiv output centers on three threads. First, the nature of "Little Red Dots" (LRDs): he co-signs multiple spectral studies arguing for a dense-gas cocoon / "black hole star" envelope, with new evidence of Paschen jumps that point to nebular free-bound recombination continua at Te ≲ 10,000 K rather than thermalised blackbodies. Second, dust geometry at z = 2.7–7 via Balmer-decrement vs Prospector SED reddening on 283 JADES galaxies, showing that nebular-stellar differential reddening ΔE(B–V) shrinks above z ≈ 5 and that metallicity correlates with nebular reddening but not stellar reddening. Third, early BH–galaxy co-evolution with GNz7q, a super-Eddington (λEdd = 2.7 ± 0.4) red quasar with log MBH/M⊙ = 7.55 and log M∗/M⊙ = 10.5 at z = 7.19, positioned on the local MBH–M∗ relation and argued to be a direct pathway to z ~ 6 SDSS quasars. Additional recurring threads include quiescent-galaxy discovery (RUBIES quiescent galaxy at z = 7.3), ultra-luminous galaxies at z = 14.44, and the Infinity Galaxy direct-collapse SMBH candidate.
nodes.w_text: Brammer's current work is dominated by three linked JWST-driven threads. Infrastructure: he continues to develop and operate grizli, msaexp and the DAWN JWST Archive, providing reductions and catalogues that underpin JADES, CEERS, COSMOS-Web, UNCOVER/MegaScience, PANORAMIC, RUBIES, CANUCS/Technicolor, FRESCO and GLIMPSE. "Little Red Dots": his 2026 papers present evidence for Paschen jumps that favour nebular free-bound continua (Te ≲ 10,000 K) over thermalised blackbodies inside a stratified gas cocoon / "black hole star" envelope. Dust geometry at cosmic noon to reionisation: a JADES study (283 individual + 327 composite spectra) finds Balmer-decrement–M∗ invariance out to z ∼ 7, a metallicity–nebular-reddening link, and a breakdown of the classic two-component attenuation picture above z ≈ 5. Early BH–galaxy co-evolution: GNz7q at z = 7.19 is a super-Eddington red quasar (λEdd = 2.7, log MBH = 7.55, log M∗ = 10.5) positioned as a direct pathway to SDSS z ∼ 6 quasars.

nodes.name: Lise Bech Christensen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Lise Bech Christensen is a galaxy-evolution and observational cosmologist in the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) at NBI. Her scientific portfolio is unusually broad and weaves together three threads. (1) High-redshift galaxy chemistry and the mass-metallicity / fundamental-metallicity relations, using JWST/NIRCam wide-field slitless spectroscopy (ASPIRE and EIGER programs) to place hundreds of galaxies at z = 5-7 on the MZR and to test how overdense / protocluster environments accelerate chemical enrichment. (2) Quasar-absorption-line cosmology and the cosmic HI content — she is co-PI/co-lead of the Qz5 spectroscopic survey (63 bright z ≳ 5 QSOs at R ~ 7000-9000 with VLT/X-SHOOTER and Keck/ESI), which is setting the first high-resolution measurement of ρ_HI at z ~ 5 and re-evaluating lower-resolution DLA-based constraints. Her expertise in Damped Lyman-α absorbers goes back to the DLA host-galaxy and 'H I-selected galaxy' programs. (3) Strong-lensing cosmology as part of the TDCOSMO collaboration, measuring H_0 from time-delay cosmography in lensed quasar systems — most recently the first TDCOSMO analysis of a doubly lensed quasar (HE 1104-1805), a stepping-stone toward 1%-precision H_0 by expanding the sample from rare quads to the more-abundant doubles. Across all of these she works at the intersection of spectroscopy, cosmology, and galaxy baryon-cycle physics, and contributes heavily to gravitationally lensed follow-up science at DAWN (e.g. the Cosmic Grapes / SLSN lensing / A1689-zD1 ISM studies).
nodes.w_text: Lise Christensen is weaving three observational-cosmology threads forward: extending TDCOSMO time-delay H_0 cosmography from doubly-lensed quasars to additional lenses for systematic cross-checks; growing the JWST ASPIRE/EIGER mass–metallicity sample at z = 5–7 beyond the current 604-galaxy catalogue to disentangle environmental from intrinsic drivers; and extending her non-monotonic ρ_HI(z) measurement (peak at z~3) to higher redshift with deep 21cm stacking and damped Lyman-α surveys. She is also integrating lensed-SN observations (SN 2025wny at z=2, the first strongly-lensed superluminous SN) and GRB-host electron-temperature metallicities at z≈4.2 into the joint chemistry-evolution picture.

nodes.name: Georgios Magdis
nodes.title: Affiliate Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Georgios Magdis is an extragalactic observational astrophysicist at the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) who studies the dust, gas and star-formation properties of galaxies from the local Universe out to z > 10 using panchromatic data from Herschel, Spitzer, ALMA, NOEMA, HST and JWST (particularly NIRCam and MIRI imaging in COSMOS-Web, CEERS, and the DAWN JWST Archive). His signature methods include SED fitting from FUV to far-infrared/submm to derive stellar mass, dust mass, specific dust mass sMdust = Mdust/M∗ and star-formation rate, combined with ALMA dust continuum + greybody models (with a fixed dust-to-gas ratio of 92) to infer molecular gas masses, and one-zone chemical-evolution modelling to interpret scaling laws. Recent work establishes that combining DustPedia and the Local Volume Legacy (1011 local galaxies) gives a non-monotonic log10(sMdust)–log10(M∗) trend peaking at log10(M∗/M⊙) = 9.5, interpreted as a competition between initial gas-mass budget, galaxy age, grain photofragmentation and grain growth in clouds. He is co-leader of ECOGAL, an ALMA archival mining project that uniformly reprocesses ALMA data under the JWST/HST CANDELS/3D-HST footprints (COSMOS, GOODS-S, UDS) to produce prior-based and blind continuum catalogues (1288 detections, 622 unique over ~130k NIR-selected galaxies at 0 < z < 15), expanding A³COSMOS and A³GOODS-S into the JWST era and the UDS field. He studies quiescent-galaxy assembly in overdense environments with the Cosmic Vine protocluster at z = 3.44 in CEERS (183 spec-z members, 11 quiescent galaxies, an early red sequence, top-heavy SMF, halo mass log(Mh/M⊙) = 13.2 ± 0.3). Recurring quantities: dust mass, gas fraction, cold dust temperature, stellar-to-halo mass relation, obscured star-formation fraction.
nodes.w_text: Magdis's current work splits across three tightly linked threads. First, local-galaxy dust evolution: a combined DustPedia + Local Volume Legacy sample (1011 galaxies) reveals a non-monotonic sMdust–M∗ trend peaking at log10(M∗/M⊙)=9.5, reproducible only with tuned grain photofragmentation and grain-growth rates in chemical-evolution models. Second, high-redshift ALMA + JWST legacy mining: he is co-PI of ECOGAL, a uniform reprocessing of ALMA archival data across COSMOS, GOODS-S and UDS under the DAWN JWST Archive, producing prior-based and blind catalogues (1288 detections, 622 unique over ~130k sources at 0 < z < 15) targeting cosmic gas/dust mass densities, obscured SF evolution, and optically dark sources. Third, protocluster assembly: the z=3.44 Cosmic Vine analysis finds 11 quiescent members, an early red sequence, a top-heavy SMF, and a core halo mass log(Mh/M⊙)=13.2±0.3, with three subgroups infalling.

nodes.name: Charlotte Mason
nodes.title: Associate Professor - Promotion Programme
nodes.p_text: Charlotte Mason probes reionization geometry and the early-Universe galaxy population using JWST NIRSpec and NIRCam observations of Lyα and rest-UV emission. She leads/PI the SPURS Cycle 4 Large Program that delivers 29-hour G140M R~1000 NIRSpec grating spectra to test the dense-gas-cocoon picture of Little Red Dots, finding in Abell2744-QSO1 (z=7.04) a high-EW Lyα profile incompatible with a uniform N_HI ~ 10^24 cm^-2 envelope and instead favouring clumpy neutral gas plus two newly-confirmed z≈7.04 neighbours that imply a ≳1 pMpc ionized region. With her GO 4287 program she used Lyα transmission T_IGM = 0.26_{-0.14}^{+0.25} among z=8.7 EGS galaxies (including EGSY8p7) to rule out the previously proposed R_b ≳ 2 pMpc bubble, while a 2.4-3.6x overdensity is still consistent with R_b ~ 0.5-1 pMpc. With a GOODS-N MMT/Binospec + JADES/FRESCO sample of 236 z~5-6 galaxies (62 Lyα detections) she builds empirical baseline EW_Lyα and f_esc^Lyα models conditional on M_UV and β, finding a median Lyα FWHM of 245 km/s and 258 km/s velocity offset. She authored the widely used Lyα equivalent-width / neutral-fraction inference framework. She also contributes to the GLIMPSE ultra-deep lensed survey behind Abell S1063 reaching intrinsic depths of 33 AB and finding α_UVLF ~ -2.0 to -2.1 over z~9-13 and a (1+z)^{-2.94} SFRD slope, to BEACON pure-parallel NIRCam searches for z=7-14 candidates and z=1.5-5 overdensities, to JWST/NIRCam WFSS showing galaxy underdensities at z~5.7 traced by [OIII] emitters lie behind the most transmissive Lyα-forest sightlines, and to radiative-transfer modelling (resonance, Raman, Thomson scattering) of LRD hydrogen line formation.
nodes.w_text: Mason's current frontier is using JWST Lyα observations to simultaneously stress-test dense-gas models of Little Red Dots and map the patchy topology of cosmic reionization. Her SPURS 29-hour G140M spectra of Abell2744-QSO1 at z=7.04 reveal a broad Lyα whose profile matches Hα — impossible for a uniform N_HI~10^24 cm^-2 cocoon — forcing a clumpy neutral-envelope geometry; three Lyα emitters within 0.7 pMpc then imply a ≳1 pMpc ionized region carved around the LRD. Her GO-4287 observations in EGS push T_IGM = 0.26_{-0.14}^{+0.25} at z=8.7, ruling out the previously proposed R_b ≳ 2 pMpc EGSY8p7 bubble while leaving room for R_b ~ 0.5-1 pMpc. In parallel she uses NIRCam WFSS [OIII]-emitter maps toward the most transmissive z~5.7 QSO sightlines to deliver the first 2D transmission-galaxy cross-correlation, revealing ~2.5σ off-axis transmission enhancement at ~0.8x the mean free path — direct evidence for anisotropic ionized structures along large-scale filaments, challenging isotropic-bubble models.

nodes.name: Pascal Oesch
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Pascal Oesch is a cosmic-dawn observational astronomer at the Niels Bohr Institute's Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) and a leading PI of major JWST surveys targeting the first billion years of the universe. He leads or co-leads the FRESCO (First Reionization Epoch Spectroscopically Complete Observations) NIRCam grism survey, the PANORAMIC pure-parallel wide-area NIRCam imaging survey (~1000 arcmin²), and contributes to RUBIES, AURORA, UNCOVER, GLIMPSE and the DAWN JWST Archive. His intellectual fingerprint is combining NIRCam slitless grism spectroscopy with photometric selection to produce luminosity-limited, spectroscopically-complete samples of Hα and [OIII] emitters across z~3-10, enabling measurements of the UV, Hα, and Paschen-α luminosity functions, stellar-mass functions, cosmic variance at z~10, and the spatial distribution of star formation. A recent characteristic result is showing that Paschen emission at z~2 is preferentially concentrated in the outskirts of galaxies (inside-out star formation) and that non-unity dust covering fractions bias Balmer-decrement SFRs low relative to Paschen-based tracers. He has been central to the JWST "Little Red Dot" (LRD) phenomenology: compact broad-Balmer-line sources at z~3-9 that are X-ray and infrared weak, host strong Balmer breaks, and whose spectra—his recent co-authored work shows—are driven primarily by radiative-transfer effects in dense, partially-ionized gas envelopes rather than by virial motion, implying much lower black-hole masses than standard calibrations would suggest. Other signature contributions include the spectroscopic confirmation of z=14.4 luminous galaxies, NIRCam-imaging-based stellar-mass build-up measurements at z=4-9, and the discovery of an ultra-massive grand-design spiral at z~5.2.
nodes.w_text: Oesch's current arc has three tightly coupled fronts. First, JWST Little Red Dots: his team's March 2026 work on PAN-BH*-1 (z=1.73) demonstrates that extreme-Balmer-break LRDs exist at cosmic noon, and the "Engine and its Flows" paper on 18 broad-Hα sources argues their Balmer line profiles are shaped primarily by dense gas-envelope radiative transfer rather than virial motion, deflating inferred BH masses by orders of magnitude and motivating Euclid/Roman follow-up of low-z analogs. Second, PANORAMIC: pure-parallel NIRCam imaging over ~1000 arcmin² is now constraining massive-quiescent-galaxy number densities at cosmic dawn, z~10 cosmic variance, and the bright end of the UV luminosity function at z~9-17. Third, FRESCO + AURORA Paschen/Balmer diagnostics at z~1-3 are spatially resolving inside-out star formation and exposing how dust covering fractions bias Balmer-based SFRs. Next steps include systematic LRD searches at cosmic noon and pushing PANORAMIC-II clustering measurements into the z~10 regime.

nodes.name: Radoslaw Jan Wojtak
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Radoslaw Jan Wojtak specializes in resolving the Hubble tension through systematic reanalysis of Type Ia supernova standardization, with particular focus on host-galaxy dust extinction as the primary driver of bias in local H₀ measurements. He develops and applies Bayesian hierarchical models—specifically two-population frameworks that partition supernovae by the bimodal distribution of the SALT2 stretch parameter—to simultaneously constrain intrinsic supernova properties and extrinsic dust reddening parameters, finding that population differences are primarily driven by mean absolute magnitudes and total-to-selective extinction coefficients R_B. A recurring finding across his work is that applying a Milky Way-like extinction law with R_B ≈ 4 in Cepheid-calibration galaxies, rather than the lower R_B ≈ 3 adopted in SH0ES, systematically lowers inferred H₀ to 70.5–71.45 km/s/Mpc, reducing the Hubble tension by at least 30 and up to 50 per cent. He critically examines whether the commonly assumed exponential exp(-E(B-V)/τ) reddening prior is universal across host-galaxy morphological types, demonstrating through dust distribution simulations differentiated by stellar-to-dust mass ratios that reddening in early-type hosts is substantially lower than in late-type hosts, and that a universal prior introduces spurious scatter in derived extinction properties and biases relative distances between supernovae from different host-galaxy populations. He has extended H₀ inference beyond supernovae to kilonovae, applying the Expanding Photosphere Method to AT2017gfo VLT/X-shooter spectra to obtain a luminosity distance of 44.5 ± 0.8 Mpc and H₀ = 67.0 ± 3.6 km/s/Mpc, and forecasting that five to ten kilonovae from LIGO O4/O5 runs would yield 1% precision H₀ constraints. He also investigates strongly lensed Type Ia supernovae as an independent H₀ probe, combining an ensemble of five convolutional neural networks with a simulation-based inference framework trained on Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope mock image time-series to achieve a 4.4% H₀ estimate from 100 simulated lensed systems. His methodological contributions include developing the cosmology-independent relative intrinsic scatter parameter σ_Rel within a principled Bayesian framework for analyzing supernova siblings, applied to the NGC 3147 triplet of SNe 2021hpr, 1997bq, and 2008fv using a newly trained BayeSN light-curve model.
nodes.w_text: Radoslaw Wojtak is pursuing two intertwined problems: whether dust-reddening systematics or flawed statistical inference—or both—drive the Hubble tension. He recently built a simulation framework using Herschel-derived dust masses and DustPedia scaling relations to compute morphology-dependent reddening distributions, finding that early-type SN Ia hosts show unexpectedly red colour tails and that a universal exponential reddening prior biases H0 upward. He also constructed a resampling-based cosmological inference tool resilient to assumed measurement uncertainties, revealing that joint fits to inconsistent datasets underestimate rather than inflate errors, and that MCMC boundary choices and nuisance parameters spuriously shift Ωm and ΩΛ. Next, he will apply morphology-separated reddening priors, expand the ~6-object blue-SN Ia calibrator sample with LSST data, and test whether restricting analyses to one host-galaxy class eliminates cross-type dust bias.

nodes.name: Markus Tobias Ahlers
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Markus Tobias Ahlers works at the intersection of high-energy neutrino astrophysics and fundamental physics, with a particular focus on constraining extragalactic neutrino source populations—detected by IceCube as a diffuse 10 TeV–10 PeV flux—through general neutrino luminosity function analyses and combined weighted stacking and non-Poissonian fluctuation searches that improve discovery potential by a factor of 2–3 relative to brightest-source searches alone. He has derived analytical, unitarity-based bounds on the flavor composition of TeV–PeV astrophysical neutrinos that extend to active-sterile mixing scenarios without requiring sampling over unknown active-sterile mixing parameters. His work on gamma-ray bursts includes modeling high-energy neutrino fluence from internal shocks in structured, off-axis relativistic jets—providing improved scaling relations for off-axis fluences relative to on-axis predictions and examining angular dependence for GRB 170817A—and he contributed to the IceCube search for neutrinos from GRB 221009A across the MeV-to-PeV range, placing upper limits on hadronic emission from the brightest GRB observed by Fermi-GBM to date. He applies Leggett-Garg Inequality formalisms to neutrino flavor oscillation data, critically reexamining the macrorealistic background models used in statistical hypothesis tests and deriving revised evidence for LGI violations at the 2–3σ level using MINOS muon-neutrino survival data. In cosmic ray physics, he developed a maximum-likelihood anisotropy reconstruction method to suppress detector-induced spurious signals, applying it to approximately 23.7 million KASCADE-Grande events above 1 PeV and identifying a 4σ medium-scale feature at a median energy of 33 PeV with an angular diameter of 40 degrees, and separately derived an analytical quasi-linear perturbative computation of the angular power spectrum of small-scale cosmic ray arrival-direction anisotropies arising from streaming in a particular realization of the local turbulent magnetic field. Across his work he recurrently employs IceCube, KASCADE-Grande, and MINOS datasets as primary experimental inputs, and he contributes to multimessenger science strategy through community white papers connecting IceCube's diffuse astrophysical neutrino flux discovery with gravitational wave and photon observatories including LIGO.
nodes.w_text: Markus Tobias Ahlers is pursuing two intertwined frontiers: testing quantum foundations with neutrino oscillations and identifying the extragalactic sources of IceCube's 10 TeV–10 PeV diffuse flux. He has built a generalized Leggett-Garg string formalism with an RMS z-score statistic and improved pseudo-data simulations, which deflated the MINOS 6.2σ violation claim to 2–3σ, and he will now re-examine NOvA, Daya Bay, RENO, and KamLAND data with the corrected methodology. In parallel, he developed a luminosity-function framework and k-th-brightest-source flux distributions to constrain source populations, finding that weighted stacking improves luminosity limits by an order of magnitude, and plans to extend this to γ-ray bursts, tidal disruption events, and flaring AGN while also mapping flavor-composition boundaries under 3+1 active-sterile mixing.

nodes.name: Jørgen Beck Hansen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Jørgen Beck Hansen is Associate Professor in the Experimental Particle Physics group at NBI and a long-standing member of ATLAS at CERN, with a research career that reaches back through ALEPH at LEP. His individual fingerprint within ATLAS is di-boson physics: precision measurements of gauge-boson self-interactions interpreted through effective Lagrangians and anomalous triple- and quartic-gauge couplings, used as probes of new physics beyond the Standard Model including Higgsless and gravity-inspired scenarios. That analysis line is rooted in his earlier service as Convener of the Gauge-Boson Coupling group and subsequently the Triple Gauge-Boson Coupling group in ALEPH, and his 1999–2004 service as Convener of the ATLAS e/gamma performance groups (electron and photon reconstruction, identification, and calibration). More recently he has contributed to ATLAS b-jet trigger work for the Run-3 2022–2023 data-taking period, documented in the JINST b-jet trigger configuration and commissioning paper. At NBI he supervises PhD and MSc students on ATLAS di-boson and anomalous-coupling analyses and contributes to experimental-particle-physics teaching.
nodes.w_text: Beck Hansen's current individually attributable threads within ATLAS centre on di-boson physics at Run-3 energies: precision measurements of W±W±, W±Z, and related multi-boson final states interpreted via effective-field-theory anomalous triple- and quartic-gauge couplings, as tests of Higgsless and gravity-inspired new physics. Through 2022–2023 he contributed to commissioning and performance characterisation of the ATLAS b-jet triggers for early Run-3, work published in JINST in 2025. His historical e/gamma convenership (1999–2004) has long rolled off, but object-performance expertise continues to underpin the di-boson analyses his group leads. At NBI he supervises PhD/MSc students on these analysis and trigger-performance threads, while the co-authored ATLAS papers in 2025–2026 (radial-flow, top-Yukawa, flavour-tagging calibration) reflect collaboration output rather than his personal lead.

nodes.name: D. Jason Koskinen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: D. Jason Koskinen is an experimental neutrino physicist in NBI's Experimental Subatomic Physics section, long embedded in the IceCube collaboration where he is a central contributor to the atmospheric- and astrophysical-neutrino oscillation programme, especially through the DeepCore low-energy infill array. His flagship collaboration-internal work includes measuring atmospheric neutrino oscillation parameters (theta_23, delta m_31^2) from muon-neutrino disappearance, searching for and constraining eV- and heavy-neutral-lepton sterile-neutrino mixing, measuring atmospheric tau-neutrino appearance and its DIS cross sections, and measuring the inelasticity distribution of neutrino-nucleon interactions from 80 GeV to many TeV. He is also a repeat contributor on IceCube Upgrade hardware (D-Egg and mDOM optical modules, LED calibration systems, PMT acceptance tests), and on detector-level calibration and reconstruction methods (graph-neural-network and CNN-based event reconstruction, SnowStorm systematics propagation, muon-track reconstruction with stochastic losses, the LeptonInjector/LeptonWeighter generator, pseudodata-based statistical methods for small signals). Separately, Koskinen leads a smaller-author-list phenomenology thread at NBI with Stuttard, Ahlers and PhD students (Kozynets, Hilding-Norkjaer, Ioannou-Nikolaides, Groth) that uses neutrino oscillations as a probe of fundamental physics: Lorentz-invariance violation, CPT tests, non-unitary neutrino mixing, quantum-gravity-induced decoherence, and most recently macrorealism / Leggett-Garg inequality tests via MINOS muon-neutrino survival data. His methodological signature is pairing IceCube/DeepCore-class datasets with careful matter-propagation and systematics modelling (SnowStorm, PDF-driven DIS cross-section uncertainties, sidereal-modulation analyses) to squeeze fundamental-physics limits out of atmospheric neutrino fluxes.
nodes.w_text: Koskinen's current small-team NBI thread, run with Stuttard and PhD students, exploits IceCube / DeepCore atmospheric neutrinos and archival MINOS data as a quantum-foundations laboratory. The freshest paper (2026) shows that anisotropic Lorentz-invariance-violating operators in the Standard Model Extension interfere with the Earth's matter potential to produce resonant oscillation enhancements, a macroscopic breakdown of neutrino-antineutrino symmetry, and a tau-flux boost via the regeneration cycle. In parallel (2025) he and Ahlers' group reanalysed accelerator/reactor LGI claims, revising the macrorealism-violation significance in MINOS data down to 2-3 sigma once the macrorealistic null is modelled properly. An earlier (2024) paper established how to correlate nu_tau vs nu_mu DIS cross-section uncertainties in tau-appearance measurements. Meanwhile he continues as a long-standing IceCube collaboration member on Upgrade hardware, sterile-neutrino searches, and cascade reconstruction.

nodes.name: Troels Christian Petersen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Troels Christian Petersen is an experimental particle physicist at NBI whose recent work centers on machine-learning-driven event reconstruction and statistical methods for neutrino and collider experiments. He is a core contributor to GraphNeT, an open-source PyTorch/PyG/Lightning framework that applies graph neural networks to irregular optical-module hit geometries in IceCube, KM3NeT, ANTARES, Baikal-GVD and P-ONE, replacing slow likelihood reconstructions with GPU inference at real-time (order-millisecond) rates. Earlier work includes a strongly-first-order electroweak phase-transition study in a CP-violating type-II Two-Higgs-Doublet Model (2HDM5), performed as a Bayesian fit that disfavored the hidden-Higgs scenario and required the charged Higgs H± above ~490 GeV to satisfy b→sγ. A related 2013 paper with Damgaard, O'Connell and Tranberg used LHC Higgs data plus the baryogenesis requirement to squeeze the parameter space of a singlet-scalar extension of the Standard Model to a small viable island. He has co-authored the HEP Software Foundation roadmap and the 2026 "AI-native Research Ecosystem for Experimental Particle Physics" community whitepaper targeting HL-LHC, DUNE, EIC, FCC-ee and IceCube-Gen2. A recurring thread across his work is treating reconstruction and inference as end-to-end learnable problems rather than hand-tuned likelihood pipelines. He frames cosmic-neutrino alerts for sub-10-TeV events, previously unfeasible in real time, as a concrete deliverable of the GNN approach.
nodes.w_text: Petersen's current trajectory is AI-native experimental particle physics. GraphNeT has become his group's flagship: a PyTorch/PyG framework that replaces likelihood reconstruction in IceCube, KM3NeT, ANTARES, Baikal-GVD and P-ONE with GNNs delivering millisecond GPU inference, opening real-time cosmic-neutrino alerts below 10 TeV. In parallel he co-signs the 2026 community whitepaper arguing for AI-native pipelines at HL-LHC, DUNE, EIC, FCC-ee, IceCube-Gen2 and a future Muon Collider. An older but active strand links LHC Higgs precision and electroweak baryogenesis: Bayesian fits of the CP-violating 2HDM5 that disfavor a first-order phase transition and the hidden-Higgs scenario. The unifying theme is treating reconstruction and BSM inference as end-to-end statistical-learning problems rather than hand-crafted likelihoods.

nodes.name: Oleg Ruchayskiy
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Oleg Ruchayskiy is a theoretical particle physicist at the Niels Bohr Institute (Experimental Subatomic Physics section) who works at the interface of hep-ph, cosmology, and astrophysics, with a signature focus on the phenomenology of heavy neutral leptons (HNLs), sterile neutrinos, and other feebly interacting particles that extend the Standard Model. Over nearly two decades he has built a near-canonical body of work on the νMSM and minimal type-I seesaw models, covering theoretical sensitivity frameworks (from BBN and cosmological constraints on HNL lifetimes to tree-level unitarity bounds and baryon-asymmetry constraints on the neutrino sector), their imprint on neutrino oscillations, and collider or fixed-target strategies at the LHC, CMS, ATLAS, NA62, SHiP and FASER. His 2025 work pushes two specific fronts: (i) revisiting semileptonic charged lepton flavor violation in the minimal seesaw, showing that τ→ℓρ and τ→ℓπ decays can dominate over τ→3ℓ and τ→ℓγ in parts of parameter space with branching ratios near Belle-II reach (~10⁻¹⁰), while meson and quarkonia cLFV decays are at most ~10⁻¹⁴ and therefore inaccessible; and (ii) demonstrating that existing ATLAS displaced-vertex searches can be reinterpreted to probe HNLs coupling to the tau-neutrino flavor via pp→W→τN with hadronic τ and N→ℓ+ℓ⁻ν, gaining an order of magnitude in sensitivity already with Run 2 data and up to three orders of magnitude at HL-LHC. A separate astrophysics thread uses stacked Fermi/LAT AGN spectra observed through galaxy clusters to search for axion-like-particle-induced step-like spectral suppression at nano-eV masses, with a 2025 follow-up showing that AGN flux-hardness variability can mimic the ALP signal unless flux-binned baseline modelling is used. Older, widely cited work includes the 3.5 keV X-ray line as a sterile-neutrino-dark-matter candidate, chiral magnetohydrodynamics in the early universe, and warm dark matter limits from the Lyman-α forest.
nodes.w_text: Ruchayskiy's current arc spans three interlocking fronts. First, indirect/"tabletop" cLFV: his November 2025 paper revisits τ and meson decays in the minimal type-I seesaw with updated form factors and oscillation data, showing τ→ℓρ/π can dominate over τ→3ℓ and τ→ℓγ and reach ~10⁻¹⁰, near Belle-II sensitivity. Second, LHC displaced-vertex HNL searches: October 2025 work demonstrates that dropping the prompt-lepton requirement opens ATLAS/CMS to ντ-flavor HNLs via pp→W→τN with hadronic τ, improving existing limits by an order of magnitude with 139 fb⁻¹ and up to three orders at HL-LHC, with optimized (m_dv, r_dv) cuts. Third, astrophysical ALP searches using stacked AGN-behind-cluster spectra: a September 2025 follow-up refines his 2025 nano-eV ALP bounds by exposing AGN flux–hardness variability as a false-detection systematic, motivating flux-binned baseline modelling. Next likely moves include extending the cLFV analysis to inverse/double seesaw variants and pushing LRT detector upgrades for tau-flavor HNLs.

nodes.name: You Zhou
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: You Zhou is an experimental heavy-ion physicist in the Niels Bohr Institute's Experimental Subatomic Physics section (ALICE collaboration), whose career has been defined by developing and applying multi-particle azimuthal-correlation techniques to characterise the quark–gluon plasma. He is one of the architects of the "Generic Framework" and its successor "Generic Algorithm" for efficient evaluation of flow cumulants under realistic detector conditions, tools now standard at RHIC and LHC. His more recent work has pivoted toward "imaging-by-smashing": using final-state anisotropic flow v_n and mean-p_T fluctuations in ultra-relativistic heavy-ion and fixed-target collisions to extract the ground-state structure of the colliding nuclei. With his NBI group (Nielsen, Nathanson, Gulbrandsen) he has built a unified recursive algorithm for arbitrary-order multi-particle correlations between azimuthal angle and transverse momentum, identifying the six-particle cumulant C(v2^4, δpT^2) as a uniquely sensitive probe of triaxiality fluctuations and a possible shape phase transition in 129Xe. He has led AMPT-based predictions showing an α-clustering signature of 20Ne and 16O in LHCb SMOG2 fixed-target Pb–Ne and Pb–O collisions, and proposed that calcium-isotope runs at the LHC (48Ca+48Ca vs 40Ca+40Ca) can probe the neutron skin of 48Ca via v3, v4, and mean-pT skewness, potentially helping resolve the PREX–CREX tension. Zhou's signature is bridging traditional low-energy nuclear structure to TeV-scale QGP physics through precision flow observables.
nodes.w_text: Zhou's current programme is a coherent push of "imaging-by-smashing" across LHC species. Near-term, expect experimental results from ALICE and LHCb (SMOG2) on 16O–16O, 20Ne-based fixed-target, and Xe–Xe flow cumulants, exploiting his group's unified multi-particle v_n–pT algorithm. His recent 2025–2026 theory papers lay direct proposals for LHC calcium-isotope runs (48Ca+48Ca vs 40Ca+40Ca) to probe the 48Ca neutron skin, addressing PREX–CREX tension, and continue mapping α-clustering in light nuclei at the energy frontier. He is also driving methodology development: higher-order cumulants such as C(v2^4, δpT^2) targeted at triaxiality fluctuations and nuclear shape phase transitions in 129Xe, transverse-momentum decorrelations of flow vectors, and improved non-flow suppression via sub-event methods. Graduate students (Nielsen, Nathanson, Vitsos, Soranzo) are central to this programme, and collaborations with RHIC/LHC theory colleagues (Jia, Giacalone) are recurring.

nodes.name: Leonardo Midolo
nodes.title: Associate Professor - Promotion Programme
nodes.p_text: Leonardo Midolo is a quantum photonics experimentalist at NBI whose group fabricates and operates integrated single-photon sources and reconfigurable photonic circuits built on III-V (GaAs) and SiN platforms. His distinctive niche is the 'nano-opto-electro-mechanical' toolbox - tiny, voltage-driven moving parts (nanomechanical phase shifters, suspended cantilevers, double-membrane cavities, slot-mode electromechanical waveguides) that tune, route, and switch single photons at cryogenic temperatures while preserving the quantum coherence of embedded self-assembled InGaAs quantum dots. A central thread over recent years is delivering near-transform-limited, blinking-free single-photon emission by embedding QDs in p-i-n diode heterostructures inside GaAs nanobeam waveguides, then heterogeneously integrating those emitters onto low-loss SiN photonic circuits via micro-transfer printing with >94% yield, CMOS-compatible electrical tuning, and >80% GaAs-to-SiN coupling efficiency. A second thread pushes telecom-band (O-band) quantum-coherent photon-emitter interfaces and GaAs-on-insulator platforms that interface natively with silicon photonics. A third thread uses these deterministic single-photon sources for protocol-level experiments: contextuality-based semi-device-independent QKD, photonic fusion of entangled resource states, programmable nonlinear quantum circuits, and waveguide-engineered 'artificial molecules' of two QDs coupled across tens of wavelengths via dispersive dipole-dipole interaction. He also builds curved cantilever out-couplers, shallow-etched gratings, and whispering-gallery-mode resonators as the mechanical infrastructure making all of this work. His signature is combining genuinely fabrication-driven engineering (micro-transfer printing, suspended MEMS) with state-of-the-art quantum-optical coherence in a single, scalable chip.
nodes.w_text: Midolo's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to turn InGaAs quantum-dot single-photon sources into scalable, telecom-compatible, electrically controlled quantum photonic integrated circuits. He has demonstrated micro-transfer printing of p-i-n-embedded GaAs nanobeam emitters onto low-loss SiN at 94.7% yield with CMOS-level Stark tuning and 82.2% GaAs-to-SiN coupling, pushing beyond lossy hybrid fiber-chip architectures. In parallel he has built the first fully quantum-coherent photon-emitter interface at O-band telecom wavelengths, with transform-limited linewidths and bright 41.7 MHz detected emission, opening direct interfacing to silicon photonics and low-loss fibers. Above this hardware he has realized reconfigurable nano-opto-electro-mechanical phase shifters for in-operando emitter-field tuning, programmable nonlinear photonic circuits, and contextuality-based semi-device-independent QKD. The unifying drive is removing photon-loss bottlenecks that block fault-tolerant photonic quantum computing.

nodes.name: Jörg Helge Müller
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Jörg Helge Müller is an experimental quantum optics physicist at the Niels Bohr Institute, working at the QUANTOP / Polzik group where he has been a long-time collaborator. His research spans entangled light–atom hybrid networks, cavity QED with cold strontium, and precision atomic magnetometry. In recent flagship work he helped build a hybrid quantum network in which a tunable EPR light source is coupled to a cesium spin ensemble engineered as a negative- or positive-mass oscillator, producing frequency-dependent quantum-noise reduction over an octave in the acoustic band — aimed squarely at next-generation gravitational-wave detectors, continuous-variable quantum repeaters, and distributed quantum sensing. A parallel strand of his programme develops EXAAQ magnetometry, the first optical cesium D2 high-field magnetometer that operates inside a 7 T clinical MRI scanner using saturated absorption on extreme angular-momentum states, offering ppm-level continuous, non-metallic, fiber-coupled field readout as a rival to NMR probes. On the narrow-line cavity QED side, he contributes to collectively enhanced Ramsey readout on ultracold ⁸⁸Sr via engineered sub- to superradiant transitions, and to proposals and demonstrations of superradiant lasers based on thermal and guided ⁸⁸Sr beams. His co-authorship pattern, often as a senior author alongside Eugene Polzik, Jan W. Thomsen, and Helmut Ritsch, identifies him as a bridge between nanofiber-trapped atom optics, cavity-QED clocks, and medical-imaging-relevant precision magnetometry.
nodes.w_text: Müller's current trajectory sits at the intersection of the Polzik-group hybrid quantum network and the narrow-line Sr cavity-QED experiments he co-leads at NBI. Expect follow-up work pushing the cesium negative-mass spin oscillator into vacuum enclosures to match LIGO/Virgo bandwidths, demonstrations of cascaded spin ensembles engineering tailored quantum-noise spectra, and integration with optomechanical nanoparticle sensors. On the atomic side, ongoing efforts likely deepen the superradiant / sub-to-superradiant strontium programme — continuous superradiant lasing from thermal or guided ⁸⁸Sr beams, and further exploitation of collectively enhanced Ramsey readout for narrow-line optical clocks. In parallel, the EXAAQ optical cesium magnetometer is being matured from a 7 T MRI prototype toward calibrated clinical and accelerator/fusion deployments. A recurring theme is using entanglement and collective atomic effects to push sensors past the standard quantum limit in medically and metrologically relevant regimes.

nodes.name: Stefano Paesani
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Stefano Paesani works primarily on integrated photonic quantum computing, with a recurring focus on encoding, distributing, and processing quantum information in silicon and silica photonic chips. He develops fusion-based quantum computing architectures that combine deterministic photon sources—particularly quantum dots and quantum emitters with spin-photon interfaces—with linear-optical fusion gates and graph-state resource generation, proposing complete blueprints that feature time-bin qubit encoding, reconfigurable entangled-photon sources, and low optical connectivity to reduce optical depth per photon. A central theme is fault-tolerant photonic computation, including the design of architectures tailored to noise models specific to quantum emitters, with explicit fault-tolerance thresholds of 8% for photon loss, 4% for photon distinguishability between emitters, and spin noise thresholds above memory-induced errors for typical spin-photon interfaces. He works on high-dimensional and hybrid qudit encodings, including the generation of hyperentangled states with fidelity 67.3 ± 0.2% and GHZ₄-style states with fidelity 85.2 ± 0.4% using path and transverse electric mode degrees of freedom on reprogrammable integrated chips, and has developed a multimode phase stabilisation algorithm that completes one iteration in two measurement rounds for an arbitrary number of modes, enabling distribution of four-dimensional path-encoded entangled states between chips with verified fidelity of 86% and entanglement entropy of 0.995 ± 0.002. His work on graph-state generation applies dynamic programming to construct a lookup table covering approximately 2.8 × 10⁷ non-isomorphic graph states of up to eight qubits using the minimum number of linear-optics Bell-state measurement fusions from a single quantum emitter. He also engages with quantum error-correcting codes beyond surface codes, including Calderbank-Shor-Steane qLDPC and Bivariate Bicycle codes implemented via photonic fusions, and has developed breadth-first union-find decoder variants adapted to handle both qubit erasures and Pauli errors in photonic systems with erasure-dominated noise. Additionally, he has shown that combining indistinguishable photons in superposition over field modes with a binary phase-shifter array and a linear-optical interferometer yields output photon statistics described by a p-body Hopfield Hamiltonian with p = 2Nph, demonstrating a transition from memory retrieval to a spin-glass phase as stored memory increases.
nodes.w_text: Stefano Paesani is pushing simultaneously on hardware and architecture for photonic quantum computing. He has just built an experimental process for generating multiple colour centre species in silicon-on-insulator, a reprogrammable integrated chip using hybrid path/TE-mode qudit encoding that demonstrated on-chip entanglement distillation with a 9.1% fidelity gain, and a Monte Carlo framework assessing qLDPC codes in fusion-based quantum computing. He is now chasing how coupled colour-centre formation dynamics and nanofabrication steps affect emitter yield and coherence in fully integrated SOI devices, whether higher-dimensional hybrid encoding can reduce chip footprint, and whether Bivariate Bicycle or other qLDPC codes can push loss pseudo-thresholds closer to Toric code performance while delivering superior encoding rates.

nodes.name: Emil Zeuthen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Emil Zeuthen works at the theory-experiment interface of optomechanics and hybrid spin-optomechanical sensing, with a consistent focus on beating the Standard Quantum Limit for force and displacement measurement. Several papers develop and refine the negative-mass spin-oscillator scheme for evading quantum back action in gravitational wave detectors, including the "virtual rigidity" trick that matches effective susceptibilities via homodyne, probe and squeezing phases without modifying core interferometer optics. He co-authored the first experimental entanglement between a millimetre-scale SiN membrane and an atomic spin ensemble, witnessed by an EPR variance of 0.83, and follow-up work that operates the atomic spin oscillator down to 6 kHz in the quantum regime and uses a multicolour EPR source with an 8-cm atomic cell equivalent to a ~5 m filter cavity of finesse 6000. On the theory side he proved a unified sensitivity bound encompassing the Quantum Cramer-Rao and newly named Dissipative Quantum Limits, analysed quantum-mechanics-free subspaces between systems of disparate spectra via two-tone driving, and studied prediction-retrodiction time-non-local measurements for oscillator-field teleportation. Recent proposals include a hybrid SU(1,1) optomechanical interferometer using two mode-matched pulse interactions and simultaneous ponderomotive squeezing of 4.8/4.2 dB at 1.32 and 2.43 MHz from two modes of a soft-clamped 20 nm SiN membrane in a cryogenic Fabry-Perot.
nodes.w_text: Zeuthen's current frontier is engineering hybrid optomechanical/atomic-spin platforms that beat the Standard Quantum Limit in practical force and phase sensing. He proposes a hybrid SU(1,1) interferometer where a single mechanical mode is interrogated by two mode-matched pulses so beam splitters are replaced by two-mode squeezers, giving sub-shot-noise sensitivity without input squeezing. In parallel he co-developed a multi-colour EPR-light source linked to a tunable atomic spin oscillator whose 8-cm cell is equivalent to a 5-10 m filter cavity of finesse ~6000, providing frequency-dependent noise reduction tailored to kHz-MHz detector bands and pointing towards next-generation gravitational wave detectors. Experimentally he contributed the theory for a cryogenic soft-clamped SiN membrane that delivers 4.8 and 4.2 dB of simultaneous ponderomotive squeezing from two mechanical modes at 1.32 MHz and 2.43 MHz, extending squeezed-light generation beyond a single octave.

nodes.name: Emil Bjerrum-Bohr
nodes.title: Associate Professor, Group Leader
nodes.p_text: Emil (N. Emil J.) Bjerrum-Bohr is a theoretical physicist in the Theoretical Particle Physics and Cosmology section at the Niels Bohr Institute, working at the intersection of scattering amplitudes, gravity, and black hole dynamics. His 2026 collaboration with Gang Chen and Chenliang Su introduces a hypersurface-based formulation, called an Extended Polyakov Action, whose rigid geometric constraints yield a solution that reproduces the Kerr black hole three-point amplitude expected from amplitude methods. This places his work in the modern 'amplitudes meet gravity' program, where classical spinning black hole dynamics are recovered from effective worldvolume or worldsheet-like actions rather than solved directly from Einstein's equations. The distinctive methodological choice is building a geometrically constrained hypersurface model rather than an effective field theory expansion, and then asking which black hole solutions it admits. Beyond the Kerr amplitude match, he is exploring generalizations of the extended Polyakov construction, including potential applications to gravitational attraction between spinning black holes, relevant to binary black hole inspiral phenomenology. The research thread situates Copenhagen theory at the overlap of string-inspired methods, amplitude techniques, and general relativity-derived observables targeted ultimately at gravitational-wave era physics.
nodes.w_text: Bjerrum-Bohr's ongoing thread, reconstructed from abstracts, uses an extended Polyakov hypersurface action to recover classical spinning black hole dynamics. The recent result reproduces the Kerr three-point amplitude as a solution under rigid geometric constraints, positioning the construction within the scattering-amplitudes-meets-gravity program. Active directions include generalizing the formalism beyond these constraints and applying it to gravitational attraction between spinning black holes, a target directly relevant for binary inspiral observables. The work bridges amplitude methods, worldvolume-style effective actions, and general-relativistic black hole solutions and is aimed at extending Copenhagen's contributions to gravitational-wave era theory.

nodes.name: Mauricio Bustamante
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Mauricio Bustamante is a high-energy neutrino phenomenologist in NBI's Theoretical Particle Physics and Cosmology section whose career has been built around using TeV-EeV astrophysical neutrinos as a laboratory for fundamental physics. His signature technical tool is the flavour composition (ratio of nu_e : nu_mu : nu_tau) of astrophysical neutrinos as it appears at Earth after oscillations over cosmological baselines: starting from his 2010 work on 'Extreme scenarios of new physics in the UHE astrophysical neutrino flavour ratios' he has used this observable to constrain Lorentz-invariance violation, CPT violation, quantum decoherence, Planck-scale new physics, neutrino decay, renormalization-group running of neutrino mixing from TeV to EeV, and dimension-6 SMEFT operators. He is a central author on flavour-composition forecasts for IceCube-Gen2, KM3NeT, and EeV radio arrays (GRAND, RNO-G, TAMBO, IceCube-Gen2-Radio), and leads the methodology of jointly fitting astrophysical and detector uncertainties. A second long-running thread is neutrino emission from astrophysical sources (gamma-ray bursts, active galactic nuclei, starburst galaxies, tidal disruption events) and the UHECR-neutrino connection, including ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray escape mechanisms and the diffuse TeV-PeV flux. A third, more recent line is flavour-dependent long-range neutrino interactions and their joint probing at DUNE/T2HK and high-energy telescopes. A fourth is direct involvement in the Giant Radio Array for Neutrino Detection (GRAND and GRANDProto300): cosmic-ray and neutrino reconstruction with sparse radio arrays, simulation pipelines (GRANDlib), and TAMBO detector concepts. Methodologically his group (Barenboim, Liu, Bai, Song, Song-Ester) produces roadmap-style forecast papers that combine current IceCube MESE data with future multi-telescope synergy and uncertainty budgets for source modelling and mixing-parameter running.
nodes.w_text: Bustamante's current front line is using astrophysical-neutrino flavour composition to turn the TeV-EeV window into a precision laboratory for high-energy neutrino mixing.
