His two latest papers (Feb and April 2026) show, respectively, that upcoming combined neutrino-telescope measurements can for the first time reach theta_23 and theta_13 at TeV-PeV and that the same flavour-composition measurements, interpreted within dim-6 SMEFT or phenomenologically, bound the renormalization-group running of mixing parameters from sub-TeV up to EeV. In parallel he is a core member of the GRAND collaboration (GRANDProto300, GRAND@Auger, GRANDlib), developing the cosmic-ray and neutrino pipeline for sparse radio arrays and the TAMBO deep-valley tau-neutrino observatory. He continues pushing flavour-based constraints on Lorentz-invariance violation, flavour-dependent long-range neutrino interactions probed jointly by DUNE/T2HK and astrophysical neutrinos, and neutrino decay.

nodes.name: Gang Chen
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Gang Chen studies the classical dynamics of spinning binary black holes via modern scattering-amplitude methods, with a sustained focus on the post-Minkowskian (PM) expansion up to third order. Within the heavy-mass effective field theory formalism, he develops a systematic self-force expansion to extract observables from the relativistic scattering of spinning black holes. Using a single-worldline EFT in Schwarzschild–Tangherlini and curved backgrounds, he computes the gravitational Compton amplitude up to 3PM, exposing and regularising infrared / forward divergences to build a computational bridge to black-hole perturbation theory. He also explores a hypersurface (Polyakov-extended) action for Kerr black-hole dynamics, derives all-order-in-spin leading-order time-domain waveforms in cubic gravity EFTs, and studies systematic integral evaluation for spin-resummed binary dynamics. Adjacent contributions include new-physics projections at CEPC and analysis-reinterpretation/preservation frameworks in HEP.
nodes.w_text: Gang Chen is pushing the post-Minkowskian expansion for spinning binary black holes to 3PM and beyond, combining heavy-mass effective field theory with a systematic self-force expansion. He is establishing a computational bridge between a single-worldline EFT in curved backgrounds and black-hole perturbation theory by exposing and regularising infrared / forward divergences. In parallel he is developing a hypersurface (Polyakov-extended) action for Kerr dynamics, deriving all-order-in-spin time-domain waveforms in cubic-gravity EFTs, and refining systematic integral evaluation for spin-resummed binary observables. Open questions include the resummation structure of the PM expansion at higher orders and the extension of curved-background results to Kerr.

nodes.name: Jose Maria Ezquiaga
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Jose María Ezquiaga is a theoretical cosmologist and gravitational-wave theorist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose work centers on using gravitational-wave (GW) observations to probe dark matter, the nature of gravity itself, and the cosmic expansion. His career fingerprint includes two well-known early-career contributions: ruling out a broad class of dark-energy theories after the GW170817 neutron-star-merger speed-of-gravity test ('Dark Energy after GW170817: dead ends and the road ahead'), and establishing 'spectral sirens' — inferring cosmology from the full mass distribution of compact binaries without requiring electromagnetic counterparts. His recent shift, now anchored at NBI's Center of Gravity, is into GW gravitational lensing: identifying lensed binary-black-hole signals, mapping the wave-optics regime where diffraction reveals dark-matter substructure, and building deep-learning inference infrastructure (DINGO-lensing) to make millions of lensing simulations tractable. He runs a substantial junior-author team and holds ERC/Villum/DNRF funding, positioning NBI as a world hub for GW-lensing theory.
nodes.w_text: Ezquiaga's current research program is dominated by gravitational-wave gravitational lensing, pursued on three coupled fronts. The machine-learning front produced DINGO-lensing, a neural posterior estimator that reduces lensed-GW parameter estimation from weeks to seconds, trained on 5 million waveforms and already applied in a full 200,000-simulation reanalysis of GW231123 (which failed to cross the 4σ threshold). The dark-matter front uses strongly-lensed GWs as a substructure probe: higher-order catastrophes from subhalos break the universal μ_r-ΔT relation of smooth lenses, giving a clean observable signature at 1-10% of CDM-lensed events. The cosmology and fundamental-physics front continues earlier threads on spectral sirens and tests of general relativity with GW propagation. All three strands converge on preparing for the first confident GW lensing detection in the upcoming LVK observing runs.

nodes.name: Troels Harmark
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Troels Harmark is a string theorist / mathematical gravity researcher at NBI whose career has spanned several distinctive programs. His early and most-cited work maps the phase diagram of higher-dimensional black holes and black strings on cylinders and Kaluza-Klein circles, including the Gregory-Laflamme instability landscape, new phases of Kaluza-Klein black holes, and the blackfold effective theory of higher-dimensional horizons. A second long-running thread is the Hagedorn temperature of string theories: matching Hagedorn behaviour across AdS/CFT, computing it at finite coupling via the Quantum Spectral Curve, and (single-author) deriving curvature corrections from a thermal-scalar EOM in AdS and pp-wave backgrounds. A third thread is Spin Matrix Theory, a quantum-mechanical reformulation of the planar AdS/CFT correspondence in near-BPS decoupling limits of N=4 SYM, extended with SU(1,1) and SU(1,2) non-Lorentzian conformal symmetry. A fourth thread is non-relativistic (Newton-Cartan / torsional string Newton-Cartan) string theory: systematic Galilean and Carrollian limits of the worldsheet, gravitational solitons in NRST, and conformal mappings of non-Lorentzian geometries. Most recently, Harmark has opened a strong-gravity-astrophysics line with the Perugia group (Grignani, Orselli, Cocco, Pica, van de Meent) on tidal perturbations of Kerr black holes and hierarchical triple (b-EMRI) systems near supermassive black holes: post-Newtonian precession resonances, electric and magnetic tidal moments, and LISA-observable signatures of the Blandford-Znajek mechanism in modified gravity. The common methodological thread is extracting controlled analytic structure (decoupling limits, effective actions, integrability, metric reconstruction) from otherwise-intractable gravitational or AdS/CFT problems.
nodes.w_text: Harmark's most visible current line is a series of strong-gravity papers with Grignani, Orselli, Cocco, Pica, and van de Meent on hierarchical triple systems and tidal effects for Kerr black holes. Within a year they have moved from Schwarzschild precession resonances with a two-frequency resonance condition, to including 0.5PN magnetic-tidal moments that produce brand-new resonances with q=1 absent at Newtonian order, to full metric reconstruction of a tidally deformed Kerr black hole with explicit spin-dependent ISCO and light-ring shifts. In parallel, he continues the non-relativistic/Newton-Cartan string program (gravitational solitons in NRST, SU(1,2) non-Lorentzian conformal mappings) and the Hagedorn-temperature thread via the thermal scalar EOM with curvature corrections in AdS and pp-wave backgrounds, connecting to the Quantum Spectral Curve.

nodes.name: Alessia Benedetta Platania
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Alessia Platania is a theoretical physicist at NBI's Center of Gravity whose core expertise is asymptotically safe quantum gravity (ASQG) - the program pursuing a non-Gaussian UV fixed point (the 'Reuter fixed point') that would make gravity a predictive, non-perturbatively renormalizable QFT - and its consequences for black hole physics, cosmology, and phenomenology. Her distinctive position is building concrete bridges between the abstract functional renormalization group (FRG) machinery of ASQG and physical regimes where quantum gravity matters: black hole interiors and horizons, inflationary and bouncing cosmologies, stellar interiors, and the 'swampland'/weak-gravity-conjecture effective-field-theory constraints. A flagship recent thread charts the landscape of 'gravitationally localized objects' (GLOBs) - black holes, wormholes, and various naked singularities - allowed in effective actions derived from UV-complete trajectories emanating from the Reuter fixed point, and uses the ASQG prediction for the Weyl-squared Wilson coefficient to disfavor certain classical solutions in an Einstein-Weyl phase diagram. She has also shown that, if the QG scale is at least Planckian, the 'UV sensitivity' of extremal Kerr black holes (divergent horizon tidal forces from higher-curvature corrections) is cured within ASQG via the Goroff-Sagnotti coupling. A second thread extends ASQG to matter fields and beyond-GR vector theories, including a very recent 'Proca fixed point' triplet as a first step toward a UV completion of Generalized Proca dark-energy models. A third thread addresses foundational questions: non-perturbative unitarity, fictitious ghosts, causality, Lorentz invariance, positivity bounds, swampland compatibility, and black-hole singularity resolution. She is a frequent author of community 'Visions in Quantum Gravity' and 'Black Holes Inside and Out' roadmaps. Across her work, her signature is pulling testable landscape constraints out of a non-perturbative QFT of gravity.
nodes.w_text: Platania's current arc, read across her most recent papers, is to turn asymptotically safe quantum gravity into a source of concrete, falsifiable predictions for black-hole physics and beyond. With collaborators she has derived the Weyl-squared Wilson coefficient from the UV-complete Reuter trajectory in the Einstein-Weyl truncation, used it to chart a 'GLOB phase diagram' of black holes, wormholes and naked singularities, and shown that wormholes dominate while attractive Bachian naked singularities are disfavored. She has predicted a positive Goroff-Sagnotti coupling within ASQG, curing the recently discovered UV sensitivity of extremal Kerr horizons provided the QG scale is Planckian. In a 2026 extension she has explored whether Generalized Proca (dark-energy) theories admit a UV fixed point, finding a closely-spaced triplet of non-Gaussian fixed points with one non-tachyonic 'Proca fixed point' as candidate UV completion. She also co-authors foundational conceptual assessments (asymptotic safety vs swampland, Lectures/Visions in Quantum Gravity). The unifying drive is deriving the landscape of allowed EFTs and spacetimes from a single UV-complete quantum theory of gravity.

nodes.name: Ziqi Yan
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Ziqi Yan is a Villum Young Investigator in the Theoretical Particle Physics and Cosmology section and the Center of Gravity (DNRF Center of Excellence), working on non-Lorentzian string/M-theory, non-relativistic gravity, and the worldsheet structure of matrix theories. He is among the principal architects of the modern non-relativistic string theory programme: his body of work has systematized nonrelativistic string theory in background fields, derived stringy Newton-Cartan supergravity equations from beta-function vanishing, studied KLT factorization of nonrelativistic (winding) string amplitudes, set up torsional deformations, dual D-brane actions, open-string T-duality, and the renormalization of supersymmetric Lifshitz sigma models. Distinctively, his recent focus turns non-Lorentzian geometry into a concrete tool for holography: showing that decoupling limits of matrix (gauge) theories on D-branes give rise to non-Lorentzian target-space geometries, that at large N the D-particle backreaction deforms this non-Lorentzian supergravity into the familiar Lorentzian type IIA bulk dual to BFSS, and that ambitwistor worldsheet techniques link non-Lorentzian supergravity dynamics to current-algebra anomalies. In parallel he proposes an M-theoretic UV embedding of axions as brane-position modes of D3/M5-branes, offering a gauge-symmetric alternative to the QCD-like global-U(1) story and a concrete bridge between formal non-Lorentzian geometry and cosmological axion/dark-energy phenomenology. His 2025-2026 papers also extend these ideas to Carroll-like geometry in de Sitter holography.
nodes.w_text: Yan's current research program, supported by the Villum Young Investigator grant and the Center of Gravity, is turning non-Lorentzian geometry into an organising principle for holography and quantum gravity. His most recent paper with Maskalaniec and Zorba shows that BPS decoupling limits on D-branes generate non-Lorentzian target-space supergravities which at moderately large N map holographically onto null-reduced 11D supergravity and deform, at large N via D-particle backreaction, into the familiar Lorentzian type IIA dual of BFSS matrix theory; ambitwistor worldsheet techniques link their dynamics to current-algebra anomalies. In parallel he has introduced a geometric UV model with Niedermann that identifies the axion with a brane-position mode of a 3-brane coupled to a four-form, uplifted to open M2-branes on an M5-brane, providing a gauge-invariant completion relevant to DESI-era dark energy and early-dark-energy models. A third line, with Blair and Obers, extends Carroll geometry into hypothetical de Sitter holography.

nodes.name: Maarten van de Meent
nodes.title: Associate Professor
nodes.p_text: Maarten van de Meent is a theoretical gravitational-wave physicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose defining contribution is the black-hole gravitational self-force calculation for extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs). His single-author and small-group papers from 2013-2019 laid the technical foundation: gravitational self-force for eccentric equatorial orbits around Kerr, analytic solutions for parallel transport along generic bound Kerr geodesics, completion of metric reconstruction, and fast self-forced inspirals. He is one of a handful of people worldwide who can compute gravitational-wave signals to EMRI-required accuracy for LISA. His current trajectory shows a sharp pivot from pure perturbation theory toward building LISA-ready waveform models: he is a central contributor to the SEOBNR family (SEOB-TML, SEOB-TMLE), fusing effective-one-body methods with self-force inputs, and is publishing actively on eccentric mergers, resonances in binary-EMRI systems, and tidal perturbations. His fingerprint is rigorous, analytical, and LISA-facing — he is a bridge from deep mathematical general relativity to space-based GW data analysis.
nodes.w_text: Van de Meent's current work is squarely focused on LISA-ready effective-one-body waveform models built from gravitational self-force inputs. His SEOB-TML framework (test-mass limit, quasi-circular, spin-aligned) introduces a Q-factorized flux prescription and mode-dependent attachment that dramatically reduce dephasing against Teukolsky benchmarks; SEOB-TMLE extends this to equatorial-eccentric plunges into Kerr, fitting merger-ringdown features across spin, eccentricity, and relativistic-anomaly parameter space. A parallel line explores 'resonances in binary extreme-mass-ratio inspirals' — stellar-mass binaries near supermassive black holes acting as gravitational tuning forks exciting the SMBH's quasinormal modes. His group keeps up the deep perturbation-theory pipeline (orbital resonances, tidal perturbations, environmental effects in Kerr) that feeds these waveform models with ingredients competitors cannot easily reproduce.

nodes.name: Johan Georg Mulvad Samsing
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Johan Samsing is a theoretical/computational astrophysicist at NBI (ERC Starting Grant BlackHoleMergs, Villum Fonden grant 29466, Center of Gravity) working on the dynamical formation of compact-object mergers. His program uses post-Newtonian N-body simulations of binary–single and binary–binary scatterings in dense stellar clusters, globular clusters, galactic nuclei, and AGN discs to predict eccentric gravitational-wave sources observable by LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA, LISA, DECIGO, TianQin, Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer. He has argued that highly eccentric mergers are a smoking-gun signature of dynamical formation and recently introduced the "pinhole regime" idea: in the high-eccentricity limit (e10 ≳ 0.05) every dynamical channel converges on the same universal eccentricity distribution, because the astrophysical formation scale is orders of magnitude larger than the scale required for eccentric capture. He models environmental effects (Rømer delays / line-of-sight acceleration, gas dynamical friction, three-body perturbations) that imprint measurable phase shifts on inspiral waveforms, enabling discrimination between isolated, cluster, AGN-disc and triple channels on an event-by-event basis. His group has also worked on strongly lensed GW sources as stereoscopic probes of proper motion and formation environment, spin-orbit misalignments of eccentric mergers in AGN discs, and dark-matter capture amplified by compact binaries (using GW170817 to bound bosonic DM). Tools include N-body scattering codes with fast drag-force prescriptions for tidal and GW energy losses and dephasing prescriptions that go beyond local Taylor expansion to handle curvature- and projection-dependent LOSA.
nodes.w_text: Samsing's current program centers on turning eccentric gravitational waves and environmental dephasing into sharp discriminators between dynamical BBH formation channels. His 2026 Letter establishes a universal "pinhole-regime" eccentricity distribution to which every dynamical channel (binary–single, triples, AGN discs, gas-assisted) converges at LVK frequencies, overturning the commonly used 1/e prior. In parallel, his group shows that moderate eccentricity (e10Hz ∼ 0.2–0.4) boosts detectability of neutron-star g-mode resonances by over an order of magnitude via epicyclic harmonic transport, putting NS nuclear EoS constraints within LVK reach. A third strand introduces a self-consistent Rømer-delay / LOSA model that breaks the mass–distance degeneracy for ET, reanalyses GW190814 (finding no LOSA), and targets a few to tens of ET detections per year.

nodes.name: Mathias Spliid Heltberg
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Mathias Spliid Heltberg works at the intersection of nonlinear dynamics and cell biology, with a particular focus on how oscillatory signaling encodes information for cellular decision-making. His research uses the p53 tumor suppressor as a model oscillator to probe entrainment modes, multi-stability, Arnold tongues, phase response curves, period doubling, and chaos in living human cells subjected to external drug pulses of varying periods and strengths. He combines ODE-based mathematical modeling and sensitivity analysis with live-cell fluorescence imaging and intravital microscopy to show that low Wip1 phosphatase levels—rather than oscillatory p53 pulses—underlie the single broad p53 pulse observed in highly confluent breast cancer cells and in breast tumors in vivo. He investigates how stochastic biochemical noise interacts with oscillatory driving frequencies in excitable media, uncovering a multimodal frequency dependency with local optima in one and two dimensions, and derives thermodynamic quantities such as free-energy dissipation to characterize the relationship between energy expenditure and signaling precision in coupled-cell networks. His work on circadian biology applies coupled-oscillator theory combined with live population and single-cell recordings to demonstrate that loss of extracellular circadian synchronization disrupts circadian–cell-cycle coordination within individual cells and impedes collective tissue growth, connecting clock disruption to oncogenic proliferation. He is also engaged with biomolecular condensates and phase separation as mechanisms of spatial regulation, reviewing nucleation, diffusion, and mechanical feedback as physical principles governing condensate formation at genomic loci involved in DNA repair, transcription, and epigenetic control. Across projects he integrates non-equilibrium thermodynamics as a unifying conceptual lens, connecting temporal heterogeneities and oscillations with spatial heterogeneities and condensates as the two principal modes through which cells execute regulatory decisions out of equilibrium.
nodes.w_text: Mathias Spliid Heltberg is investigating how the temporal dynamics of signaling proteins—particularly p53 oscillations versus single broad pulses—govern cell-fate decisions in cancer. His recent work used intravital imaging in fluorescent-reporter mouse breast tumors to discover that high cell confluency, acting through reduced Wip1 phosphatase levels rather than upstream signaling changes, switches p53 from oscillatory to single-pulse dynamics in vivo. He built a mathematical model linking Wip1 levels to this switch and now aims to determine how each dynamic mode differentially regulates gene expression and fate in cancerous tissue, what additional microenvironmental factors reproduce in vivo heterogeneity, and how biomolecular condensates mechanistically coordinate transcription and DNA repair at specific genomic loci.

nodes.name: Weria Pezeshkian
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Weria Pezeshkian is a computational biophysicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose research centres on multi-scale simulation of biological membranes. His career arc moved from early electrostatics work on counterions and dielectric inhomogeneities at charged interfaces, through membrane curvature and lipid-domain physics, to his current position as a major contributor to the Martini coarse-grained force-field ecosystem (notably the Martini 3 carbohydrate parameterisation done with the Marrink group). His distinctive current contribution is methodological infrastructure that truly bridges nano- and meso-scales: coupling coarse-grained molecular-dynamics to Monte Carlo simulations on triangulated vesicles via renormalisation-group-informed parameter transfer, and then back-mapping to atomistic detail. This forward-and-backward scale bridging positions him as a rare 'workflow physicist' in cell biology — someone building the plumbing that lets whole vesicles, organelles, and eventually cells be simulated with correct physics at every length scale. His work is foundational for exascale-era molecular biology and is closely tied to the structural-biology and glycobiology communities.
nodes.w_text: Pezeshkian's active thread is scale-bridging in membrane biophysics. His lipid-vesicle patterning workflow shows how to extract CG-MD parameters (line tension, spontaneous curvature, bending moduli) and inject them via renormalization-group arguments into a triangulated mesoscale Monte Carlo model of biphasic vesicles — then back-map to CG for atomic-level equilibration at the full vesicle scale. This gave concrete insight into GM1 glycolipid's role in stabilising mesophase-separated domains through combined curvature generation and line-tension reduction. In parallel, his contribution to the Martini 3 carbohydrate force field provides the community with a canonical fragment-decomposition scheme that accurately reproduces osmotic pressures and glycolipid-membrane interactions. The through-line: build and disseminate the methodological infrastructure that makes organelle-, virus-, and cell-scale molecular biology tractable in the exascale era.

nodes.name: Eliza Cook
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Eliza Cook is a tephrochronologist in NBI's Climate and Geophysics section specialising in using volcanic ash (cryptotephra) preserved in ice cores as chronological and forcing markers for past climate. Her recent work identifies a prolonged period of Icelandic volcanism between 751 and 940 CE - the 'Icelandic Active Period' - using cryptotephra analyses, high-resolution sulphur isotopes and glaciochemical tracers in Greenland ice cores; this directly challenges the long-held view that AD 700-1000 was volcanically quiescent, and implicates Iceland in European winter cooling anomalies. In East Antarctica she has produced a Mid-to-Late Holocene tephra framework (15 cryptotephras plus one visible horizon over 5500 years) with sources traced to the South Sandwich Islands, South Shetlands, Victoria Land and South America, refining latitudes of sulphur injection for Southern Hemisphere eruption climate-forcing estimates. Beyond her volcanic-record work, she co-authors ice-sheet mechanics studies using Greenland borehole fibre-optic observations that revealed englacial ice-quake cascades nucleating near volcanism-related impurities - a brittle deformation mode invisible at the surface that may explain the gap between viscous-flow ice-sheet models and geodetic observations. She has also co-developed a deep-neural-network classification framework for insoluble particles (mineral dust, felsic/mafic tephra, pollen, contamination) in continuous-flow ice-core imaging that reaches 96.8% classification accuracy and removes a major labour bottleneck in ice-core particle analysis.
nodes.w_text: Based on publicly available abstracts from KU's Pure profile (no full-text PDFs available in the corpus), Cook's active threads include (1) extending Greenland-ice-core cryptotephra and sulphur isotope records to challenge the 'Medieval Quiet Period', notably defining the Icelandic Active Period 751-940 whose Hrafnkatla episode aligns with European winter cooling; (2) building a Mid-to-Late Holocene tephra framework in interior East Antarctic cores with 15+1 horizons traced to regional sources; (3) collaborating on borehole fibre-optic detection of englacial ice-quake cascades nucleating at volcanism-related impurities as a missing link in ice-sheet mechanics; and (4) co-developing deep-neural-network classification of flow-imaging microscopy data for seven classes of ice-core particles at 96.8% accuracy, unlocking higher-throughput paleoclimate work.

nodes.name: Johannes Jakob Lohmann
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Johannes Lohmann is a climate physicist at the Niels Bohr Institute whose work sits at the unusual intersection of dynamical-systems theory and paleoclimate/tipping-point analysis. His distinguishing agenda is to treat large-scale climate components — chiefly the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and the glacial Dansgaard–Oeschger (DO) oscillations — as high-dimensional nonlinear dynamical systems, and to rigorously test whether early-warning signals (EWS) based on critical slowing down are actually reliable bifurcation predictors. He has spent recent years systematically showing that EWS are observable-dependent, that AMOC tipping is multi-pathway (full vs partial collapse), and that the naive saddle-node normal-form picture often fails. On the methodology side, he pushes intermingledness, edge-state analysis, and operator-theoretic dimension reduction (diffusion maps) as more principled alternatives to variance-and-autocorrelation EWS. Many of his papers are single-authored or small-group, with open-source Julia code tied into DynamicalSystems.jl, giving his fingerprint an unusually quantitative-rigorous flavour within climate science.
nodes.w_text: Lohmann is running a unified program that treats AMOC collapse and Dansgaard-Oeschger events as test cases for the theory and practice of climate tipping points. His active threads include: a conceptual multi-box AMOC model demonstrating that full-vs-partial collapse pathways give misleading EWS unless the right observable is chosen; a direct-CSD test using stacked volcanic-eruption responses in NGRIP ice-core proxies, showing bifurcation precursors only for DO cooling and not DO warming; and an algorithmic workflow for objectively detecting multistability in high-dimensional climate simulations (with a new 'intermingledness' metric). Much of his output is single-authored, ties into DynamicalSystems.jl, and sits in a methodological-rigour niche at the boundary of applied dynamical systems and empirical paleoclimate.

nodes.name: Nicholas Mossor Rathmann
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Nicholas Mossor Rathmann is a climate and geophysics researcher at the Niels Bohr Institute whose accessible arXiv trail is concentrated in fundamental turbulence theory with Peter Ditlevsen (2016-2019), with more recent activity in the NBI Climate and Geophysics section on ice-sheet physics. His turbulence work uses the spectral-helical (Waleffe 1992) decomposition of the Navier-Stokes equation to analyze the eight triadic interactions between helical modes and identify subsets that conserve new invariants beyond energy and helicity. He introduced a shell model derived as a special case of the helically decomposed NSE in which three of four triad-interaction submodels produce dual forward cascades of energy and helicity while the fourth, which conserves both signs of helicity separately (enstrophy-like), drives an inverse energy cascade. A signature theoretical contribution is the discovery of pseudo-invariants—additional partially conserved quantities beyond energy and helicity—that can drive either forward or inverse energy cascades depending on specific triad geometry. He extended this framework to magnetohydrodynamic turbulence, showing that partially conserved enstrophy-like quantities in spectral-helical MHD map onto the triad interactions responsible for large-scale α-dynamo action and the inverse transfer of magnetic helicity, and that the inverse transfer is more local in scale than the α-dynamo itself. His methodological habit is to build minimal shell models (minimum triad interactions) that isolate specific conservation laws and then verify their flux predictions numerically against full NSE/MHD cascades. His affiliation at the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section suggests these cascade and invariant analyses are being applied to geophysical flow problems including ice-sheet and atmospheric turbulence.
nodes.w_text: Rathmann's available arXiv record ends in 2019 with the MHD turbulence paper (Rathmann & Ditlevsen 2019) extending the spectral-helical pseudo-invariant framework from Navier-Stokes to magnetohydrodynamics and connecting it to α-dynamo action and inverse magnetic-helicity transfer. His current affiliation with the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section suggests a pivot from fundamental turbulence theory toward geophysical applications—plausibly ice-sheet anisotropic flow, crystal-orientation-fabric modelling, or atmospheric turbulence—but specifics are not inferable from the available public record, which is dominated by the 2016-2019 Ditlevsen collaboration on triad invariants in 3D HD and MHD turbulence.

nodes.name: Julien Westhoff
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Julien Westhoff is an ice-core glaciologist in the Niels Bohr Institute's Climate and Geophysics section, working within the long-running Danish ice-core drilling programme (EastGRIP and related projects in central Greenland). His research marries field engineering of ice-core drills with microstructural and geophysical analysis of the recovered cores. On the hardware side, he led first field tests of a new Danish replicate drilling system, a mechanism that broaches a guide groove on the uphill wall of an existing borehole and mills a notch on the downhill side so that gravity steers the drill into a new branch producing full-diameter replicate ice cores — a capability important for recovering deep pristine ice alongside the parent hole. On the science side he has shown that the characteristic upright folds seen in cloudy bands at cm scale and in internal reflection horizons at km scale in Greenland ice share a power-law fold power spectrum, implicating ice's strong mechanical anisotropy (lattice preferred orientation) rather than classical Biot buckling as the folding mechanism. He has also contributed to borehole fiber-optic observations revealing englacial ice-quake cascades near volcanism-linked impurities — a brittle, seismic deformation mode that is invisible at the surface but produces strain rates comparable to geodetic measurements, offering a missing link between viscous ice-sheet models and Greenland ice-stream observations. His collaborators include Svensson, Cook, Boeckmann, and Rathmann at NBI's Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth group.
nodes.w_text: Because Westhoff's corpus was built from Pure abstracts only (no arXiv, no PDFs), his ongoing research is inferred indirectly from three very recent 2025 publications. Expect continued work on the EastGRIP and future deep-drilling campaigns, including operational deployment of the Danish replicate drilling system at greater depths; deeper investigation of ice-sheet fold mechanics combining cloudy-band core imaging with km-scale radar stratigraphy; and extension of fiber-optic borehole seismology to map englacial ice-quake cascades across Greenland ice streams. Likely collaborators continue to be Svensson, Cook, Rathmann, and Boeckmann within NBI's Physics of Ice, Climate and Earth group, plus international partners on EastGRIP. The inferred trajectory points toward reconciling brittle and viscous deformation modes in ice-sheet models, better fabric-anisotropy-aware interpretations of radargrams, and new drilling hardware to obtain replicate cores for geochemical and physical cross-checking.

nodes.name: Morten Holm Christensen
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Morten Holm Christensen is a condensed-matter theorist at NBI whose work sits at the intersection of lattice geometry, multi-orbital electron correlations, and unconventional order parameters. His signature move is to take a specific lattice (kagome, Lieb, honeycomb, or the Fe-based square-lattice) and ask: what symmetry-allowed orders do the Bloch wavefunctions, sublattice structure, and van Hove points on this lattice uniquely enable or forbid? This has led him to a program on the vanadium-based kagome metals AV3Sb5, where he has built microscopic models of the charge-density-wave (including exotic 'imaginary' loop-current states with toroidal and octupolar magnetic orders), proposed orbital-selective electron-phonon coupling as a pairing mechanism, and shown that spin-singlet superconductivity on the kagome lattice is anomalously robust to disorder because of sublattice-character Bloch weights. A second strand extends to Fe-based superconductors, where he studies intertwined spin-orbital orders, nematic instabilities from multi-orbital Hubbard-Hund models, and most recently identifies coplanar Fe-based magnets as hosts of 'odd-parity' (h-wave) magnetism. A third strand develops the theory of collective hybridized nemato-elastic modes - how electronic nematic fluctuations hybridize with transverse acoustic phonons - with implications for nematic-mediated pairing. He also does ab initio surveys of kagome-layered shandites (Rh/Pd based) connecting van Hove saddle points to structural instabilities. His collaborators span Copenhagen, Minnesota, and elsewhere; the unifying thread is explaining why and how lattice geometry and orbital character sculpt unconventional order.
nodes.w_text: Christensen's current arc, read across his most recent papers, is to connect lattice geometry and orbital content to the robustness and character of unconventional order. He has argued that kagome and Lieb band structures generically protect spin-singlet unconventional superconductivity from disorder through sublattice-resolved Bloch weights, unlike square or honeycomb lattices. In parallel he identifies Fe-based coplanar magnets as hosts of h-wave 'odd-parity' magnetism, with non-trivial Berry-curvature and anomalous-Hall signatures. A third thread develops a microscopic theory of hybridized nemato-elastic modes - intertwined nematic fluctuations and transverse acoustic phonons near a nematic QCP - with implications for nematic-mediated pairing. He also surveys Rh/Pd kagome-layered shandites via DFT/DFPT, pinpointing when van Hove saddle points can drive structural instabilities. The unifying drive is: which lattices, orbitals and symmetries generate, stabilize or destroy exotic electronic order.

nodes.name: Andreas Haahr Larsen
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Andreas Haahr Larsen is a soft-matter / structural biophysics researcher in the Condensed Matter Physics section at NBI, whose public arXiv author-only trail is dominated by small-angle X-ray and neutron scattering (SAXS/SANS) of biological assemblies together with integrative molecular-simulation-plus-experiment methodology. Visible work (prior to this paper batch) includes Shape2SAS, a web application that simulates SAS data and pair-distance distributions from user-defined shapes; a Bayesian indirect Fourier transformation (BIFT) approach for assessing experimental noise in SAS; a methodology paper on optimal weights and priors for simultaneous fitting of multiple SAS datasets; and reviews on what molecular simulations can reveal about the specific interactions between peripheral membrane proteins and lipid bilayers, plus a more general paper on integrating molecular simulations with experimental (scattering) data when the two disagree. Methodologically, he operates at the interface between Bayesian inference, structural biology, and scattering experiment: extracting low-resolution 3D shape information, membrane-protein conformational ensembles, and lipid-bilayer structure from solution scattering while using molecular-dynamics simulations as priors. Note: the author-only arXiv query used for his paper batch is broadened ('Andreas Larsen'), so the three extracted papers in this ingest (memory safety, satellite scheduling, AIGC 3D content) correspond to other authors of the same common name and do not represent his research; the background_candidates list above is the reliable source of his actual topical footprint.
nodes.w_text: Manual-review flag: Andreas Haahr Larsen's arXiv identifier was not uniquely resolvable, so the three papers auto-extracted for this record belong to other 'Andreas Larsen' authors and are not reliable indicators of his current research. Based on the broader author-only candidate list (the background_candidates index here), his ongoing work continues the SAXS/SANS plus molecular-simulation integration line: Bayesian indirect Fourier transformation for experimental-noise assessment, simultaneous fitting of multiple small-angle-scattering datasets with optimal weights and priors, and the Shape2SAS web tool for forward-simulating SAS data from user-defined shapes. The peripheral-membrane-protein-on-lipids simulation review points to an active biophysics programme on membrane-embedded proteins. A confident ongoing summary requires manual resolution of his arXiv identity.

nodes.name: William Iain Leonard Lawrie
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: William Iain Leonard Lawrie is a Condensed Matter Physics experimentalist at the Niels Bohr Institute working on semiconductor quantum-dot spin qubits in silicon and germanium. His PhD-era output at Delft (Veldhorst group) delivered foundational benchmarks for the germanium hole-spin qubit platform: spin relaxation times T_1 up to 32 ms for single-hole QDs and 1.2 ms for five-hole QDs in a 2x2 Ge/SiGe array, strong individual gate addressability with >20x differential plunger-gate response for multi-hole qubits, and single-qubit randomized-benchmarking fidelities of 99.992% with novel N-copy randomized benchmarking of 99.905% for two-copy and 99.34% for four-copy simultaneous driving, crossing the conventional fault-tolerant threshold for simultaneous gates. His broader contributions span universal six-qubit silicon processors, bichromatic Rabi control, coherent spin-qubit shuttling through germanium quantum dots, programmable Ge quantum simulators exhibiting resonating valence-bond physics, and operation with hopping-spin qubits. Distinctively, his most recent co-authored work pivots the programme toward hybrid superconductor-semiconductor devices in the same Ge/SiGe platform: demonstrating a PtGeSi-proximitized quantum dot (SC-QD-SC junction) with gate-tunable coupling, singlet-doublet control, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov sub-gap physics, and critical out-of-plane field 0.90 T. This positions group-IV germanium as the first material compatible with isotopic purification that supports both long-coherence spin qubits and hard-gapped superconductivity, opening routes to Andreev spin qubits, protected superconducting qubits, and quantum-dot Kitaev chains.
nodes.w_text: Lawrie's current trajectory carries him from the foundational germanium hole-spin qubit work to Copenhagen's emerging hybrid semiconductor-superconductor programme. His recent 2024 co-authored paper with Lakic, Kuemmeth, Chatterjee and the Delft/TU Delft team demonstrates a Ge/SiGe quantum dot proximitised by PtGeSi with gate-tunable SC-QD coupling, singlet-doublet ground-state control, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov sub-gap physics, and a 0.90 T out-of-plane critical field, making group-IV Ge the first isotopically purifiable material to combine long-coherence spin qubits with hard-gap superconductivity. In parallel he continues to push silicon and germanium qubit benchmarks: simultaneous single-qubit gates above the fault-tolerant threshold (99.9%) and coherent spin shuttling in germanium arrays. The ongoing targets are RF-reflectometry parity readout, Andreev spin qubits, and quantum-dot Kitaev chains in isotopically purified Ge.

nodes.name: Joachim Elbeshausen Sestoft
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Joachim Elbeshausen Sestoft is a condensed-matter researcher at the Niels Bohr Institute's Center for Quantum Devices and Nano-science Center whose work spans III/V nanowire materials engineering, scalable nanodevice fabrication, and nano-optoelectronic neuromorphic hardware. His most recent first-author work (2025) demonstrates a nanoscale photonic neuron—an artificial nano-optoelectronic unit with picowatt operating powers and a circuit footprint at least 100× smaller than existing photonic neuromorphic technology—that can deterministically receive both excitatory and inhibitory signals, implement a nonlinear activation function, and exhibit biologically relevant memory timescales and input-channel weighting, all compatible with commercial silicon technology. Earlier, as first author in 2021 he introduced microtomy-plus-VLS-growth as a scalable platform for nanocrystal-based quantum electronics, using an ultramicrotome with a diamond knife to section thin lamellae containing arrays of embedded InAs nanowires, and demonstrated conductance quantization, single-electron charging and wave interference in the resulting cross-sectioned devices with good yield. His thesis-era work contributed to field-effect-enhanced buffered InAs-on-GaAs nanowire networks for Majorana-relevant platforms, epitaxial InAsSb/Al hybrid nanowires designed for stronger topological protection, epitaxial Pb on InAs nanowires, and transparent gatable superconducting shadow junctions—all building blocks for the Center for Quantum Devices' topological superconductor roadmap. His methodological fingerprint is the deterministic, wafer-scale transfer of precisely shaped III/V nanostructures to arbitrary target substrates using either selective-area growth or microtomy, bridging quantum electronics and optoelectronic/neuromorphic applications on the same material platform.
nodes.w_text: Sestoft's current arc, anchored by his September 2025 first-author paper, is the nanoscale photonic neuron—a sub-picowatt nano-optoelectronic device with 100×-reduced footprint that implements excitation, inhibition, nonlinear activation, and biological memory timescales on silicon-compatible III/V nanostructures. This builds directly on his 2021 microtomy+VLS-growth platform for deterministically transferring arrays of cross-sectioned III/V nanocrystals to arbitrary substrates. Next steps visible in the paper outlook are scaling single neurons into larger neuromorphic networks, using the same modular architecture as a compact adaptive-optical-sensing front end, and merging the microtomy platform with hybrid-epitaxy semiconductor-superconductor technology for quantum-device arrays. His trajectory effectively unifies the Center for Quantum Devices' Majorana-relevant nanowire heritage with neuromorphic photonic hardware in a shared fabrication paradigm.

nodes.name: Kasper Elm Heintz
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Kasper Elm Heintz works primarily on the spectroscopic characterisation of high-redshift galaxies and AGN using JWST/NIRSpec Prism and NIRCam grism modes, with a specific focus on modelling rest-frame UV-to-optical continua from free-bound recombination emission and modified blackbodies. A central contribution in his work is the detection of Paschen jumps in a subset of LRDs consistent with free-bound recombination to hydrogen n=3, where minimally reddened emission from low-temperature gas (T_e ≲ 10,000 K) and a tight observed correlation between Hα and the continuum luminosity support a nebular recombination origin for both line and continuum emission. He has co-led systematic population studies of 116 LRDs across 2.3 < z < 9.3 using the DAWN JWST Archive, demonstrating that LRD continuum spectra are ubiquitously well described by modified blackbodies with typical T ~ 5,000 K—directly analogous to stars on the Hayashi track—and that the Balmer decrement increases strongly toward higher Hα and optical continuum luminosities as evidence for luminosity-dependent collisional de-excitation and resonant scattering in dense gas envelopes. He applies damped Lyman-α absorption modelling to JWST/NIRSpec Prism spectra of z > 9 galaxies to constrain neutral HI column densities, showing that derived HI column densities and star-formation rate surface densities place all galaxies on depletion timescales of ~10–100 Myr, greatly in excess of the canonical Kennicutt-Schmidt relation. He uses a cosmological Friends-of-Friends algorithm applied to rest-frame optical JWST/NIRCam grism spectroscopy of the Abell 2744 lensing field (JWST-ALT survey) to identify six galaxy overdensities at z = 5.66–6.77, finding that two of six overdensities show excess HI absorption relative to the field average and that overdensity members are younger and less massive than field galaxies at similar redshifts. His work on chemical enrichment exploits direct-T_e metallicity measurements stacked from ~800 [OIII]-selected NIRCam grism galaxies at 5 < z < 7, finding a flatter mass–metallicity relation slope than predicted by the SPHINX²⁰ and FLARES simulations and little dependence of metallicity variations on star-formation rate at fixed mass, consistent with rapid out-of-equilibrium metal enrichment in low-mass galaxies. He also pursues multi-wavelength follow-up including deep ALMA Band 7 observations that detect a broad [OIII] 88 μm component (FWHM = 1,366 km/s) in UNCOVER-10646 at z = 8.51 indicating an ionised outflow with mass-loading factor η ~ 2
nodes.w_text: Kasper Elm Heintz is using Sirocco 2D radiative-transfer models and a custom Paschen/Brackett continuum fitting pipeline on JWST NIRSpec/PRISM spectra to test whether Little Red Dot continua arise from free-bound recombination rather than dust-reddened AGN emission—finding electron temperatures ≲10,000 K and nebular light fractions exceeding 95%. He is extending wavelength coverage to 5.5 μm to anchor the Brackett continuum at z≤3.5, and probing Paschen-jump shapes across larger LRD samples to quantify thermalisation. In parallel, using a uniform 48-galaxy JWST/NIRSpec Prism sample, he is disentangling local HI damping wings from IGM opacity at z>9, measuring extreme star-formation efficiencies that violate the Kennicutt-Schmidt relation, and chasing a candidate metal-free 'blueberry' companion at z=5.124.

nodes.name: Koki Kakiichi
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Koki Kakiichi studies the end stages of cosmic reionization and the environments of the earliest luminous quasars, using JWST-era slitless spectroscopy and photometric IGM tomography. Within the ASPIRE program he measures the auto-correlation function of [OIII] emitters at 5.3 < z < 7.0 and the quasar–[OIII] emitter cross-correlation at z ≃ 6.6 to constrain host halo masses and duty cycles of luminous quasars. Using JWST NIRCam WFSS in COSMOS-3D (0.3 deg² F444W), he builds a spectroscopic catalogue of 237 [OIII]+Hβ emitters at 6.75 < z < 9.05 and the first [OIII] 5008 Å luminosity function in that window. He develops photometric IGM tomography via deep narrow-band imaging to map Lyman-α transmission and hence neutral islands during the end stages of reionization. He extends these analyses to quasar-galaxy clustering at z ≃ 7.3, to little red dots at z ≳ 10 selected with MIRI+NIRCam, and to cross-identifications of faint DSFGs via ALMA CHAMPS.
nodes.w_text: Koki Kakiichi is tying together three reionisation-era probes: a JWST NIRCam F444W WFSS [OIII] emitter catalogue at 6.75 < z < 9.05 feeding the first spectroscopic [OIII] luminosity function in that window; first quasar–galaxy clustering constraints at z ≃ 7.3 anchored by J0252−0503 and J1007+2115; and a photometric IGM tomography technique to map neutral islands via Lyman-α transmission in deep narrow-band imaging. Next he is combining the [OIII] auto-correlation with the quasar–[OIII] cross-correlation at z ≃ 6.6 to jointly constrain host halo masses and duty cycles, and extending clustering analyses to additional z ≳ 7 quasars and little red dot candidates at z ≳ 10.

nodes.name: Conor John Ryan Mc Partland
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Conor John Ryan Mc Partland is a postdoctoral/early-career extragalactic astronomer affiliated with the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) at the Niels Bohr Institute, working with Sune Toft's group on high-redshift galaxy surveys. His research focuses on finding and characterising the earliest structures in the Universe using deep multiband imaging and large catalogues. He has been central to systematic searches for protocluster candidates at z ≥ 6 in the COSMOS field, using the COSMOS2020 source catalogue together with Weighted Adaptive Kernel and Weighted Voronoi Tessellation density estimators to identify 15 significant (>4σ) galaxy overdensities in the range 6 ≤ z ≤ 7.7 — inferred to host dark-matter halos of 10^11–10^13 M_sun that should evolve into Virgo/Coma-class z=0 clusters of 10^14–10^15 M_sun. More recently he has contributed to the Galaxy Zoo: Cosmic Dawn citizen-science + Zoobot deep-learning classification programme on the Hawaii Twenty Square Degree (H20) ultra-deep Hyper Suprime-Cam imaging of the Euclid Deep Field North, producing a public morphological classification catalogue of over 41,000 galaxies to m(HSC-i)=21.5 that serves as a training truth set for UNIONS and the Vera C. Rubin Observatory. His work sits at the intersection of deep photometric surveys, machine-learning classification, and early-Universe large-scale structure, typically with collaborators Sune Toft, Gabriel Brammer, Marko Shuntov, Shuowen Jin, and Lukas Zalesky.
nodes.w_text: Because Mc Partland's corpus was built via Pure abstracts only (no arXiv, no PDFs), his current work is inferred from two recent publications and his DAWN affiliation. Expect continued involvement in the Cosmic Dawn survey and Euclid Deep Field North programme: scaling Galaxy Zoo + Zoobot active-learning morphology classification to larger samples and deeper imaging, and using those catalogues as training truth sets for UNIONS and Vera C. Rubin LSST pipelines. On the science side, he is likely extending high-z protocluster searches with JWST and ALMA follow-up of the COSMOS2020 candidates, and exploring how the photometric versus narrow-band selection differences bias inferred stellar-mass and SFR evolution. Typical collaborators remain Sune Toft, Gabriel Brammer, Marko Shuntov, Shuowen Jin, and Lukas Zalesky. Because this summary rests on two abstracts, the visible slice likely understates his broader contributions.

nodes.name: Marko Shuntov
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Marko Shuntov is a young researcher at the Cosmic Dawn Center (DAWN) at NBI working on observational galaxy evolution using wide-field JWST surveys, with a particular specialty in the galaxy-halo connection via abundance matching and clustering. He is a lead/first author on several COSMOS2020 and COSMOS-Web flagship catalog papers, including 'The cosmic evolution of the stellar-to-halo mass relation for central and satellite galaxies up to z~5' (COSMOS2020, first author, 2022) and the COSMOS2025/COSMOS-Web galaxy catalog of photometry, morphology, redshifts and physical parameters (2025, first author). He has also led the 'stellar mass assembly in relation to dark matter halos across 0.2<z<12' COSMOS-Web paper (2024), the 'Tracing the galaxy-halo connection' clustering analysis, 'Constraints on the early Universe star formation efficiency from galaxy clustering and halo modeling of Halpha and [OIII] emitters' (2025, first author), and the stellar mass function of quiescent vs star-forming galaxies as a function of morphology in COSMOS-Web (2025, first author). He is also first author on 'The COSMOS-Web ring: Spectroscopic confirmation of the background source at z=5.1' (2025), and on the DAWN JWST Archive morphology paper profiling 340,000+ galaxies. Beyond his own lead work, he is a member of many consortium papers covering little red dots, JWST lens surveys, and PASSAGE NIRISS spectroscopy. His methodological toolkit spans halo occupation modeling, self-organizing maps, photo-z refinement via spectroscopic cross-correlation (PhotoWeb, first author 2020), and panchromatic catalog construction. The through-line is using COSMOS-Web as the premier probe of how galaxies populate dark matter halos from cosmic dawn to the present.
nodes.w_text: Shuntov's ongoing program is anchored in COSMOS-Web (COSMOS2025) as the premier wide-field JWST deep field for building the galaxy-halo connection from cosmic dawn to today. He leads the flagship COSMOS2025 catalog paper with photometry, morphology, redshifts and physical parameters, the COSMOS-Web stellar-mass-function-vs-morphology paper, a clustering-plus-halo-modeling constraint on early-Universe star-formation efficiency from Halpha and [OIII] emitters, and the COSMOS-Web ring z=5.1 spectroscopic confirmation. In parallel he drives the DAWN JWST Archive morphology measurements across 340,000 galaxies. His cosmic-web thread uses Simba plus COSMOS to detect at >=5 sigma a redshift-dependent differential evolution of starburst-vs-main-sequence distance-to-filaments. He also contributes broadly to consortium efforts on PASSAGE NIRISS spectroscopy, lens surveys, little red dots and high-z quiescent galaxies.

nodes.name: Sarah Pearson
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Sarah Pearson is an astrophysicist at the Dark Cosmology Centre (DARK) whose research programme centres on stellar streams as dynamical probes of dark matter and galaxy formation. She works across the full observational-theoretical chain: modelling tidal disruption of globular clusters (e.g. Palomar 5 with her 2017 work on galactic bar perturbations), developing detection techniques for thin streams in external galaxies (the Hough Stream Spotter; walter for resolved stellar population forecasting), and leading extragalactic surveys for streams around both Milky Way-mass hosts (e.g. Centaurus A, Stellar Stream Legacy Survey) and, most recently, dwarf galaxies. Her work is positioned where Gaia-era Milky Way substructure science meets the upcoming Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope and ERC-funded BeyondSTREAMS programme. Distinctively, she bridges near-field Galactic dynamics and extragalactic imaging at low surface brightness, using substructure in streams (gaps, fanning, shells, bifurcations) to constrain dark matter subhalos and halo geometry. Recent 2026 papers place her on the authorship of Roman HLWAS near-field planning, on the introduction of the KRIOS basis-expansion N-body code for self-consistent cluster-plus-stream modelling, and on the first systematic search for stellar streams around Local Volume dwarf galaxies in DES and DECaLS imaging. She is supported by a VILLUM FONDEN grant and the ERC BeyondSTREAMS project, making her one of DARK's core figures for stream-based dark matter science in the coming decade.
nodes.w_text: Pearson's 2025-2026 work converges on three pillars. First, she is co-shaping the near-field science case for the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, cataloguing the stream/satellite/globular-cluster content of the planned HLWAS and GPS footprints and pushing back against the 6-month revisit cadence to preserve proper-motion science. Second, through the KRIOS basis-expansion N-body code she is closing the accuracy-runtime gap between direct N-body and particle-spray stream modelling, with an eye to generating large model grids for dark-matter subhalo inference. Third, as part of her ERC BeyondSTREAMS programme she is extending the Stellar Stream Legacy Survey into the dwarf-galaxy regime in DES/DECaLS, producing the first systematic frequency measurement (5.1%) of accretion features around Local Volume dwarfs and identifying a tentative shell-versus-stream detection bias that motivates targeted dwarf-dwarf merger simulations.

nodes.name: Alessandra Camplani
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Alessandra Camplani is an Assistant Professor in Experimental Particle Physics at the Niels Bohr Institute, working on the ATLAS experiment with a profile sharply concentrated on trigger electronics, real-time track reconstruction, and the firmware/algorithm co-design that underpins them. Her PhD work established her as an FPGA firmware developer, writing VHDL for the Phase-I trigger back-end boards of the ATLAS Liquid-Argon electromagnetic calorimeter. That hardware line continued into the HL-LHC upgrade program: she is first author of the 2023 paper describing the Intel Stratix 10 MX FPGA design for the Pattern Recognition Mezzanine inside the ATLAS Hardware Tracking Trigger, which couples Associative Memory ASICs with linearized track-fit algorithms for the Phase-II Inner Tracker. On the physics-output side she appears on the 2022-2023 ATLAS b-jet trigger configuration-and-commissioning paper (JINST 20 (2025) 002), the upstream gate feeding HL-LHC jet-flavour-tagging chains. She also co-authors ATLAS-collaboration physics results spanning jet calibration, flavour-tagging, and heavy-ion measurements.
nodes.w_text: Camplani's active work sits upstream of the physics papers, in Phase-II trigger hardware and real-time tracking algorithms. She is the System Design and Integration Manager for ATLAS Event Filter Tracking — the HL-LHC real-time reconstruction system that mixes CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs with neural-network track finding to sustain a 1 MHz input rate and a 10 kHz output rate under 200 pileup. She is leading a GNN-based track-finding pipeline for the ATLAS Inner Tracker on FPGAs, covering graph construction, interaction-network edge classification, and track segmentation, with quantization-aware training and pruning to fit resource budgets. In parallel she continues FPGA firmware work on the Phase-II Pattern Recognition Mezzanine for the Hardware Tracking Trigger, and she contributed to commissioning the ATLAS b-jet trigger for the 2022-2023 Run 3 data-taking periods.

nodes.name: Nicola Minafra
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Nicola Minafra develops Ultra-Fast Silicon Detectors (UFSD) for picosecond-level timing of charged particles, with applications in forward physics at the LHC and in medical dosimetry. He designs and characterises 50 µm UFSD prototypes for the TOTEM Roman Pot upgrade, achieving ~16 ps timing resolution with the first production batch using 180 GeV/c pion beams. He contributes to the CMS Precision Proton Spectrometer (PPS), where precise time-of-flight of diffractively scattered protons is required. He demonstrates deep-neural-network techniques that improve fast-sampled timing-detector precision beyond constant-fraction-discriminator limits by correcting time-walk from charge fluctuations. He tests low-gain avalanche detectors (LGAD) in medical linacs for dosimetry and fluence monitoring. He participates in the ALICE ITS3 upgrade via characterisation of the first wafer-scale monolithic stitched sensor (MOSS) prototype, and historically engaged in diamond-detector timing for the TOTEM upgrade and elastic pp scattering measurements at √s = 8 TeV in the Coulomb-nuclear interference region.
nodes.w_text: Nicola Minafra is pushing picosecond-class UFSD timing from bench prototypes into production for LHC forward-physics Roman Pots and the CMS Precision Proton Spectrometer. He is integrating DNN-based time-walk correction with fast-sampled readout to beat classical constant-fraction-discriminator limits, and qualifying low-cost multipurpose readout boards with 50 µm UFSDs in test beams. Adjacent threads: RF characterisation of the new TOTEM Roman Pot to minimise beam-coupling impedance near-beam; LGAD performance in medical linacs for dosimetry; and characterisation of the first wafer-scale MOSS prototype for the ALICE ITS3 upgrade. Key open question: radiation-hardness ceiling of 50 µm UFSDs at Run-4 integrated luminosities.

nodes.name: Inar Timiryasov
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Inar Timiryasov is a hep-ph theorist in NBI's Experimental Subatomic Physics section whose work sits at the interface between BSM phenomenology (especially heavy neutral leptons, HNLs) and early-universe cosmology (Higgs inflation, Einstein-Cartan gravity, leptogenesis). His dominant research thread is the phenomenology of HNLs in the nu-MSM and related extensions: Dirac-vs-Majorana discrimination and HNL oscillations at SHiP, reinterpreting ATLAS HNL bounds in realistic neutrino-oscillation models, projecting NA62 sensitivity to K+ -> pi0 e+ N, and identifying an allowed window for HNLs below the kaon mass. With Ruchayskiy and Urquia-Calderon he has pushed HNL constraints into the TeV-PeV domain via charged-lepton-flavour-violation loops (whose bounds tighten with mass), and recently derived tree-level unitarity constraints on the total HNL Yukawa coupling |Y_tot|^2 <= 8 pi / phi (with phi the golden ratio) from J=1 partial waves. A parallel line is resonant / low-scale leptogenesis: reconciling leptogenesis with neutrino-oscillation baryogenesis, uniting low-scale leptogeneses, and freeze-in/freeze-out lepton-asymmetry generation after baryogenesis in the nu-MSM. A third line is Higgs-inflation in Palatini and Einstein-Cartan formulations of GR: preheating on the lattice, critical-point enhancement of the scalar power spectrum, an Einstein-Cartan portal to dark matter, and the Higgs-Dilaton positivity/viability analysis. He also contributes to SHiP physics cases, LHCb stealth-new-physics reinterpretations, and HNL community review papers. Methodologically he combines careful field-theory technology (partial-wave unitarity, renormalization-group running, out-of-equilibrium kinetics) with direct collider/fixed-target phenomenology, and he is known for sharp reinterpretations of experimental limits in realistic seesaw models.
nodes.w_text: Timiryasov's most recent arXiv output (2024) with Urquia-Calderon and Ruchayskiy derives a tree-level unitarity bound on the total HNL Yukawa coupling, |Y_tot|^2 <= 8 pi / phi (J=1 partial wave, phi the golden ratio), which together with charged-lepton-flavour-violation loop bounds fixes a sharp maximum mass for an HNL that is the sole source of neutrino masses in type-I seesaw. That paper caps his 'HNL to PeV' programme, which he has been advancing with Ruchayskiy and experimental interpretation papers for ATLAS, NA62, and SHiP, and which intersects with his earlier low-scale leptogenesis work in the nu-MSM. In parallel he continues a cosmology thread on Palatini and Einstein-Cartan Higgs inflation, including critical-point power-spectrum enhancement at small xi and preheating on the lattice. He also appears on large community HNL reviews and the 2026 'AI-native Research Ecosystem for Experimental Particle Physics' community vision paper.

nodes.name: Graig Wiglesworth
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Graig Wiglesworth is an experimental subatomic physicist in the NBI ATLAS group. The available record comes from the Pure fallback and consists exclusively of large ATLAS-collaboration papers shared with the other NBI ATLAS members (Dam, Camplani, Beck Hansen, Petersen, Peter Henrik Hansen). Recent topics clustered on his profile include heavy-ion collective-flow measurements (first pT-dependent radial-flow-fluctuation measurement v0(pT) in Pb+Pb at √sNN = 5.02 TeV via two-particle correlations, revealing long-range pseudorapidity correlations, pT factorization, and bulk-viscosity sensitivity in the QGP); the top-quark Yukawa coupling measurement from tt̄ lepton+jets at √s = 13 TeV; a continuous calibration of ATLAS flavour-tagging classifiers via optimal-transportation maps trained on tt̄→eμννbb data that simultaneously corrects b-, c- and light-jet probabilities; a neural simulation-based inference implementation for parameter estimation in ATLAS; and a precise jet-energy-scale derivation from single-particle and in-situ techniques in pp at 13 TeV. These topics indicate involvement in both heavy-ion and Higgs/top physics analyses at ATLAS with a clear ML-calibration thread. The individual intellectual fingerprint relative to the rest of the NBI ATLAS cluster is not attributable from this public record.
nodes.w_text: Wiglesworth's current ATLAS activity spans heavy-ion collective-flow measurements (the first pT-dependent measurement of radial-flow fluctuations v0(pT) in Pb+Pb at √sNN = 5.02 TeV, sensitive to QGP bulk viscosity via two-particle correlations), precision Standard Model physics (top-quark Yukawa coupling in tt̄ lepton+jets at 13 TeV; precise jet energy scale from single-particle and in-situ techniques), and ML-based analysis tooling (continuous flavour-tagging calibration via optimal-transportation maps using tt̄→eμννbb data; neural simulation-based inference for parameter estimation). All items are large-collaboration ATLAS papers co-authored with the NBI ATLAS cluster (Dam, Camplani, the two Hansens, Petersen), so individual contribution cannot be isolated from public record.

nodes.name: Andrew Alan Chael
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Andrew Chael is an astrophysicist at the Niels Bohr International Academy who works at the intersection of Event Horizon Telescope imaging, general-relativistic MHD simulations, and the theory of black-hole accretion and jet launching. He is the author or core developer of several of the computational tools the EHT relies on, including the EHT-HOPS pipeline for mm-VLBI data reduction, interferometric imaging directly from closure phases and amplitudes, two-temperature radiative GRMHD simulations of low-luminosity black-hole accretion flows, and recent hybrid GRMHD / force-free simulations. He first-authored the M87* 'Two-temperature magnetically arrested disc' jet simulation (2018) and a recent 'Survey of radiative, two-temperature MAD simulations of M87* I: turbulent electron heating' (2025, sole author). A central theme of his work is using polarimetric imaging of M87* and Sgr A* - through the image-integrated angle(beta_2) observable - to constrain black-hole spin and the Blandford-Znajek electromagnetic energy extraction mechanism. His Black Hole Polarimetry series (I 2023, II 2025; Off-Axis Jets 2026) builds physically transparent semi-analytic inflow and jet-polarization frameworks that interpolate between the force-free monopole and fully turbulent GRMHD, quantitatively predicting spin-dependent polarization patterns and already disfavouring high-spin configurations for both M87* and Sgr A*. Parallel tracks include work with Katherine Bouman's group on physics-informed neural-field dynamic tomography of black-hole emission (PI-DEF), multi-frequency black-hole imaging for the next-generation EHT, the 'inner shadow' direct view of the event horizon, and jet-launching physics in MAD flows including spin-down. His collaborators span EHT, ngEHT, BHEX, and computer-vision groups; the overall thrust is making near-horizon imaging a precision tool for general relativity and plasma physics.
nodes.w_text: Chael's current work revolves around turning near-horizon polarimetric imaging into a precision probe of black-hole spin and the Blandford-Znajek mechanism. His Black Hole Polarimetry series (I 2023, II 2025, plus Off-Axis Jets 2026 with Gelles and Quataert) uses analytic force-free limits, semi-analytic stationary axisymmetric GRMHD inflow models tunable via magnetization, and comparison to MAD GRMHD simulations to show that the image-integrated angle(beta_2) polarization observable maps robustly to spin - already disfavouring high-spin configurations for both M87* and Sgr A* when applied to EHT data. In parallel he is first author on a 2025 sole-author 'Survey of radiative, two-temperature MAD simulations of M87*' on turbulent electron heating, a 2024 hybrid GRMHD + force-free simulation framework, and he collaborates with Bouman's group on PI-DEF physics-informed neural-field dynamic 3D tomography of black-hole emission. Multi-frequency ngEHT/BHEX imaging prospects are a consistent horizon across his output.

nodes.name: Kristian Thijssen
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Kristian Thijssen works primarily on the computational and theoretical rheology of soft matter systems, with particular focus on how mechanical properties—storage and loss moduli, shear thinning, viscoelasticity—emerge from microscopic structure in colloidal suspensions, gels, and active fluids. He demonstrated that motility-induced phase separation (MIPS) in suspensions of pseudo-hard active Brownian particles behaves as a viscoelastic Maxwell fluid, with a non-monotonic crossover point between storage and loss moduli that rises with activity before the MIPS transition and falls after it. He mapped the rheological properties of two-dimensional Lennard-Jones suspensions across the gas/liquid and gas/solid coexistence lines, showing that phase coexistence—not density or temperature alone—dictates the transition from viscous to elastic-dominated behavior under shear. He has a strong specialization in active nematics, investigating how flow-alignment coupling controls whether the Berezinskii–Kosterlitz–Thouless defect-unbinding transition is suppressed: for strain-rate-aligning nematics (λ≠0), bend-splay walls lower the defect nucleation threshold and prevent sustained ±1/2 defect pair recombination, while the canonical BKT scenario is recovered only when flow alignment is absent. He used mesoscale simulations to show that colloidal diffusivity in active nematic turbulence is non-monotonic with activity, with topological kicks from motile bulk defects enhancing transport at low activity and isotropic defect interactions decorrelating colloidal motion at high activity. His work on colloidal gels combines continuum rheological modeling, local stress tensor analysis, and particle-scale structural measurements to characterize necking failure of single gel arms, identifying plastic flow near the yield stress as the microscopic signature of the necking instability. He further employed persistent homology analyses of three-dimensional colloidal gels doped with active particles to reveal highly porous yet compact gel structures formed by local energy injection, and demonstrated topologically distinct roles of passive interfaces in 2D versus 3D active-doped systems. He developed an active Darcy's law framework showing that hybrid active/pressure-driven flows through porous media exceed pure-driven flux, with a non-monotonic optimum in activity that maximizes flow rate. He also presented analytical and numerical investigations of activity-induced hydrodynamic instabilities in model brain organoids, identifying active cellular stress as a previously overlooked contributor to surface deformation in these systems.
nodes.w_text: Kristian Thijssen is probing how structural heterogeneity and phase coexistence—rather than activity per se—generate complex viscoelastic and topological behavior in soft matter. Using 2D Brownian dynamics simulations of Lennard-Jones suspensions and large-scale fluctuating nematohydrodynamic simulations with tunable flow-alignment parameter λ, he showed that MIPS alone produces Maxwell-like relaxation and exclusive shear thinning, and that finite λ drives persistent defect unbinding across all temperatures, likely precluding BKT transitions in real nematics. He is now extending these frameworks to active heterogeneous materials, comparing MIPS rheology against passive liquid-gas coexistence systems, testing wall-mediated defect recombination mechanisms, and pushing into three dimensions where line defects, biaxiality, and secondary instabilities introduce qualitatively new topological transitions.

nodes.name: Farzan Vafa
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Farzan Vafa works at the intersection of soft condensed matter theory and biological physics, with a particular focus on how topological defects in liquid-crystalline and active materials interact with surface geometry. A recurring methodological signature is the use of isothermal coordinates to explicitly encode a geometric contribution to the elastic potential, and the Born-Oppenheimer approximation to decouple fast p-atic texture relaxation from slow defect motion on curved surfaces. He has systematically characterized defect absorption, emission, and pair nucleation at cone apices for p-atic liquid crystals, deriving closed-form expressions for the effective topological charge Q_eff = (σ − σ²/2) at the apex and ground-state defect ring radii d = ((p−1)/(3p−1))^(1/2p) R as functions of deficit angle and p. His work on active nematics on cones identified how the sign of the deficit angle flips the effective motility of +1/2 defects—switching extensile from contractile behavior when the deficit angle exceeds π—and predicted exotic periodic orbits and unbinding transitions confirmed by numerical simulations using the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. He extends these geometric frameworks to biological morphogenesis, modeling how relaxational dynamics of an active curved surface driven by its nematic texture can reproduce activity-stabilized bound +1 defect states forming incipient Hydra tentacles and cell accumulation at positive defects in murine neural progenitor cell monolayers. He further showed that a p-atic defect of charge +1/p dynamically generates a cone whose semi-cone angle β satisfies sin β ≥ 1 − 1/p + 1/(2p²), linking defect charge to surface shape in epithelial morphogenesis. More recently, he applied the complex Ginzburg-Landau equation on realistic cortical surfaces to show that curvature enters through the Laplace-Beltrami operator as a spatial modulation of effective diffusion, and that gradients of this effective diffusion generate geometric forces that stabilize and localize spiral waves in folded brain geometry, while progressive cortical smoothing erases these structures. He has also developed a dynamic-programming algorithm for Hidden Markov Models on trees with coupled branches that solves the likelihood, decoding, and parameter learning problems with polynomial scaling in states and nodes, applied to lineage-dependent biological data. In nematopolar systems, he identified a tricritical point and a novel universality class for the nematopolar-nematic transition, and showed that nematic +1/2 defect pairs in the locked nematopolar phase are connected and confined by strings of constant tension arising from orientational frustration.
nodes.w_text: Farzan Vafa is probing how curved geometry actively shapes physical and biological dynamics across three fronts. On cortical dynamics, he built an analytical CGLE framework using the Laplace-Beltrami operator to show that cortical folding localizes spiral waves at negatively curved regions, and now aims to couple this with heterogeneous synaptic connectivity and surface growth to study co-evolving brain form and function. In active matter, he constructed a numerical phase-diagram framework revealing that nematopolar systems form a new universality class with string-confined +1/2 defect pairs, and plans to probe fluctuation effects near tricritical points and defect dynamics under external fields. Separately, he built a polynomial-complexity dynamic programming algorithm for coupled-branch tree HMMs and intends to apply it to cellular lineage data.

nodes.name: Tulio Brito Brasil
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Tulio Brito Brasil is a Quantum Optics experimentalist at the Niels Bohr Institute in Eugene Polzik's group, working on the interface between continuous-variable (CV) entangled light and atomic spin ensembles, with sensing applications down to the acoustic frequency range. His distinguishing contribution is the coupling of two-colour EPR-entangled light, generated from a nondegenerate optical parametric oscillator (NOPO), with a macroscopic atomic spin oscillator configured as a negative-mass reference. This hybrid architecture provides broadband frequency-dependent quantum-noise reduction at disparate optical wavelengths, directly relevant for next-generation gravitational-wave detectors (GWDs) such as LIGO and Virgo, where standard quantum-limit-breaking sensitivity below 1 kHz is needed. His 2023-2025 publications span three milestones: (i) demonstration of quantum back-action, ponderomotive squeezing and virtual spring softening of an atomic spin oscillator at acoustic (sub-kHz) frequencies, (ii) first hybrid quantum network linking a multi-wavelength CV EPR source to a spin oscillator acting as an effective filter cavity for GWDs, equivalent in squeezing rotation to a several-metre Fabry-Perot cavity, and (iii) coherent phase control of a two-colour EPR state with 9 dB of two-mode squeezing in the acoustic band. Funded through VILLUM FONDEN, the ERC QUANTUM-N Advanced grant, NNF Quantum for Life, and the Copenhagen Center for Biomedical Quantum Sensing, his work positions Copenhagen as a leader in practical quantum-noise engineering beyond the MHz regime.
nodes.w_text: Brito Brasil's current work in Polzik's lab is converging on a deployable hybrid quantum-sensing architecture. His 2025 paper delivered 9 dB of two-mode squeezing in the acoustic range using a single-seed coherent phase control scheme for a frequency-nondegenerate OPO with 200 nm separation. Building on this, his group has coupled the tunable EPR light to a macroscopic Cs atomic spin oscillator acting as a negative-mass reference, demonstrating octave-spanning quantum-noise reduction at a disparate wavelength and showing the equivalence of the 8 cm spin cell to a multi-metre Fabry-Perot filter cavity. The active targets are reducing thermal occupation and broadband spin noise enough to reach EPR-limited bandwidth, operating in vacuum to push QBA-dominated performance down to 1-2 kHz, and cascading spin ensembles to shape the noise-reduction spectral profile for LIGO/Virgo and biomedical magnetometry.

nodes.name: Jean-Baptiste Sylvain Béguin
