nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Jean-Baptiste Beguin is a Quantum Optics experimentalist at the Niels Bohr Institute who builds hybrid interfaces between ultracold neutral atoms and nanophotonic chips. His Caltech-era work with H. J. Kimble on two-nanobeam alligator photonic crystal waveguides (APCWs) and his later Copenhagen programme converge on a single goal: deterministically coupling single Cesium atoms and atomic arrays to guided light in suspended on-chip silicon nitride circuits. Distinctively, he combines nanofabrication, atom trapping with optical tweezers and nanofiber/evanescent-field traps, and quantum measurement theory; he has contributed protocols for generation of nonclassical spin states by QND measurement, sub-Poissonian atom-number distributions, and polariton tomography. His November 2025 demonstration is a milestone: a 2D tweezer lattice of Cs atoms is transported millimetre distances and placed deterministically into the evanescent field of a suspended SiN nanophotonic waveguide, delivering a platform compatible with integrated solid-state photon sources and detectors. In early 2026 he has contributed theoretically to a scheme using subradiance, superradiance, and the quantum Zeno effect to dissipatively prepare W-type entangled steady states in waveguide QED. He is funded through Hy-Q (DNRF139), Quantum for Life (NNF), and local Danish partnerships, positioning Copenhagen uniquely at the nanophotonics-atom interface for scalable quantum networks.
nodes.w_text: Beguin's ongoing Copenhagen programme integrates three lines. Experimentally, he has just demonstrated deterministic transport of a 2D Cs tweezer lattice into the evanescent field of a suspended SiN photonic waveguide (late 2025), establishing a hybrid atom-nanophotonic platform with <3 dB chip coupling loss and single-atom evanescent-field probes. The next experimental push is to enter the TE strong-coupling regime per atom and to realise light-mediated atom-atom interactions by delivering pairs with near-unit probability. Theoretically, with Sorensen and Alba, he is developing scalable dissipative protocols that use subradiance, superradiance, and the quantum Zeno effect to prepare W-type steady-state entanglement with favourable 1/C fidelity scaling. In parallel he continues to analyse optomechanics of the underlying photonic crystal waveguides as a quantum sensing resource.

nodes.name: Yinhui Kan
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Yinhui Kan is a nanophotonics researcher in the Niels Bohr Institute's Quantum Optics section (working closely with Sergey Bozhevolnyi's group at SDU), whose research centres on on-chip, room-temperature solid-state quantum light sources built from metasurface-integrated quantum emitters (metaQE / QEMS). His signature idea is to use nitrogen-, germanium- or silicon-vacancy colour centres in nanodiamonds positioned atop engineered dielectric metasurfaces on silver films: the colour centre excites non-radiative surface plasmon polaritons that propagate radially and are then sculpted by the surrounding meta-atom array into well-defined free-space photonic modes. Using this platform he has demonstrated a zoo of structured single-photon sources — room-temperature on-chip orbital-angular-momentum emission, linearly and circularly polarized vortex beams, scattering-holography-channelled emission, and (in 2026) single-photon skyrmions and anti-skyrmions/skyrmionium as the first single-photon realizations of these topological polarization textures. He has also led work on integrating QEMS with MEMS-tuned microcavities (MEMS-QEMS), yielding angstrom-resolution spectral tuning that narrows broadband NV emission to 3.7 nm and enables sub-millisecond polarization switching across 650–700 nm. His most recent 2026 work introduces a forward-designed elliptic/hyperbolic SPP nanophotonic platform that mediates long-range QE–QE interactions with experimentally enhanced and suppressed energy-transfer rates, predicting transient QE–QE entanglement with concurrence up to 0.493 and extending to three-emitter configurations. Typical collaborators: Bozhevolnyi, Shailesh Kumar, Xujing Liu, Paul C. V. Thrane, Radu Malureanu, Fernández-Domínguez.
nodes.w_text: Kan's 2025–2026 output marks an intense, accelerating phase. He is simultaneously pushing three fronts of the metaQE / QEMS programme: (1) topologically structured single-photon states, with the recent demonstration of on-chip single-photon skyrmions, anti-skyrmions, and skyrmionium from NV and GeV centres in nanodiamonds; (2) dynamic, chip-integrated tunability, where MEMS-QEMS platforms provide angstrom-resolution spectral and sub-millisecond polarization switching of solid-state photon sources; and (3) SPP-mediated long-range QE–QE interactions, culminating in the 2026 forward-designed nanophotonic platform that experimentally tunes energy-transfer rates between distant emitters and predicts transient multi-emitter entanglement approaching the theoretical bound. Expect continued work with Bozhevolnyi, Kumar, Liu, Thrane, Malureanu, and Fernández-Domínguez on steady-state entanglement engineering, integration with other colour centres (SiV, SnV), combination of topological and dynamic tunability, and practical applications in high-dimensional quantum information, sensing, and biological imaging.

nodes.name: Shikai Liu
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Shikai Liu is a Quantum Optics experimentalist at the Niels Bohr Institute who works at the intersection of single-emitter nanophotonics and high-dimensional photonic entanglement. His flagship 2023 first-author paper, with Lodahl, Sorensen, Tiranov and collaborators, demonstrates a CHSH Bell-inequality violation (S = 2.67 +/- 0.16) by scattering single-photon-level light off a quantum-dot two-level emitter deterministically coupled to a nanophotonic waveguide. The distinguishing feature is that the emitter stays passive in its ground state, generating energy-time entangled photon pairs via a 'giant' two-photon nonlinearity without any spin control, pointing toward ultra-low energy-consumption photonic entanglement sources for simulation and metrology. His earlier USTC-era work (with Shilong Liu, Zhou, Shi) built the precision nonlinear-optics toolkit behind this programme: cavity-enhanced sum-frequency generation producing 525 nm light at 77% quantum conversion efficiency for two-colour entangled-pair and OAM down-conversion experiments; high-dimensional quantum frequency conversion; multiplexing of heralded single photons in orbital-angular-momentum space; and pump-beam shaping (exponential/inverse-Gaussian profiles) to broaden the OAM spiral bandwidth of SPDC entangled pairs. Distinctively, he bridges nonlinear-optics engineering and quantum-emitter physics, positioning Copenhagen to synthesise photonic entanglement across discrete- and continuous-variable, polarisation, energy-time, and orbital-angular-momentum degrees of freedom simultaneously.
nodes.w_text: Liu's headline current result, with Lodahl and Sorensen at NBI, is a Bell-inequality violation (S = 2.67 +/- 0.16) achieved by scattering single photons off a deterministically coupled quantum-dot two-level emitter in a nanophotonic waveguide, with the emitter remaining passive in its ground state. This demonstrates an energy-conserving route to high-fidelity photonic entanglement that sidesteps active spin control. The ongoing directions connect this waveguide-emitter entanglement platform with the nonlinear-optics toolkit he carried over from USTC: cavity-enhanced SFG at 525 nm (reaching 77% QCE), high-dimensional quantum frequency converters, multiplexed heralded single photons in OAM, and pump-beam shaping for broadening SPDC spiral bandwidth. Collectively these tools target scalable photonic entanglement synthesis across energy-time, OAM, and spectral degrees of freedom for networked quantum simulators and metrology.

nodes.name: Ying Wang
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Ying Wang is a researcher in the Niels Bohr Institute's Quantum Optics section. The arXiv trail assembled for this pid is heavily contaminated by name collisions with several other "Ying Wang" authors across unrelated fields (plasma photonic crystals in Harbin, quantum-circuit compilation, quantum machine learning, 2D magnetism, ion transport through nanopores), which makes the three tier-A selected papers unreliable as a guide to her research. Cross-referencing authorship patterns elsewhere in the candidate list, the NBI-Quantum-Optics Ying Wang appears as first author on "Deterministic photon source interfaced with a programmable silicon-nitride integrated circuit" (2302.06282, 2023) and "Electroabsorption in gated GaAs nanophotonic waveguides" (2102.06119, 2021), and as a frequent second author on Lodahl-group waveguide-quantum-emitter papers including integration of GaAs nanomechanical phase shifters with quantum-dot single-photon sources (2025), controlling emitter-field coupling in waveguides with a nanomechanical phase shifter (2025), and electrical control of quantum dots in GaAs-on-insulator waveguides for coherent single-photon generation (2025). This pattern places her research in deterministic semiconductor-quantum-dot single-photon sources embedded in GaAs photonic-crystal waveguides, with interfaces to silicon-nitride programmable circuits, gate-controlled electroabsorption modulators, and nanomechanical phase shifters for on-chip photonic quantum networking. Because the ingested paper corpus is dominated by papers from unrelated authors, specific technical detail beyond this broad fingerprint cannot be safely attributed here.
nodes.w_text: The arXiv corpus collected for this pid is severely contaminated by name collisions across multiple unrelated "Ying Wang" authors (plasma photonic crystals, quantum machine-learning robustness, quantum-circuit layout synthesis, 2D magnets, ionic transport), so the top-3 selected papers do not reflect the NBI Quantum Optics researcher's current direction and cannot be used to infer it. Cross-referencing the broader candidate list, however, suggests that the NBI-Quantum-Optics Ying Wang is active in the Lodahl-group programme on deterministic quantum-dot single-photon sources in GaAs photonic-crystal waveguides and their integration with silicon-nitride programmable photonic circuits, gate-controlled electroabsorption, and GaAs nanomechanical phase shifters for on-chip quantum networking. Specific current projects cannot be attributed reliably from this contaminated record.

nodes.name: Xiang Xi
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Xiang Xi is a Quantum Optics researcher at the Niels Bohr Institute, working in Albert Schliesser's group on topological phononics in soft-clamped nanomechanical membranes, while also maintaining strong collaborations with Zhen Gao's photonic-topology group at SUSTech. His signature contribution is the demonstration of a soft-clamped topological waveguide for phonons (arXiv:2408.08717, first author, 2024): a valley-Hall topological phononic crystal etched into high-stress silicon-nitride membranes where backscattering-suppressed megahertz flexural edge modes propagate around sharp bends while simultaneously benefitting from soft-clamping-induced dissipation dilution that pushes phononic quality factors into the regime relevant for quantum optomechanics. A recent follow-up (2510.21619, 2025) introduces a frequency-detuned collimated-beam interferometer with stroboscopic pulse probing and a standard CMOS camera to image megahertz Valley-Hall topological edge modes across >20 mm² with ~6 µm resolution in a 2-second acquisition—replacing slow raster-scan protocols and providing the first direct visual evidence that large backscattering distorts mode profiles and causes frequency splitting. An earlier single-first-author paper demonstrated a topological Dirac-vortex parametric phonon laser (2107.11162), and he pioneered topological photonic crystals with large valley Chern numbers, topological photonic integrated circuits based on valley kink states, and nanomechanical topological insulators with auxiliary orbital degrees of freedom. On the optomechanics side he contributed to membrane-in-the-middle experiments with soft-clamped membranes at milliKelvin temperatures. Broader topology-physics collaborations have produced results on spatiotemporal topological phase transitions in photonic spacetime crystals, non-Hermitian skin effects, 3D higher-order topological insulators, and acoustic quadrupole insulators.
nodes.w_text: Xi's current arc combines two threads. First, at NBI in the Schliesser group, he is building and characterizing soft-clamped topological phononic waveguides in high-stress silicon-nitride membranes: a 2025 paper co-led by his group introduces a detuned-stroboscopic wide-field CMOS-camera interferometer that images MHz Valley-Hall topological edge modes over 20 mm² at 6 µm resolution in 2 s, directly revealing that backscattering distorts mode profiles and splits mode frequencies. This extends his 2024 first-author "soft-clamped topological waveguide for phonons," and will likely drive next-generation integrated phononic circuits for quantum optomechanics. Second, with the Zhen Gao group at SUSTech he is pushing topological photonics into space-time domains: the December 2025 photonic-spacetime-crystal paper experimentally realizes spatiotemporal topological phase transitions and space-time topological events in a dynamically modulated transmission line, and the April 2026 paper applies quasi-BIC metasurfaces to ultrasensitive THz biosensing.

nodes.name: Asta Heinesen
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Asta Heinesen's program is model-independent, geometry-first cosmology aimed at testing whether the cosmic expansion is truly FLRW. Central themes: curvature-consistency null tests built from derivatives of the angular diameter distance d_A(z) and the line-of-sight expansion H(z), including a new null statistic T that vanishes for Dyer-Roeder-type optical deviations while C ≠ 0. She pairs this with symbolic-regression+bootstrap reconstructions on Pantheon+/SN and BAO/DESI data that reveal ~2-4σ mild-to-moderate tension with FLRW over z ∈ [0.38, ~2]. On the geometric side she derives a quasi-Newtonian (shear-free, vorticity-free) foliation of general cosmological space-times that expresses redshift as Hubble + integrated Sachs-Wolfe + gravitational contributions and recovers a Newton-Poisson limit for LTB, Szekeres and Bianchi cosmologies, illustrated with the degenerate Kasner solution. Further work: covariant cosmography to 4th order in redshift for Λ-Szekeres models and a theoretical dipole prediction for nearby luminosity distances tested against fully-relativistic ray tracing of gevolution-style numerical-relativity simulations (accurate to ~10% for z ≲ 0.07 in smooth runs, z ≲ 0.02 in non-linear ones). Also: cosmic drift and Gaia DR3 proper-motion analysis isolating a 2-3σ redshift-dependent spheroidal dipole, and a differential-age / cosmic-chronometer identity that pins the volume-average expansion rate without assuming FLRW.
nodes.w_text: Heinesen is pushing a fully model-independent, derivative-based diagnostic of whether our Universe really obeys a single FLRW metric on the largest scales. She builds curvature-consistency null tests from d_A(z) and H(z) — including a new T statistic that vanishes identically for any Dyer-Roeder-type optical deviation, cleanly separating optical from geometric FLRW violations — and a Friedmann-equation-independent estimator of the cosmic density field. In parallel she develops a quasi-Newtonian (shear-free, vorticity-free) foliation that rewrites the redshift of arbitrary cosmologies as Hubble+integrated Sachs-Wolfe+gravitational contributions and recovers a Newton-Poisson limit, giving a unified language for LTB, Szekeres, Bianchi and Kasner space-times. The arc of the work connects these theoretical tools to emerging 2-4σ hints of FLRW violation in Pantheon+/BOSS/DESI and in Gaia DR3 proper-motion dipoles.

nodes.name: Andres Luna Godoy
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Andres Luna (Luna Godoy) is a theorist in the Theoretical Particle Physics and Cosmology section whose recent sole-author work applies scattering-amplitude and worldline-effective-field-theory methods to compact binary dynamics in general relativity. His distinctive finding is that physical scattering observables for spinning compact objects — impulse, spin kick, leading-order waveform — depend on additional degrees of freedom that must be packaged into an unconstrained spin tensor rather than a spin vector alone. A direct consequence is a change in the magnitude of the spin vector under conservative Hamiltonian evolution, with the commutation relations and Poisson brackets inherited from the underlying Lorentz symmetry. He shows that the conservative impulse, boost, spin kick and the evolution of the extra degrees of freedom are captured by the eikonal phase (equivalently the radial action) up to next-to-leading order in Newton's constant and third order in spin. He connects these degrees of freedom to dynamical worldline multipole moments and notes that they decouple for Kerr black holes but are present in generic compact objects, including a Newtonian two-particle bound system and a black-hole solution in an exotic matter-coupled gravitational theory. He constructs effective two-body Hamiltonians that explicitly require the extra degrees of freedom to describe the full dynamics, and removes certain unphysical singularities. He also discusses a Goldstone-theorem interpretation of the extra degrees of freedom.
nodes.w_text: Luna's recent solo-author work sharpens the scattering-amplitude / worldline-effective-field-theory programme for spinning compact binaries in general relativity. His central thesis is that the spin vector is insufficient: an unconstrained spin tensor carrying additional Lorentz-inherited degrees of freedom is required to describe the complete dynamics, and produces a change in spin magnitude under conservative Hamiltonian evolution. He computes impulse, boost, spin kick and leading-order waveform up to NLO in Newton's constant and third order in spin, packages them in the eikonal phase / radial action, connects the extra degrees of freedom to dynamical worldline multipole moments, builds effective two-body Hamiltonians, removes unphysical singularities, and explores a Goldstone-theorem interpretation. The degrees of freedom decouple for Kerr black holes but survive for generic compact objects.

nodes.name: Rodrigo Panosso Macedo
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Rodrigo Panosso Macedo is a mathematical/numerical relativist in Theoretical Particle Physics and Cosmology whose career has systematically developed the hyperboloidal framework for black-hole perturbation theory. His signature technical contribution is to replace Boyer-Lindquist-type time slicings with future hyperboloidal foliations that terminate at the black-hole horizon H+ and future null infinity ℐ+, rendering quasinormal-mode (QNM) eigenfunctions finite and producing geometrically clean definitions of excitation factors, amplitudes, and QNM projections. He has applied this framework across Schwarzschild, Reissner-Nordström, Kerr, Schwarzschild-de Sitter, anti-de Sitter, and horizonless compact objects. A second major strand is the QNM pseudospectrum / spectral-instability program: with collaborators he introduced the pseudospectrum into gravitational physics (arXiv:2004.06434), derived a Weyl's law for black-hole QNMs, established universal spectral instabilities of Reissner-Nordström black holes and horizonless compact objects, and identified "elephant-and-flea" fundamental-mode destabilisation. A third strand connects these tools to gravitational-wave astronomy: environmental effects and self-force calculations for extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (including a multi-domain spectral method, a Detweiler–Whiting singular-field computation, and a hyperboloidal frequency-domain self-force pipeline), waveform modelling for LISA, and ringdown phenomena beyond the QNM paradigm such as exceptional points and avoided-crossing resonances. Most recently he has derived the conformal Teukolsky equation directly from Friedrich's Conformal Einstein Field Equations (CEFEs) around Petrov-type D backgrounds, including for a blown-up cylinder representation of spatial infinity i0 — a first-principles CEFE derivation that reproduces and extends standard hyperboloidal Teukolsky results. He is supported by the Villum Investigator programme (VIL37766), the DNRF Chair programme (DNRF162), and the Center of Gravity.
nodes.w_text: Panosso Macedo's current program pushes the hyperboloidal framework as the unifying geometric tool for black-hole perturbation theory and ringdown. Recent 2026 papers include: (i) a regularisation of QNM bilinear products on hyperboloidal foliations for Schwarzschild, introducing past-hyperboloidal coordinates to encode the CPT/𝒥 reflection and reconciling bulk + flux contributions via Tricomi-function and complex-contour methods; (ii) a first-principles derivation of the conformal Teukolsky equation from Friedrich's Conformal Einstein Field Equations linearised around a Petrov-type D background, with the conformal factor decoupling from ϕ0 and ϕ4 and a new formulation in a blown-up cylinder at spatial infinity i0; and (iii) an exceptional-point framework for black-hole ringdown beyond QNMs, showing that avoided-crossing resonances produce characteristic departures from exponentially damped oscillations and that the averaged EP frequency is the physical observable. He is supported by the Villum Investigator programme (VIL37766), the DNRF Chair (DNRF162), and the Center of Gravity.

nodes.name: Shashank Shalgar
nodes.title: Assistant Professor
nodes.p_text: Shashank Shalgar works on neutrino flavor evolution in dense astrophysical environments, centred on numerical solutions of the quantum kinetic equations (QKEs) in core-collapse supernovae and neutron star mergers. A running theme is debunking common simplifications: he shows that the steady-state (time-independent) formalism underlying the neutrino-bulb model and prior matter-neutrino-resonance (MNR) studies produces unstable solutions and qualitatively wrong survival probabilities versus fully time-dependent multi-angle calculations. He argues that the length scale of collective neutrino oscillations is not set by the inverse self-interaction potential — a widely held numerical-convergence myth he explicitly refutes. He runs multi-energy, multi-angle three-flavor QKE simulations without attenuation of the self-interaction strength, including advection and non-forward collisions, within an 18.6 M☉ CCSN model, finding flavor equipartition at t_pb = 1 s but not at t_pb = 0.25 s, with up to 30% enhancement of neutrino heating, and demonstrates that 1% matter-density perturbations (at length scales 1-100 m) do not destabilise the solutions because neutrino mean free paths exceed the perturbation scale. Other contributions: (2+1+1)-D QKE simulations showing advection-driven spreading of flavor waves, Pauli-blocking beyond-mean-field corrections shifting flavor-instability regions, dependence of electron-lepton-number angular crossings on nuclear EOS, muon creation and proto-neutron-star convection in 12 18.6 M☉ SN models, and implications of lepton-number-violating neutrino self-interactions for the pre-supernova electron fraction.
nodes.w_text: Shalgar's current frontier is retiring the steady-state and single-angle simplifications that have shaped collective neutrino flavor-evolution since the neutrino-bulb era. In one paper he runs the first time-dependent multi-angle matter-neutrino-resonance simulations in a neutron-star-merger-relevant setup and shows the steady-state MNR solutions are intrinsically unstable, producing both quantitatively wrong survival probabilities and spurious small-scale angular structure — with implications that propagate back into the entire bulb-model literature. In parallel he stress-tests spherical multi-flavor QKE solutions in an 18.6 M☉ CCSN against 1% matter-density fluctuations over radial scales of 1-100 m, showing that angular distributions and number densities are essentially untouched because neutrinos cannot resolve structure below their mean free path. The third strand, outside neutrino physics, is a Gaia DR3 analysis of the QSO position-drift field reporting a 2-3σ redshift-dependent spheroidal dipole that could be either a Gaia systematic or a cosmological signature.
